explanation: These rock carvings are at Chilas in Kashmir. They are along a major trade route in the Gilgit valley. The carvings depict a pair of figures, holding what seem to be a plough and a disc, wearing wide open coats similar to that of the Kushana period (2nd-3rd cent CE). They are identified as Balarama and Krishna based on the Khartoshti inscriptions found in the area. Coins of 2nd Century BCE depicting Balarama and Krishna have also been found in Al-Khanoum in Afghanistan. Source: Upinder Singh. "A History of Medieval India from the Stone Age to the 12th Century." Picture Credit: Wikimedia

hook: Prof S. R Rao discovered a submerged Mahabharata city in the 1980s using underwater excavations. Based on dating of remains, he proposed the date for Mahabharata to 1500 BCE. Which city did he discover?
explanation: Prof. S. R. Rao conducted offshore marine excavations in Dwarka. His findings matched the description given in Mahabharata about Dwaraka. He found evidence for a port city with active sea trade. The layout of the submerged city, high fort walls and stone bases of flag posts correlate with the description of Dwarka in Mahabharata. Pottery found in the submerged city matches with 15th century BCE pottery culture. Seals were found (dated to 15th/ 16th century BCE) which correspond with the statement in Harivamsa that all residents of Dwaraka were required to carry a mudra. He found that bastions and protection walls were built on boulder foundation. This technique is used even now for building on reclaimed land. The evidence of reclamation matches the description in Harivamsa that 12 yojanas of land was reclaimed to build Dwarka. His excavations pushed the dating of Mahabharata earlier by about 5 centuries compared to the excavations of Prof B.B. Lal in Hastinapur. Source: Further Excavations of the Submerged City of Dwaraka. Prof S. R Rao Picture Credit: Lost City of Dwaraka. Prof S. R Rao

hook: Andal is a female saint, one among the 12 Alvars or Azhwars who lived in the 7th to 9th CE. Her compositions express purest love and devotion for Krishna and are sung widely in this South Indian State. Which State is it?
explanation: Azhwars catalysed the Bhakti form of worship, a passionate and total surrender. They made Vaishnavism popular in South India. Andal is the only female saint among the 12 Azhwars. Her father found her in a Tulsi garden and brought her up. As a child, Azhwar felt deep love and affection for Krishna and would imagine her to be Krishna’s bride. She adorned herself with the garlands that her father had prepared for the Lord. When her father scolded about it, Lord Ranganatha appeared in his dreams and insisted that he will only wear garlands worn by Andal. She is believed to have merged with Lord Ranganatha in Srirangam in Tamil Nadu. She is the most loved of Tamil saint poets. She composed Thiruppavai, an extraordinarily beautiful Tamil poem of 30 verses. In this poem Andal imagines herself to be a cowherd girl during the incarnation of Lord Krishna. She yearns to serve Him and achieve happiness not just in this birth, but for all eternity. Thiruppavai is sung in all Sri Vaishnava temples and Andal finds a place in all Sri Vaishnava temples. Ecstatic Krishna bhakti was later spread by Chaitanya Mahaprabhu in the East India, Mirabai in the North and by the Varkari/Pandharpur tradition of Maharashtra. Source: ramanuja.org

hook: Henry Ford’s great grandson is the biggest donor of this under construction temple, which will probably be the largest Hindu temple in the world. Where is it coming up?
explanation: ISKCON movement is another step in the evolution of Krishna worship. The movement has drawn people across the world towards Krishna Bhakti. ISKCON was founded by Swami Prabhupāda in New York City in 1966 and has rapidly grown in over 500 centres across the world. ISKCON celebrates the legacy of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. Mayapur, on the banks of Ganga, is close to Kolkata and is the birthplace of Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. The Chandrodaya Mandir or the Temple of the Vedic Planetarium will open its first phase in 2024. Alfred Ford, now known as Ambarisa Das is the largest donor and is the Chairperson of the project. The Vedic Planetarium in the temple will take visitors through the various regions of the cosmic creation. It features a giant rotating model that would present the movements of the planetary systems as described in sacred texts such as the Srimad Bhagavatam. When completed it may be the second largest religious structure and the world’s largest temple. It looks like Capitol Hill, because it is modelled on it, as per the wishes of Swami Prabhupāda. Source: tovp.org, mayapur.com Picture Credit: tovp.org

hook: ‘Gudi Padwa’ is the traditional new year day in Maharashtra & Konkan. What does ‘Gudi’ mean?
explanation: Gudi Padwa, also called Samvatsar Padvo, is celebrated on the first day of the Chaitra month (March/ April) of the Hindu calendar. The name Gudi Padwa is made using two words. ‘Gudi’ means flag or emblem. ‘Padwa’ means the first day of the phase of the moon. On this day, Lord Brahma is believed to have created the universe and the concept of days, weeks, months and years. On Gudi Padwa, a long bamboo stick is fixed onto the ground. Its tip is tied with a bright colored zari cloth, and some gaathi (sugar crystals), neem leaves, mango leaves and a garland of red flowers. The ingredients represent the sweet and bitter natures of life. A copper or silver pot (kalash) is placed in an inverted position over the tip of this stick. The Gudi signifies the victory of good over evil and wards off negative energies. Picture Credit: Rogertex2100, Flickr.

hook: Kannada & Telugu speaking people celebrate ‘Ugadi’ as their new year. What does ‘Ugadi’ mean?
explanation: Ugadi, Yugadi or Samvatsaradi is celebrated on the first day of the waxing phase of the moon in the month of Chaitra. Hindus living between the Vindhya-Kaveri belt celebrate it as a New Year day. The name Ugadi or Yugadi is derived from the Sanskrit roots of Yuga or "age"; and Adi, which means, "beginning." Combined, this word means "the beginning of a new age." The reckoning of time is supposed to have begun from sunrise on this day. A dish with six tastes is prepared on Ugadi. It is called Ugadi Pacchadi in Telugu and Bevu-Bella in Kannada. Neem flowers, jaggery, tamarind, raw mango, salt and chilli are common ingredients though there are variations. The pacchadi symbolizes that we accept life in the year ahead with all its different experiences. Source: Hindu Festivals and Sacred Days, RamaKrishna Mutt. The Hindu New Year – Flavors of Celebration, www.dollsofindia.com Picture Credit: Canva

hook: In this State, new year is marked by seeing, first thing in the morning, a plate of offerings. Children, blindfolded or with closed eyes are led to the Puja room by elders early in the morning. Where is this new year celebrated?
explanation: Vishu, the new year, is an important festival for Malayalis and is second only to Onam in importance. It falls on either April 14th or 15th of each year. "Vishukani," means the "first thing seen" on the morning of Vishu. For this ritual, people arrange articles such as clean white cloth, jackfruit, raw rice, fresh vegetables and fruits, coconut with betel leaves, arecanut, the sacred Konna Poo (the bright yellow Cassia fistula variety of flowers), sacred texts, mirror, gold, silver, coins and money. These are kept in front of an image of Krishna and alongside a nilavilakku (lighted lamp). This Vishukani is prepared the previous night, just before going to bed. Kanikanal is the seeing of Vishukani first thing in the morning. The elder women lead children blindfolded to the Vishukani. Source: The Encyclopedia of New Year’s Holidays Worldwide, William Crump. The Hindu New Year – Flavors of Celebration, www.dollsofindia.com Picture Credit: Aroon Kalandy, Flickr.

hook: Cheti Chand is the new year day of which community or region?
explanation: Cheti Chand is an important festival for Sindhis. This day falls on the first day of the lunar month Chet (Chaitra) as per Sindhi calendar. This gives rise to the name Chet-i-Chand – the rise of full moon in the Chaitra month. Sindhis commemorate the birth of Uderolal, more popularly known as Jhulelal, the Patron Saint of the Sindhis. On this day, people worship water, the giver of life. It is said that Jhulelal worshipped Lord Varuna, the God of Water. Source: The Hindu New Year – Flavors of Celebration, www.dollsofindia.com Picture Credit: Latestly

hook: For Punjabis, Baisakhi is also a special day in Sikh history. What event in Sikh History are we referring to?
explanation: Tamils (Puthandu), Punjabis (Baisakhi), Odiyas (Bishuva) & Bengalis (Pohela Baisakh) share the same new year though they are geographically far apart from each other. They follow solar calendars unlike other parts of India. Their new year normally falls on April 14th or 15th. Baisakhi is special for Sikhs for other reasons as well. Guru Gobind Singh founded the Khalsa panth on Baisakhi day in 1699 in Anandpur. Takht Keshgarh sahib marks the spot where he administered amrit to the Panj Pyaras, the five beloved ones, and baptized them as Khalsas. The panj pyaras came from different parts of India. Source: Sikh Shrines in India, GS Randhir Picture Credit: 300 year anniversary of Baisakhi at Anandpur Saheb, Gurumustuk Singh, Flickr

hook: What is the Parsi new year day called?
explanation: Zoroastrians worldwide have three calendars, the Shahenshahi, Qadimi and Fasli calendars. The New Year as per Fasli calendar is the Spring Equinox and therefore Navroz is on March 21. However Parsis in India mostly follow the Shahenshahi calendar. It does not take leap years into account. Therefore Parsi New Year is celebrated in India with a lag, now in the month of August. The celebrations begin on the eve of the New Year and are known as Pateti._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Prochy Mehta, Telegraph_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Asian Age

hook: The Kashmiri new year day Navreh also marks the start of the Chaitra Navratras. Who do Kashmiri Pandits worship in these Navratras?
explanation: Navreh customs are remarkably similar to Vishu of Kerala that is on the other corner of India. Kashmiri Hindus consider Navreh to be as sacred and holy as Maha Shivaratri. New year in many parts of north India is associated with the first day of Chaitra (spring) Navratra, though the autumn Navratra (Durga Puja/ Dussehra) is more well known. On the eve of Navreh, a plate is prepared with many offerings including the nashpatra (almanac) and kreel pach(a scroll) that has the likeness of Ma Sharika, the Goddess venerated by Kashmiri Pandits. The plate also includes other items such as flowers, walnut, wye (a bitter herb), coins, pen, ink bottle, cooked rice etc. These represent food, knowledge and wealth. The entire family gathers together to look at this plate first thing in the morning. This is so similar to the Vishukani custom in Kerala. A special dish called Tahar is prepared which is turmeric rice with ghee. It is believed that Saptarishi era of the Kashmiri Hindus started on the day of Navreh, more than 5000 years ago. It is believed that the seven rishis gathered at the dwelling of the Mother Goddess Sharika Devi to proclaim a new era. Source: The Encyclopedia of New Year’s Holidays Worldwide, William Crump The Hindu New Year – Flavors of Celebration, www.dollsofindia.com Picture Credit: Aditya Raj Kaul, Twitter

hook: Most Indians celebrate the traditional new year in March/ April. But in one region, the new year falls on the day after Diwali (Oct/ Nov). Which region/ community celebrates this new year?
explanation: Gujarat follows a luni-solar calendar like many others. However, the Gujaratis start the new year on the first day of the Kartika month. In some areas of Gujarat like Kutch & Saurashtra, the new year starts on the first of the Ashadha month (June/ July). Bestu Varas is the Gujarati New Year’s Day and people greet each other, saying "Nutan Varsha Abhinandan" or "Saal Mubarak". This day starts by bursting plenty of firecrackers, starting as early as 4 am. Young boys gather and sell raw salt, calling it "Sabras," meaning, "all tastes." People decorate their homes with aaso palav and marigold torans or door hangings and draw colorful rangolis at their doorstep. Source: The Hindu New Year – Flavors of Celebration, www.dollsofindia.com Picture Credit: Sam Panthkay, Mint

hook: Sarhul is a new year spring festival in which part of India?
explanation: The tribals of Jharkhand worship their sacred groves which are known as ‘sarnas.’ Such a grove must have at least five sal trees, which are held to be sacred. It is in these groves that the tribes of Chotanagpur venerate their God and spirits. The Sarhul festival is celebrated when the sal tree starts flowering, usually in the first fortnight of the month of chaitra, coinciding with the beginning of the new year in these regions. It is usually a three-day event. However, some groups worship it for a month-long period till the time jyeshtha (or jeth) arrives. Etymologically, ‘sar’ refers to sal tree while ‘hul’ is a collective beginning. Worship of sacred groves is an all India phenomenon and is very pronounced in Kerala, coastal Karnataka and Coorg. Source: Hinduism and Nature, Nanditha Krishna Outlook India Picture Credit: Gurpreet Singh, Wikimedia Commons

hook: Most new years in India mark a particular astronomical phenomenon. What are we referring to?
explanation: The spring or vernal equinox (Mesha Sankaranti) marks the start of spring. On this day both day and night are of equal duration. This is the traditional new year in most of India. It was also the new year in the first ever known calendar of humankind made by Mesopotamians around 2000 BCE. On the spring equinox (Mesha Sankranti) the sun passes onto the Aries zodiac. In solar calendars (Kerala, TN, Bengal, Assam), it is around April 14th or 15th depending on how the start of the day is calculated (sunrise to sunrise vs midday to midday etc.). This date is actually 20 days behind the actual spring equinox. The Mesha Sankranti varies in a luni-solar calendar as its date is dependent on the waxing or waning of the moon to mark the start of the month. Thus the same spring equinox is marked by a wide diversity of dates in India depending on the architecture of the calendars being used. Source: Various Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: India follows the Gregorian calendar but also has its national calendar. Which era is the national calendar based on?
explanation: The national calendar of India is based on the Saka Era. It was adopted from 22 March 1957. Chaitra is the first month and it has a normal year of 365 days. 1 Chaitra falls on 22nd March every year and on 21st March in a leap year. Both the Saka era and Gregorian calendar are official calendars. The Saka era began in 78 CE. It probably marks the victory of the Andhra Satavahana ruler, Gautamiputra Satkarni over the Sakas who were of Iranian origin. There is no consensus on this though. The other major era used in Hindu religious calendars is the Vikram era which began in 57BCE. It is supposed to mark the victory of Emperor Vikramaditya of Ujjain over an earlier generation of Sakas. Both Vikramaditya & Shalivahana are said to be from the same dynasty. Historians are unclear about the identity of these two kings. Other eras used in India include the Saptarshi or Laukika era, Kali era Kollam era and Bengali san etc. Source: https://knowindia.india.gov.in Picture Credit: Government of India

hook: The North Indian calendar is based on 12 lunar months. These don’t add up to a full solar year. Therefore the lunar calendar keeps falling behind the solar calendar. The calendar is adjusted to align these two systems. How is it done?
explanation: North India, Karnataka, Andhra/ Telengana follow the luni-solar calendar. Their year consists of 12 lunar months. This is shorter than the solar year by 11 days, so the lunar calendar keeps lagging behind solar calendar. To align the lunar year to the solar year, an extra month, adhikmaas (adhik – extra, maas – month) is added approximately every three years (or precisely 7 times in 19 years). The adhikmaas is added when two new moon days (Amavasya) occur within a solar month (when the Sun passes from one zodiac to another). The adhikmaas is also called maalmaas, maalimaacha, Purushottam maas etc. It is considered auspicious as the month of Lord Vishnu. 2023 will have an adhikmaas starting 18th July. Source: Keeping Track of Time, Rahul Basu Picture Credit: ISKCON Educational

hook: Randal Maataji temples are common in Gujarat and the Randal Ghodo festival is a popular event. She is related to Surya. How?
explanation: Randal Maa is the first wife of Surya and is also known as Sanjna or Sarenu. She could not sustain the heat of the sun and left the house after creating a shadow equivalent of her called Chaaya (shadow). She went to the forest for meditation. Surya found Sanjna in the forest in the form of a mare. They reconciled and came home. Surya also gave Sanjna a boon that married couples who worshipped her would always stay together. The Randal Ghodo festival refers to the horse form of her and women dance mimicking the movement of a horse. Both Sarenu and Chaaya are shown together in iconography._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Prof Gauri Mahulikar - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KUEqBDWMulQ , randalmaa.com_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Maheronline.org

hook: _x000D_ _x000D_ Gujarat celebrates Makar Sankranti as Uttarayan. The festivities are marked by a kite flying. This is also a popular way of celebration in another State. Which is this?
explanation: Kite flying is a very popular mode of celebration in Gujarat and Rajasthan. Both Ahmedabad and Jaipur now host an international kite festival during Sankranti._x000D_ _x000D_ Source:_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: First Sky Parenting

hook: Singanpur is the location where you can find one of the earliest representations of Surya on pottery. These belong to neolothic times. In which State is Singanpur in?
explanation: On the paintings of the rock shelters of Singanpur (Raigarh) area of Chattisgarh, there is a direct representation of the rising Sun with seven rays in the sky. There is a human figure below the rising Sun which appears to be in the posture of worship._x000D_ _x000D_ A similar representation of the half rising Sun is found in another rock painting from the same place. Scholars have tried t connect such representations with the seven horsed Sun of the Puranic tradition._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: VC Srivastava, Sun Worship in Ancient India_x000D_ J.N. Bannerjea, Development of Hindu Iconography_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: VC Srivastava

hook: _x000D_ _x000D_ The earliest human depiction of Surya occurs in Bhaja caves near Mumbai and in Bodh Gaya. To which religion can this be attributed to?
explanation: In Bodh Gaya, Surya is seen riding on a one wheeled chariot (ekachakra) drawn by four horses. He is attended on either side by a female figure shooting an arrow. They are the earliest depiction of Usa and Prayusa personifying dawn and driving away darkness._x000D_ _x000D_ Surya is depicted in Bhaja caves as a royal figure riding in the company of two women in a chariot. Its wheels pass over grossly proportioned nude demons seeming to float downwards in the air. This is identified as Surya floating through the sky with his attendants and destroying the evil powers of darkness._x000D_ _x000D_ Both of these are dated to around 1st century BC. Prior to this, the earliest representation of the Sun God in human form is found on a pottery piece of the Mauryan period in which the diety is standing with Aruna on four horsed chariot._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: VC. Srivastava, Sun Worship in India_x000D_ _x000D_ J.N. Banerjea, Development of Hindu Iconography_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: _x000D_ _x000D_ What does ‘makar’ in Makara Sankranti stand for?
explanation: Capricorn of the Zodiac. The Hindu calendar divides the sky into 12 parts and each part is named after a visible star. Makar Sankranti marks the transition (Sankranti in Sanskrit) of the sun into the Capricorn zodiac (Makar)._x000D_ _x000D_ On this day, the sun is believed to begin its northward movement (uttara-ayan) in the sky and is considered auspicious. However, in reality, Uttarayan was on 22nd December in the year 2022._x000D_ _x000D_ Source:_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Vedagayathri.com

hook: _x000D_ _x000D_ Sun worship in India is prevalent from the times of Vedas. Since when has Sankranti been celebrated? Can you guess?
explanation: There is no definite record of when Sankranti was first celebrated. However, two events associated with Makar Sankranti help us estimate the time of its first celebration._x000D_ _x000D_ Makra Sankrati marks the transition of the sun into Makar rashi. It is also considered the day when the Sun begins its northward movement (Uttarayan). However, at present Uttarayana falls on December 21/22 and not on January 14/15 when Sankranti is celebrated. But at some point in time, the days would have coincided. After that the two days would have drifted slowly apart due to a phenomenon called Precession, which describes the slow rotation of earth's axis forming a circle, once in about 26,000 years. (please see white circle in the picture)._x000D_ _x000D_ P.V. Kane ji observes, citing Brahmana and Upanishads, that udagayana had been regarded for many centuries before christian era as an auspicious period. Later on Makarasankaranti became a sacred day when Rashi system was introduced. He concludes that Makarasankaranti's origins could not have been much older than the beginning of the the Christian era._x000D_ _x000D_ Prof Raj Vedam estimates, based on the duration of precession that Uttarayan and Makar Sankranti coincided between 400 BCE and early Christian era._x000D_ _x000D_ Such an antiquity is not unlikely since evidence of Surya worship is present from the Vedic times itself._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Prof P.V. Kane, History of Dharmasastra_x000D_ _x000D_ Prof Raj Vedam, https://swarajyamag.com/science/when-did-we-first-celebrate-makar-sankranti_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: NASA, Mysid

hook: _x000D_ _x000D_ Tamil Nadu celebrates Makar Sankranti with a three/four day festival called Pongal. What does Pongal mean?
explanation: Pongal is made by boiling the newly harvested rice with milk and jaggery. It literally means ‘boiling over’ symbolizing a plentiful harvest._x000D_ _x000D_ The first day of Pongal celebration is called Bhogi and marks a new beginning. People discard old possessions and burn them in bonfires. The second day is the main celebration called Thai Pongal (Thai refers to the month) dedicated to Surya. and the Pongal dish is cooked on the day. Mattu Pongal is the third day dedicated to expressing gratitude to the livestock and farm animals, especially cattle._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Jagadisa Ayyar, South Indian Festivities_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Tamil people celebrating Pongal on the streets of Dharavi in Mumbai. S. R. Ramanujam

hook: _x000D_ _x000D_ Sankranti is also a day for bathing in sacred waters. This photograph captures a pilgrimage to a sacred waterbody along the Ganges. Which pilgrimage is this?
explanation: Gangasagar Mela is held at an island in the delta of River Ganga in Bengal every Makar Sankranti. It is said to be the second largest pilgrimage gathering after the Kumbh Mela._x000D_ _x000D_ It is associated with the story of Kapila muni who systematized Samkhya philosophy. In Puranic stories King Sagara’s sons falsely accused Kapila muni of stealing their sacrificial horses. Kapila muni in anger at the false accusation burnt them to ashes. Many years later, Sagara’s descendant, King Bhagirath came to Kapila muni and requested him to release his ancestors from hell. On Kapila muni’s suggestion, Bhagirath requested Ganga to come down to earth and the touch of her waters released Bhagirath’s ancestors from hell. It is believed that bathing in the Ganga at this spot on Makar Sankranti leads to salvation._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://www.gangasagar.in/en/about/mythologicalsignificance_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Pallabh Seth Flickr

hook: _x000D_ _x000D_ In this Sankranti sport Jallikattu, men engage in a contest to try and catch a running bull’s hump. They have to secure a pouch of coins hung around the bull’s horn to win the contest. In which State is this sport popular during Sankranti?
explanation: The term ‘jallikattu,' comes from Tamil terms ‘salli kaasu' meaning coins and kattu which means package tied to the horns of bulls as prize money._x000D_ _x000D_ There are references to bull-fighting practices during the Tamil classical Sangam period (400 BC–AD 200) wherein it is called Yeruthazhuvuthal (embrace the bull). An ancient reference to bull taming is found in a seal discovered at Mohenjodaro, dated 2,500 - 1,800 BC._x000D_ _x000D_ Surya has been represented as a bull in many ancient cultures along with other symbols like the rayed orb, wheel, swastika, horse and solar bird (eagle, garuda). In the Rig Veda as well Atharva Veda, sun is called a bull. The unicorn or urus bulls so profusely represented on Indus Valley seals might be the symbol of Surya as a sun disc like object with rays emanating, is found with these bulls._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: VC Srivastava, “Sun Worship in Ancient India”_x000D_ https://www.sahapedia.org/past-and-present-jallikattu-overview-0_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Manu Manohar Flickr

hook: _x000D_ _x000D_ The Indian civilization is credited with venerating multiple aspects of Sun. One aspect of Sun worship is considered unique to Indian civilization. Which is it?
explanation: The association of Sun with knowledge or enlightenment is considered unique to Indian civilization. The Gayatri mantra is an invocation to Savitri, one of the twelve forms of Aditya (Sun) in the Rig Veda. Savitri is the stimulating form of the Sun and came to be connected to wisdom and intelligence and became the symbol of ultimate knowledge._x000D_ _x000D_ The name Gayatri refers to the metre in which the hymn is composed. It is one of the most oft quoted hymns in Hindu literature, being referred to in the Brahmanas, Upanishads, Sutras and Puranas._x000D_ _x000D_ The knowledge function of the Sun is also referred to in the story that the great sage Yajnavalkya learnt and composed the Shukla Yajur Veda with Surya as his guru. Hanuman is called “gyan gun sagar” – the ocean of knowledge, and his guru in the Ramayana story is Surya._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Prof Deepali Patwadkar, Indic Studies Toronto_x000D_ VC Srivastava, Sun Worship in Ancient India_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Sunrise in Kanyakumari, S. R. Ramanujam

hook: _x000D_ _x000D_ Which great God of popular Hinduism is said to have evolved from one of the forms of Surya depicted in the Rig Veda?
explanation: One of the most popular names of Vishnu is Narayan and sun is also referred to as Surya Narayan. The Sudarshan Chakra is believed to represent the Wheel, a depiction of Surya. Vishnu’s three steps, later immortalized in the story of Vaman avatar, refers to sunrise, noon and sunset._x000D_ _x000D_ Vishnu has been interpreted as one of the twelve aspects of Sun depicted in Rig Veda. Vishnu is associated with fertility, procreation and vegetation and hence the benefactor of humanity. It is this role that is enlarged in the Puranic age to become the preserver of the universe who comes down to earth as an avatar to help and bless humanity._x000D_ _x000D_ _x000D_ _x000D_ Source: VC Srivastava, Sun Worship in Ancient India_x000D_ J.N. Bannerjea, Development of Hindu Iconography_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikicommons

hook: _x000D_ _x000D_ The Aditya Hridaya Stotram is a powerful invocation to Surya. It is set in a battlefield in one of our celebrated books. In which book will you find this Stotra?
explanation: Aditya Hridayam was recited by Sage Agastya to Lord Rama on the battlefield just before his battle with Ravana. It was meant to dispel fatigue – Hridyam stands for that which is especially nourishing and healing for the heart._x000D_ _x000D_ Source:_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Gita Press

hook: Which territory acceded to Pakistan first, but then joined India through a referendum?
explanation: Junagadh had 80% Hindu population but a muslim ruler. Nawab Mahabat Khan of Junagadh was an easy going man. He loved his dogs so much that he would have wild celebrations when his dogs gave birth._x000D_ _x000D_ Egged on by Jinnah, Junagadh acceded to Pakistan. Patel decided to use force. The Nawab escaped with the treasury and his dogs to Karachi. His Dewan handed over the territory to India and he too left for Pakistan. A referendum was held in Junagadh along with a few other neighbouring princely States. Out of about 2 lakh voters, only 91 voted to join Pakistan._x000D_ _x000D_ The Dewan was Sir Shah Nawaz Bhutto. His son, grand daughter and grand son in law went on to be leaders of Pakistan._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Rajmohan Gandhi, Patel – A Life._x000D_ Sandeep Bamzai, Princestan._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Natioal Virtual Digital Library. Celebration of Junagadh referendum resuts.

hook: The ruler of which princely state, related to Sarah Ali Khan, wanted to stay independent under British crown, and headed a group of like minded princes?
explanation: Nawab Hamidullah Khan of Bhopal argued that if Pakistan and India could be created as separate States, a third India consisting of princely States can also be created. He became the head of the Chamber of Princes and continued to argue for the right of States to stay independent. He even tried to create a corridor of princely states between Bhopal & Pakistan. He was eventually brought around by the combined persuasion of Patel and Mountbatten. After he signed the Instrument of Accession, he cordially wrote to Patel promising full co-operation. Patel too graciously remarked that the Nawab did what was right for his people. The Nawab's younger daughter married the Nawab of Patudi. Sarah Ali Khan is their great grand daughter in the line of Mansoor Ali Khan Pataudi and Saif Ali Khan. Source: V.P Menon, The Story of Integration of the Indian States. Sandeep Bamzai, Princestan. Bhopal.nic.in Picture: Wikimedia Commons- Picture of Chamber of Princes

hook: This Kingdom was one of the best run kingdoms and had made great economic and social progress. Yet it decided to stay independent. Which is it?
explanation: Travancore was considered a well run princely State. It had an excellent maritime economy and industrial base. Both the Maharajah and the Diwan (administrative head) disliked congress leaders and communists. They also were not in favour of India's relations with Soviet Union. Perhaps due to this, they declared that Travancore will remain as an independent State. CP Ramaswami Aiyar was the Diwan. He knew that western nations would support Travancore as it had thorium deposits (to create atom bombs) which the west would like to access._x000D_ _x000D_ CP was eventually convinced by the stature and commitment of Sardar Patel and agreed to get the Instrument of Accession signed by the Maharajah. Before he could do so, an assassination attempt was made on him in Trivandrum by socialists but he survived. The Maharajah telegraphed his acceptance of the Agreement to Mountbatten. This was instrumental in convincing other rulers who were wavering._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: V.P Menon, The Story of Integration of the Indian States._x000D_ _x000D_ Manorama Online_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: National Virtual Library, Flag of Travancore State

hook: Maharajah of Jodhpur almost joined Pakistan. What eventually swung his decision?
explanation: Jinnah tried his level best to lure young Hanwant Singh of Jodhpur to his side. Through Jodhpur (and Indore), Jinnah wanted to create a contiguous corridor from Pakistan to Bhopal. Jinnah offered Jodhpur the free use of Karachi port, free import of arms, jurisdiction over railways and supply of grains to his famine affected state. He even pushed across a blank sheet with his signature for the Maharaja to write his own terms. Hanwant Singh was advised to hold on and consult family elders by the Maharaja of Jaisalmer. The elders convinced Hanwant Singh by asking if he was willing to fight for Pakistan in the event of war between India and Pakistan. Patel also convinced him by offering good terms – a new port in Kutch, railway connectivity at Jodhpur, large scale food assistance and permission for the royal family to continue to carry arms. Source: Sandeep Bamzai, Princestan. Picture: Wikimedia Commons. Umaid Bhawan Palace built by Hanwant Singh's father.

hook: Which King reluctantly sent the signed Instrument of Accession by ordinary post?
explanation: The princely States had to sign the Instrument of Accession with either Pakistan or India; and also a Standstill Agreement which would maintain status quo till final terms of accession were agreed on._x000D_ _x000D_ The Maharaja of Indore was very close to the Nawab of Bhopal and was working along with him to delay accession. Patel and Mountbatten got the rulers of other prominent Maratha states, notably Baroda and Kolhapur, to speak with him. The Maharaja was especially annoyed with Mountbatten as he felt that as the Viceroy and the representative of the British Crown, he should have been more favourable to him. But VP Menon, Patel’s deputy in the states ministry, used equal measures of charm and threats to get him in line._x000D_ _x000D_ Perhaps as a signal of his annoyance, the Maharajah sent the instrument of accession and the standstill agreement by ordinary post._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Sandeep Bamzai, Princestan._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Wikimedia Commons, Patel with Maharajah of Indore

hook: The Maharajah of Cochin readily gave up his powers but had a simple demand. What was it?
explanation: The Cochin Maharajah went to great lengths to co-operate with Sardar Patel and VP Menon in the accession. He agreed to visit Maharajah of Travancore to merge both the States, to assuage the ego of the larger kingdom, but younger Maharajah. His only request was that five copies of Panchangam (almanac) of Cochin Government should be supplied to him free of cost (they would have cost a few annas). Later on he would negotiate his privy purse. VP Menon observed that the Maharajah was prepared to efface himself completely in order that his people might enjoy a larger life. Source: V.P Menon, The Story of Integration of the Indian States. Picture: Natioal Virtual Digital Library., Patel with Maharajah of Cochin

hook: Sir Sadul Singh was instrumental in convincing many reluctant princes to accede to India. Which state did he rule?
explanation: Lt Gen Sadul Singh, veteran of World War II, was a nationalistic voice in the Chamber of Princes. In April 1947, he wrote a secret note to his fellow princes titled “ The Time for Right Decision : An Appeal to the Princes.” The letter was brilliantly argued, mapping out all scenarios including the dangers of a Communist takeover. He was equally impassioned, “… the people of the (Princely) States feel equally in regard to their own Mother Country – India… We would be straining their loyalty, if we take a step considered unpatriotic by our people.” Historians consider this letter quite significant in helping the vacillating princes make up their mind._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Sandeep Bamzai, “Princestan”_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: National Virtual Library, Sardar with the Maharajah of Bikaner

hook: ‘Operation Polo’ was the name of ‘police action’ to secure the integration of which princely state?
explanation: Mir Osman Ali Khan, Asaf Jah VII, Nizam of Hyderabad. His territories were spread across Telangana, Maharashtra & Karnataka. He claimed a direct connection with Mughal royalty and the earliest Caliphs of Islam. The Muslim League tried its level best to secure independence for the state, even coining the term ‘Osmanistan’ for it._x000D_ _x000D_ The Nizam tried to refer the case of Hyderabad to the UN, gave a 20 crore loan to Pakistan, banned Indian currency in the territory and threatened to bomb Indian territory. The Majlis, led by Qasim Rizvi unleashed communal fury in the state through ‘Razakars,’ their foot soldiers. This hardened the stand of the Congress party and Nehru fully backed Patel when he ordered the army to move in._x000D_ _x000D_ Jinnah died on 12th Sept 1948 and knowing that Pakistan would be pre-occupied, India sent in the army. Razakars capitulated within 4 days._x000D_ _x000D_ Patel was generous after victory and allotted a handsome privy purse to the Nizam, allowing him to stay on as the titular head of state._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Rajmohan Gandhi, Patel – A Life._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: National Virtual Library, Hyderabad Nizam welcoming Sardar after Operation Polo.

hook: Which was the first Kingdom to sign the Instrument of Accession, the legal seal of merging with India?
explanation: Sir Pratap Singh Gaekwad of Baroda was the first ruler to actually sign the Instrument of Accession. However, the first announcement to sign the Instrument of Accession was made by the Diwan of Gwalior on behalf of the Maharajah._x000D_ _x000D_ Bikaner and Patiala were the other States that acceded to India in the very early days._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: V.P Menon, The Story of Integration of the Indian States._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Wikimedia Commons, Flag of Baroda State

hook: Patel offered to provide the princes a grant from the treasury in perpetuity, to alleviate the loss of kingdom. What was it called?
explanation: In December 1947, Patel was negotiating with the roughly 40 small states of Odisha and today’s Chattisgarh. Most of them were too small. Patel conceded that it was “elementary justice that some form of quid pro quo should be conceded to them.” Patel offered the rulers a privy purse for their maintenance. It would also continue to their successors and would be in perpetuity. When the princes wanted more, he advised them to keep it reasonable as a high amount will attract attacks on the very concept. The Privy Purse was made a part of the constitution. Nevertheless, twenty years later, Mrs. Indira Gandhi found a way to abolish these purses, as part of her socialist agenda. The courts agreed with her stand. Source: Rajmohan Gandhi, Patel – A Life. Picture: National Virtual Library. Shankar Pillai cartoon depicting Sardar Patel as Pied Piper leading the princes

hook: Johannesburg - A Russian writer was the inspiration for Gandhi’s ashram in South Africa. Who was it?
explanation: Leo Tolstoy’s book “The Kingdom of God is Within You” inspired Gandhi to formulate his idea of ‘Satyagraha’ (passive resistance). Gandhi was not successful as a lawyer in India and he moved to South Africa._x000D_ _x000D_ He started the Natal Indian Congress in 1894 to fight for the rights of Indian settlers. 1908 onwards, he launched a major agitation to protest laws which among other things derecognized traditional Indian marriages. He led a great march in 1913 that served as a template for the famous Dandi march._x000D_ _x000D_ Gandhi established the Tolstoy ashram close to Johannesberg in 1910, to coordinate the agitation and train his supporters in Satyagraha. It was funded by his German Jew friend, Herman Kallenbach, who later became a Zionist._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Livemint, “The Gandhi You Know, and the One you Don’t."_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: The Wikimedia image is of Gandhi and Kasturba at Tolstoy Farm in 1912

hook: Wardha - A great industrialist, who called himself Gandhi’s fifth son, funded the Sevagram ashram at Wardha in Maharashtra. Who was it?
explanation: On the lines of the Tolstoy Ashram of South Africa, Gandhi started his first Indian ashram at Kochrab in 1915. He took in a Dalit family in the ashram. This led to drying up of funds. He was then quietly supported by the industrialist Ambalal Sarabhai. Ironically, Gandhi would lead a workers agitation in Sarabhai’s mills but they remained friends. They moved their ashram to the banks of Sabarmati in Ahmedabad after two years on account of plague outbreak. This ashram became his home for many years._x000D_ _x000D_ In the 1930s he shifted to Sevagram near Wardha in Maharashtra. Sevagram was a model of village industries that Gandhi championed. Bajaj was intent on fighting imported clothes made from Indian raw material and collaborated with Gandhi. He even went to jail in the Quit India agitation of 1942._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Livemint, “The Gandhi You Know, and the One you Don’t."_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: The Wikimedia image shows Gandhi’s residence at Sevagram.

hook: Wife’s deathbed - Kasturba Gandhi spent her last days with Gandhi in his prison at the Aga Khan Palace in 1944. Where is this palace?
explanation: Gandhi had been imprisoned at Pune after the Quit India agitation. He undertook a 21 day fast in 1943 and many of his associates did the same. The ordeal of Gandhi affected Kasturba and she suffered two heart attacks. The British allowed her to spend her last days with her husband in jail. She is supposed to have passed away with her head on his lap. They were married for 62 years._x000D_ _x000D_ He said after her death, “In the current sense of the word, she was uneducated; but to my mind she was a model of true education”._x000D_ _x000D_ The parents of Kasturba were wealthy merchants and friends with Gandhi family. They were married at a young age. Kasturba was a strong willed person and Gandhi found it hard to convince her. But she always supported him in his ventures, taking personal care of his ashrams, being a willing volunteer of his quaint theories on diet, medicine & community living and going to jail multiple times._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Prabhash Datta, India Today, “Why Mahatma Gandhi said Kasturba stood above him”_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: The Wikimedia image shows Gandhi and Kasturba in 1915.

hook: Champaran - Gandhi’s first satyagraha in India was at Champaran. In which state is it located?
explanation: Gandhi’s first Satyagraha in India took place at Champaran district in northern Bihar. He went there in April, 1917. Farmers were bound by law to plant three out of every twenty parts of their land with indigo under the Tinkathia system. Gandhi knew nothing of Champaran or indigo, so he did what was to become his future template. He visited villages, cross-examined 8,000 cultivators and recorded their statements. One of the volunteers was Rajendra Prasad who would go on to be the first President of free India. He came to the conclusion that the ignorance of the cultivators was one of the main reasons why it was possible for the European planters to repress them. Gandhi therefore set up voluntary organizations to improve the economic and educational conditions of the people. They opened schools and also taught the people how to improve sanitation. The awakening of the people forced the government to enact a law to regulate the cultivation, giving relief to farmers. Champaran satyagraha was where Gandhi put his ideas of Swaraj into action. Source: mkgandhi.org Picture: India Post

hook: Night of Freedom - Where was Gandhi on 15th August 1947?
explanation: Gandhi was in Calcutta trying to secure peace between Hindus and Muslims. Gandhi was on way to Noakhali, now in Bangladesh, to shield Hindus from communal violence. While stopping at Kolkata, the Muslim League leader Suhrawardy asked him to protect the Muslims of Kolkata. Gandhi agreed provided Suhrawardy helped the Hindus of Noakhali. He stayed at Hyderi Manzil in a Muslim neighbourhood, now renamed Gandhi Bhawan. Over a span of a few weeks which included a long fast, he was able to rebuild trust and restore some semblance of peace in Bengal. Many have questioned his role whether he was soft on perpetrators of violence. But others have extolled his role. Manu Gandhi wrote a book, “The Miracle of Calcutta.” Dominique Lapierre & Larry Collins in their book “Freedom at Midnight” called him ‘a one man border force.’ He was able to keep the peace in Bengal while a 50000 strong border force struggled to do the same in Punjab. Source: www.firstpost.com Picture: The Wikimedia image shows Suhrawardy fasting with Gandhi.

hook: Ahmedabad - Which lawyer refused to stop his game of bridge and ignored Gandhi when he walked past his table at Gujarat Club in Ahmedabad in the summer of 1916?
explanation: Patel studied law in London and became a lawyer at Ahmedabad. He was an expert bridge player. He seemed to be destined for judgeship or knighthood or both. When Patel learnt about Gandhi, he joked that “we have too many Mahatmas” and called him a “crank.”_x000D_ _x000D_ When Gandhi came to Gujarat Club for a speech, Patel preferred to play bridge. A friend forced him to get up and listen to Gandhi. Patel, always attracted to the idea of renunciation, found his friend and mentor in the abstemious Gandhi. Patel became the most effective leader of satyagraha – Kheda (1918), Borsad (1923) & Bardoli (1928) – earning him the title of Sardar (leader)._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: “Patel, A Life”, Rajmohan Gandhi._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: The Wikimedia image is of Patel with Gandhi

hook: Chatham House - In Oct 1931 at Chatham House, London, Gandhi said, “…India is more illiterate than it was hundred years ago… the beautiful tree has perished…” What did he refer to as the beautiful tree?
explanation: In his speech, Gandhi referred to the traditional village school system of India centred around temples and madarsas, as the beautiful tree. He contended that Indians of all classes including girls had more access to education before the advent of the British formal school system. The latter was expensive and centred around district schools and thus exclusionary._x000D_ _x000D_ Gandhi was challenged by Sir Phillip Hartog, VC of Dhaka University. Gandhian historian, Dharampal took up the challenge and using surveys done by the British themselves, proved the achievements of India’s traditional education. Dharampal used the “Survey of Indigenous Education on Madras Presidency (1822-26)”, “State of education in Bengal (1835-38), W Adam” & “History of Education in the Punjab since Annexation and in 1882, GW Leitner” among other sources._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: “The Beautiful Tree”, Dharampal_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: “The Beautiful Tree”, Dharampal

hook: Land of Pathans - Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan was a staunch Gandhian in the most unlikely of places. What is he popularly called?
explanation: Ghaffar Khan, also called Baadshah Khan or Bacha Khan, was a leader of Pashtuns who live mostly in what was the North West Frontier Province of Pakistan. A great pacifist like Gandhi, he is sometimes referred as the Frontier Gandhi. He raised the Khudai Khidmatgars (Servants of God) or Red Shirts, a 100,000 strong organization wedded to non violence and strict discipline. Gandhi called it a miracle as the Pashtuns were considered to have a code of revenge of violence. Along with Gandhi, he was the most inveterate opponent of partition. Unlike Gandhi, he survived partition by 40 years. He spent the post partition years working for the rights of Pashtuns and was viewed with suspicion by the Pakistan government. He boycotted the 1947 referendum in the province since it did not provide an option of independence or joining Afghanistan. He was twice nominated for the Nobel price for peace and was awarded the Bharat Ratna in 1987. One of his biographers J Bright wrote that he was so wedded to non violence that Gandhi should be called the ‘Indian Khan’ and not the other way round. Source: The Print, thefrontiergandhimovie.com. Picture: The Wikimedia image shows Ghafar Khan with Gandhi and Kasturba.

hook: Rangoon - In a radio speech from Rangoon in the midst of war in 1944, Netaji Subhash Bose referred to Gandhi by which title?
explanation: It is well known that Gandhi and Subhash Bose had their differences. Gandhi did not favour Bose as Cong president and was responsible for him leaving the party. They also differed in their thinking with Bose in favour of revolutionary tactics to get freedom. Nevertheless they retained great affection for each other. Bose asked for Gandhi’s blessings in a speech on Azad Hind Radio from Rangoon in June 1944 even as he was at war with the British. He said, “...........Nobody would be more happy than ourselves if by any chance our countrymen at home should succeed in liberating themselves through their own efforts or by any chance, the British Government accepts your `Quit India' resolution and gives effect to it. We are, however proceeding on the assumption that neither of the above is possible and that a struggle is inevitable. Father of our Nation in this holy war for India's liberation, we ask for your blessings and good wishes." Source: mkgandhi.org. Picture: The Wikimedia picture shows Gandhi, Bose and Patel at the momentous Haripura Congress.

hook: The land of Dhoti - Gandhi adopted his signature attire of dhoti and shawl after a trip to this South Indian town in 1921. Name it.
explanation: On 22nd September 1921, Gandhi made a momentous decision to change his attire. He decided on a simple dhoti and shawl to identify with the poor peasant who he wanted to serve. He stuck to this dress code even on his trip abroad and until his very last moment! Gandhiji’s desire to identify himself with the poor masses was not a momentary decision. He had been contemplating about it for a long time._x000D_ _x000D_ On two earlier occasions, he had thought of donning the common man’s clothes but it was ultimately in Madurai (Tamil Nadu) where he finally took the plunge. He remarked later that it was Madurai that gave him the necessary strength to take the decision._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: pib.gov.in_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: The Wikimedia image shows Gandhi wearing a business suit in South Africa in 1909.

hook: Land of learning - Which famous contemporary university did Gandhi inaugurate in 1916 in the presence of the British viceroy?
explanation: BHU was the brainchild of Pandit Madan Mohan Malviya, a senior Congress leader. It was inaugurated by Gandhi on 4th Feb 1916 in the presence of Viceroy Hardinge, Maharaja of Darbhanga and Annie Besant. In his speech Gandhi underlined the importance of education that cultivated Indian values. Source: mkgandhi.org Picture: The Wikimedia image shows Madan Mohan Malviya at a function in BHU.

hook: The final step – The most iconic pictures of Gandhi’s funeral are by the first woman photographer of India. Who was she?
explanation: Homai Vyarawalla (1913-2012) was a pioneer in more than one sense of the word. She was India’s first female photojournalist. She documented the overthrow of British colonial rule. Homai spent her earliest years in and around Bombay and studied photography at the JJ School of Art. Her presence as a woman in a sari managing a Rolleiflex, led many to dismiss her as an amateur. This dismissal seems to have come with some benefits, however, as it granted her unhindered access to her subjects. Her photos were initially published under her husband’s name and then later under a pseudonym she invented for herself, ‘Dalda 13’. It was not long before her work gained national regard. Source: hundredheroines.org Picture: The Wikimedia picture shows her photograph of Gandhi’s death procession.

hook: Shivaji escaped from Aurangzeb in this city. So, ironically, atleast for Aurangzeb, this became a city that generated hatred for Shivaji. He would lament the error till his death. Which city is this?
explanation: Aurangzeb's general Mirza Raja Jai Singh laid a siege at Purandar. Shivaji was unable to break the siege. As a settlement, Shivaji handed over twenty three of his forts retaining only twelve. He also agreed to visit Aurangzeb in Agra to receive a mansab. At Agra, Shivaji felt insulted by the lack of protocol offered to him at Aurangzeb's darbar. He discarded the khilat given by Aurangzeb. He was soon placed under house arrest. Shivaji raised a loan and began distributing gifts to saints and bribes to the Mughal courtiers. He then made his sensational escape from Agra. He returned to his capital Rajgad disguised as a travelling mendicant._x000D_ _x000D_ Aurangzeb would lament in his will, "The escape of the wretch Shiva took place through my carelessness, and I have to labour hard to the end of my life."_x000D_ _x000D_ That is the irony of the city of Taj creating lifelong agony for Aurangzeb._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Agra Fort, S.R Ramanujam

hook: Shivaji’s step brother Venkoji independently conquered a city. It would be the southern extreme of Marathas. Which city did he conquer?
explanation: Raja Venkoji was the step brother of Shivaji. He conquered Thanjavur on behalf of Bijapur Sultanate in 1676, but then established his own independent State. Thanjavur was an extremely fertile coastal plain with its own sea ports. It was a prize acquisition._x000D_ _x000D_ Shivaji and Venkoji met during the conquest of South. Venkoji died a year after Shivaji's death._x000D_ _x000D_ The Thanjavur state was placed under the control of Nawab of Arcot who was appointed by Mughals. The Marathas liberated Thanjavur in 1740. The Nawabs retook it soon but in turn came under British. Thanjavur became a subsidiary of British in 1775. Under Doctrine of Lapse it came within British provinces in 1855._x000D_ _x000D_ Under Maratha rule, Thanjavur was the fountainhead of culture, music, drama and literature for over a century. The rajas, especially Serfoji, were true patrons of arts._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is that of the Bell Tower in Thanjavur Palace which was built by the earlier Nayak rulers._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: P Jegannathan, Wikimedia Commons

hook: The Maratha forts in Maharashtra are legends. The Gingee fort is outside Maharashtra. It was a central feature of Maratha hold over this region. In which State is this fort?
explanation: Gingee is in Tamil Nadu. It is about 160 kms from Chennai. As a part of Dakshin Digvijaya, Shivaji took over the fort which was held by the sons of former Wazir of Bijapur. During his stay at Ginjee, Shivaji built up the defenses of this fort making it one of the strongest in India. This was to serve the Marathas in good stead in the years to come._x000D_ _x000D_ Shivaji's second son Rajaram would flee the Mughal siege of Raigad and Panhala and reach Ginjee in 1689. Gingee came under siege by Mughals. The Maratha chiefs Santaji and Dhanaji would force Mughals to lift the siege by 1693. From Gingee, they terrorised Mughal army in the region but turned into rivals soon after. They fought each other near Gingee in 1696. Santaji fell out of favour with Rajaram and had to flee to Maharastra where he was killed. This setback invited another Mughal siege of Ginjee, which eventually fell. The fort would change hands often and finally came under British._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Karty Jazz, Wikimedia Commons

hook: This city was an unlikely host of Shivaji in his last campaign. He didn’t fight here, but marched through the city welcomed by its Sultan. Which city could this be?
explanation: The Sultan Abul Hasan of Golconda was advised by his minister Madanna that with the support of Shivaji, he could expand his kingdom into Bijapur. A willing but nervous Sultan laid out a welcome for Shivaji and his troops at Bhaganagar, as Hyderabad was then known. Shivaji, on his part, accorded due respect to the Sultan during his visit. After a three hour meeting with the Sultan and after receiving many gifts, Shivaji marched on south on his Dakshin Digvijay and laid siege to Ginjee fort._x000D_ _x000D_ Much later, in 1760s, the Nizam of Hyderabad attacked Pune forcing people to flee the city. Pune was ransacked by the Nizam. In retaliation, Raghunathrao attacked Hyderabad and burnt its suburbs._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Sailko, Wikimedia Commons

hook: Chimaji Appa defeated a foreign power in the battle of Bassein (Vasai in Mumbai) in 1739. Who were their opponents in this battle?
explanation: The Konkan had been under Portuguese rule from the north of Mumbai upto Daman. They had built a string of forts along along the coastline. They had a strong artillery with mounted guns on the bastions._x000D_ _x000D_ Vasai fort is surrounded by sea on three sides with a long exposed access towards the land. Over a two year campaign that ended in 1739, Chimaji Appa besieged and eventually broke through inside the Vasai fort. The Portuguese surrendered and were allowed to evacuate to Goa peacefully with honour. It was the first time in many centuries that an oriental power had beaten a European power in battle._x000D_ _x000D_ The victory at Vasai liberated people living under oppressive Portuguese rule. Chimaji Appa would later write that the Lord's sudarshan hit the head of the fanatics who harbour religious ill-will._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Himanshu Sarpotdkar, Flickr

hook: In a span of ten years, Mughal empire's greatest chieftain surrendered twice to Bajirao Peshwa, first time near Aurangabad (Palkhed) without fighting and second time in Bhopal. From then on, the Mughal empire was effectively under the protection of Marathas. Who was this Mughal chieftain who surrendered twice?
explanation: The Hyderabad Nizam. In 1728, Bajirao Peshwa trapped the Nizam of Hyderabad across the river Godavari with his moves and countermoves. Cutoff from his supplies and artillery, the Nizam surrendered without a fight._x000D_ _x000D_ In 1737, Bajirao Peshwa knocked on Delhi. Bajirao's attack on Delhi was the first since Akbar took over the city from Hemu. This sent shockwaves. The Nizam of Hyderabad was recalled to Delhi to oppose Bajirao. The Nizam set out towards Deccan from Delhi. Both armies met near Bhopal. The Nizam sheltered in the walled city of Bhopal. The Marathas placed the city under siege and starved the Nizam again. The Nizam signed a humiliating treaty handing over Malwa to Marathas, acknowledged Chhatrapati Shahu as the one true king and agreed to pay a large indemnity to Bajirao._x000D_ _x000D_ The Nizam returned to Delhi and the next year Nadir Shah sacked Delhi. The Mughal empire never recovered after that and limped on till 1857._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Vijae Goray, Wikimedia Commons

hook: What was the northern-most extent of the Maratha empire?
explanation: From the late 1740s, Ahmed Shah Durrani had been invading the Mughal empire. As the Marathas had agreed to defend the Mughals, this brought them into conflict with Abdali. In 1757, Raghunathrao, along with Scindias and Holkars wrested Delhi from Abdali's nominee. They marched northwards and took the cities of Lahore and Attock. They then crossed the Indus river and reached Peshawar from where the last vestiges of Afghans were evicted. This victory marked the furthest extent of the Maratha empire. For over a year, the Marathas, with Mughal officers ruled Lahore. Adina Beg Khan, a Mughal officer, was appointed as the Governor of Lahore._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Attock Fort, Optographer, Wikimedia Commons

hook: In which Indian city would you find the Maratha ditch? It was dug as a protection against Maratha raids.
explanation: Raghuji Bhonsle of Nagpur raided Bengal. The Nawab of Bengal sought the help of Mughal emperors who had in turn been promised support by Marathas. Nanasaheb Peshwa obtained an indemnity from the Nawab and attacked Raghuji. As a settlement Bengal and Orissa were placed under Raghuji's influence. Though Marathas are blamed for their excesses in Bengal, the stand in no comparison to the massacres of civilians by Abdali or by British at Jhansi. The Maratha ditch was dug as a protection against Maratha raids. It is covered up now and forms the outer circular road. Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: The third battle of Panipat was a bloody battle that Marathas lost. Madhavarao Peshwa led the revival of Marathas after Panipat. The Marathas managed to regain the lost territories and reached Delhi again. How long did this revival take?
explanation: Only ten years._x000D_ _x000D_ The Marathas had marched northwards to stop repeated invasions by Abdali from Afghanistan. Denied support by both the Hindu and Islamic powers in the northern India, the Maratha army fought Abdali at Panipat and lost severely. Abdali, although victorious did not want a second battle with Peshwas and sought a peace treaty. Abdali found the Sikhs more than a match in his subsequent invasions on their territory. Abdali never again entered the city of Delhi._x000D_ _x000D_ Within ten years of the loss at Panipat, a young Madhavarao Peshwa would recover the lost territories of Marathas and bring the northern India under Maratha power again. In 1772, Marathas brought back Shah Alam II from Allahabad and reinstated him on the Mughal throne._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Amit20081980, Wikimedia Commons

hook: The battle of Assayee (near Ajanta caves) in 1803 is another landmark battle featuring Marathas. It was fought by a famous British general who would later on defeat Napoleon? Who was it?
explanation: The batte of Assayee is part of the second Anglo Maratha war. It was a major loss for Marathas that led to Scindias, Bhonsles and Holkars becoming subsidiaries to the East India Company._x000D_ _x000D_ Bajirao II lost a major war between the Maratha houses to Holkars. He then signed a treaty with the British at Vasai in 1802 which made him nearly a British subsidiary. Within one year, the British took Pune, Ahmedanagar, Aligarh and Delhi from the Marathas._x000D_ _x000D_ In 1803, in a fiercely fought batte of Assayee (near Ajanta caves), the British defeated the combined armies of Scindia-Bhonsle. This triggered the Maratha houses of Scindia, Bhonsle and Holkar signing subsidiary agreements with British and handing over control of north and east India to the British._x000D_ _x000D_ The battle of Assayee and the second Anglo Maratha war broke the Maratha empire and the era of British dominance was upon India._x000D_ _x000D_ Arthur Wellesley, who commanded the British troops at Assayee would later on become Duke of Wellington and defeat Napoleon in Waterloo in 1815. He is quoted as saying that Assayee was the toughest battle that he had fought._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Thomas Lawrence, Wikimedia Commons

hook: It is not well known that people of African descent controlled parts of India. Marathas fought long battles with them. Which area did the African Siddis control?
explanation: The Abyssinian Siddis were based at Janjira and were a threat to the Marathas. From Alibaug southwards, a stretch of 200 kilometers was under the control of Siddis. In 1672, Shivaji blockaded Janjira. Over a period of time, the Siddis changed their allegiance from Bijapur to Mughals to British. The Marathas continued to fight them well into the eighteenth century. In 1732, Bajirao took over the entire Konkan coast from the Siddis except the island of Janjira and a few forts to the south. In 1736, Chimaji Appa and Manaji Angre fought and killed Siddi Sat. From then onwards the Siddi’s hold over Konkan was broken leaving them only with the fort of Janjira._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Janjira Fort, Himanshu Sarpotdkar, Flickr

hook: The Maratha victories against the Nizam, Mughals and the British are well known. The Marathas also fought with other powers. Their shortest conflict was with which of these powers?
explanation: The Dutch._x000D_ _x000D_ The Marathas evicted Portuguese from Vasai and thus from Bombay too. They repeatedly defeated Hyder Ali (and his son Tipu Sultan) and extracted both territory and tributes. The Maratha navy and army shrunk the territory of Abyssinian Siddis in the Konkan to just Janjira fort._x000D_ _x000D_ There was a short naval fight in 1754 between three Dutch vessels and Maratha navy near Vijayadurg._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: http://vivaan.world/inhcrf_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: The Marathas extended their territory by appointing their generals in different regions. They became kingdoms of their own and consolidated the geographical hold of Marathas. Which of these was not part of Maratha confederacy?
explanation: As the Marathas expanded their footprint across India, Maratha commanders took charge of different regions which became kingdoms with full allegiance to the Marathas. They would later on fight amongst themselves. Eventually the Holkars took the battle to Pune and fought a combined army of Scindia and Peshwa. The Peshwa lost the war and therefore went over to British and signed the Treaty at Vasai. This led to the Second Anglo Maratha war and the eventual decline of the Maratha empire._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is a collage of the founding commanders of Holkar, Gaekwad and Scindia dynasties._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Marathas are not known to have left many architectural legacies in Maharashtra. However, they left a visible influence on a major Hindu pilgrimage site in India. Which is this?
explanation: Both Nanasaheb Peshwa and his son Madhavrao Peshwa strived to obtain possession of Kashi from the Nawab of Awadh. Radhabai, Bajirao Peshwa's mother, went on a pilgrimage to Kashi. Sadashiv Naik, a banker who stayed in Kashi, built the Dash-aswamedh Ghat and the Manikarnika Ghat out of funds provided by Bajorao Peshwa. Ahilyabhai Holkar built the Kashi Vishwanath temple. Bhonsles of Nagpur and Scindias built ghats. The last of the Peshwas, Amrutrao and Chimaji Appa II spent their last days in Kashi and left behind many ghats and buildings that survive even today._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is of Devi Ahilyabhai Holkar in the Kashi Vishwanath Corridor._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Ashish Bhatnagar, Wikimedia Commons

hook: The unification of this country inspired our revolutionaries. Which country did Giuseppe Mazzini and Giuseppe Garibaldi unite?
explanation: The war for Italian unification in the 19th century was led by the firebrand thinker Giuseppe Mazzini and the rebel general Giuseppe Garibaldi. The combination in the Chankya-Chandragupta mode inspired the earliest Indian revolutionaries. One of the first books that Savarkar read in London was the biography of Mazzini. The Irish rebellion had a profound impact too, with the Hindustan Revolutionary Army styled on the IRA. The Shakta school of Hinduism which inspired Bankim Chandra’s Vande Mataram, was a big influence. The photo from Wikimedia Commons shows the first meeting of the two.

hook: The first shot in the revolutionary war for freedom was fired by Chapekar brothers. They assassinated a senior British officer in Pune in 1897. What were they protesting?
explanation: British rule in India was marked by series of famines & plagues. In 1897, a plague raged through Pune region. In the name of firm administration soldiers committed many excesses. Pune already was a hotbed of revolt under the influence of Lokmanya Tilak. The Chapekar brothers, Damodar & Balakrishna assassinated William Rand, the plague commissioner and his assistant on Ganeshkind Road as they attended a program to celebrate Queen Victoria’s jubilee. The British clamped down. The Chapekar brothers were betrayed by some friends, arrested and hanged. Tilak was tried and sent to prison for incitement. But the incident inspired the first generation of revolutionaries. The Wikimedia image shows the Chapekar memorial in Pune.

hook: This spiritual guru, who has a famous Ashram in his name in Pondicherry, was the first acknowledged leader of the revolutionary movement. Who is this?
explanation: Aurobindo Ghose was born in an upper class Bengali Anglophile family who despised Indian traditions. He went to Britain to train for ICS, discovered Indian traditions and refused to join the ICS. He returned to work for the princely state of Baroda. The Chapekar brothers, Bankim Chandra’s “Anand Math” and the partition of Bengal inspired him to setup a revolutionary movement. He wrote a tract “Bhawani Mandir” as the first testament, set up the Anushilan samiti, started papers ‘Jugantar’ and ‘Bande Mataram’. His brother Barin setup headquarters of Anushilan at Maniktola in Kolkata. Their associates, most notably Khudiram Bose, assassinated a few British officials. The British broke the movement and Aurobindo was tried in what is now called the Alipore Bomb trials of 1908. Many were hanged but Aurobindo was externed from British India. He setup base at Pondicherry, a French enclave. There he drifted into spiritualism, never to return to politics and became Sri Aurobindo. But the Anushilan movement carried the revolutionary spirit forward.

hook: Shyamji Verma, Oxford academic established a hostel for Indian students in London in 1905. It virtually became the revolutionary headquarters overseas. What was the hostel called?
explanation: Shyamji Verma, a Kutchi from Gujarat was a Sanskrit scholar who joined Oxford in the 1880s. He also completed law, returned to India to become dewan of three princely states and then went back to London and made a fortune with stock trading. With his money, he established India House for students. Vinayak Savarkar arrived in 1906 to study law and converted it into a hotbed of revolutionaries. Many great personalities like Gandhi ji and Lenin are said to have visited India House to meet rebel students.

hook: The book “Indian War of Independence 1857” corrected the British narrative that 1857 was a sepoy mutiny and not a war for independence. This was a major influence in the revolutionary movement. Who wrote this book?
explanation: Savarkar characterized 1857 as a revolt by Indian soldiers of all communities, to secure freedom. It contradicted the British narration of it as a mutiny. Originally written in Marathi to mark 50 years of 1857, the British went to great extent to prevent its publication - the viceroy monitored the case himself. No publisher came forth in India. It was translated and sent to Europe for publication. However the British had contacted most European governments and only a small publisher in Netherlands agreed. The book was smuggled into India under the cover of books like Don Quixote. It was a big hit underground. The book’s premise to sow seeds of revolt among the Indian soldiers to gain freedom, became the guiding principle of the revolutionary movement. The idea finally succeeded with Azad Hind in WWII and the Naval revolt of 1946.

hook: This Parsi revolutionary designed and unfurled the first Indian flag in 1907. A major Delhi office locality is named after her. Who is she?
explanation: Bhikaji Cama was born in a wealthy Parsi family in 1861. She held strong nationalist views. She moved to London as secretary to Dadabhai Naoroji, turned revolutionary and had to shift to Paris. She set up a network there along with SR Rana, a successful expat businessman. She arranged a failed getaway car for Savarkar as he escaped from the ship SS Morea._x000D_ _x000D_ She unfurled the Indian flag at the Socialist congress at Stuttgart, Germany. The flag was a tricolour with green, yellow and red bands. Vande Mataram was written in Devnagari in the middle band, a string of lotuses on the top band and a sun and moon on the lower band. Though not the most aesthetic design , it compelled the British delegation led by Ramsay Mcdonald, later PM of Britain to walk out. The Wikimedia image is a photo of Madame Cama and the first Indian flag.

hook: Who was the mentor of Netaji? He was the most tenacious of revolutionaries; he fomented rebellion among Indian soldiers in both world wars, dressed as a woman to throw bombs and introduced Indian cuisine to Japan.
explanation: Inspired by Aurobindo, Rash Behari Bose tried to assassinate the viceroy in 1912 during the ceremonial transfer of capital from Calcutta to Delhi. He and his associate were dressed as woman to avoid detection. He then tried to smuggle arms from Indonesia to foment revolt during WWI. Bagha Jatin was a key associate in this. The plot was uncovered and Rash Behari escaped to Japan. The Japanese govt under pressure from Britain went after him. He was hidden by few Japanese associates and during his hiding fell in love and married a Japanese girl. This allowed him to take Japanese citizenship and escape extradition. His wife’s family were restauranteurs and he introduced Indian cuisine in their setup. The marriage was very happy but his wife died early. Rash Behari continued to maintain contacts in SE Asia and when WWII started he was the one who first organized the INA or Azad Hind Fauj from among Indian soldiers held as prisoners of war by Japan. Impressed by Netaji, he helped him become the INA leader and the icon we all revere. He is said to have told Netaji, “one more fight, last and the best”. The Wikimedia image shows a photo of Rash Behari and his wife Toshiko.

hook: Komogata Maru, a Japanese ship carrying Punjabi immigrants to Canada was harassed due to a revolutionary movement. Which movement is this?
explanation: Punjabis had started migrating to Canada, a British colony, in the 19th century. Under British pressure, many ended up in California. Inspired by Aurobindo, the scholar Lala Hardayal started the Ghadar newspaper in San Francisco in 1913. This turned into a revolutionary movement under the patronage of Jwala Singh who made his fortune selling potatoes. Sohan Singh organized the Ghadarites who cooperated with Rash Behari to harass the British in WWI with German help. A Japanese ship Komogata Maru carrying Punjabi immigrants to Canada were unintended victims. Not related to Ghadar, the ship was still not allowed to dock in Canada and sent back to India, the Punjabis on the ship faced great hardship. The Wikimedia image is the actual photo of the Komaogata Maru.

hook: The dreaded ‘Kaala Paani’ suffered by numerous revolutionaries refers to imprisonment in which jail?
explanation: The British established a penal colony at Andaman, the Malay pronunciation of Hanuman. The Cellular jail where the British housed the most inconvenient prisoners was a living hell. Since Andaman was across the seas, a stint in Cellular jail was called ‘kaala paani’ (Black Waters) from which hardly any came back. The regimen at Cellular Jail was designed to humiliate prisoners. Prisoners had cross bar fetters keeping legs apart or an iron chain around the neck, for weeks. Many broke down or went mad. The dreaded place also kept true love alive. Ullaskar Dutt went into prolonged delirium but visions of his fiancée Lila, daughter of the famous leader Bipin Lal, are said to have kept him alive. He was freed under pressure but when he returned, Lila had married and he was heartbroken. In old age, Lila was widowed and paralysed and Dutt married her at last and took care of her. A love story for the ages.

hook: Which celebrated recent film is loosely based on the Rampa rebellion in Andhra in the early 20s?
explanation: RRR is a fictionalized retelling of the deeds of the Alluri Sitarama Raju and Komaram Bheem who led a two year rebellion of tribals of the Vishakapatnam area in early 1920s. Most likely inspired by meetings with Anushilan samitis in Bengal, Rama Raju started the rebellion as he was disappointed that Gandhi ji’s non-cooperation movement of the early 20s had yielded little.

hook: The Kakori train robbery led to the penning of the famous revolutionary song “Mera rang de Basanti chola” (Colour my clothes saffron). Who organized the robbery?
explanation: In the mid 1920s, the revolutionary movement which had regrouped after the failure of WWI rebellion was short of funds. Ram Prasad Bismil devised a plan to loot the government treasury when it was being moved by train. He and his associates, notably Ashfaqullah Khan, stopped the train at Kakori station in UP and looted it. In the next few months, the conspirators were hunted down and tried in the famous Kakori case._x000D_ _x000D_ In prison, Bismil wrote some of the famous songs associated with revolutionaries. Mera Rang de Basanti Chola was adapted for the 1965 film on Bhagat Singh, ‘Shaheed’. Bhagat Singh’s mother told the filmmakers that this was her son’s favourite song._x000D_ _x000D_ The Wikimedia image shows Bhagat Singh in prison.

hook: This great revolutionary had promised that no one would catch him alive. Betrayed, he fought a gunbattle at Alfred Park in Allahabad and killed himself with the last bullet. Who was it?
explanation: Azad, Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, Rajguru and others revived the Hindustan Revolutionary Association. They avenged the death of Lala Lajpat Rai during protests in 1928. Bhagat Singh and Batukeshwar Dutt would then explode a harmless smoke bomb in the Central Assembly in 1929, stand ground to be arrested, use the trial to draw attention to the injustice of British rule and even eclipse Gandhi ji for a while as the best known name in India of the time._x000D_ _x000D_ After these incidents, British arrested and hanged most. Azad evaded them but was betrayed. The famous Hindi writer and Padma awardee Yashpal is one of the suspects. The great revolutionary would take his pen name ‘Azad’ (free) seriously and shot himself._x000D_ _x000D_ The Wikimedia image shows the tree behind which Azad shot himself.

hook: Abhishek Bachchan plays the role of Surya Sen, a revolutionary in the film ‘Khelein Hum Jee Jan Sey.’ He looted an armoury. In which city was this?
explanation: On Good Friday, 18th April 1930, Surya Sen and his men looted the armoury at Chittagong and started a rebellion. Unfortunately, they got guns but not adequate ammunition. The band retreated to the surrounding hills. They fought the British for three days and surrendered. Surya Sen escaped, carried on but was eventually arrested and hanged in 1933.

hook: Mohammed Ziauddin played a major role in our freedom struggle. How is he better known as?
explanation: After falling out with the Congress, Bose formed the Forward Bloc and explored how to exploit WWII. In 1940, the British put him under house arrest at Elgin Road, Kolkata. He escaped, disguised as Mohammed Ziauddin, traveling inspector of an insurance company. He made his way to Afghanistan, thence to Germany in April 1941 and finally Singapore. There he setup the provisional government of free India in Oct 1943 and took charge of the Indian National Army (INA) made up of soldiers defecting from the British Indian Army._x000D_ _x000D_ The INA allied with the Japanese and fought the battles of Imphal and Kohima against the British Indian Army. They were defeated and their public trial by the British fired up the country. The Japanese had ceded Andaman to Bose’s government. Symbolically the first land to be freed, was the dreaded kaala paani. Bose could thus be regarded as the first leader of free India. He also chose ‘Jai Hind’ as the INA slogan and ‘Jana Gana Mana’ as the national anthem - his great legacies._x000D_ _x000D_ The Wikimedia image shows the currency issued by the Azad Hind govt of Netaji.

hook: The Naval Mutiny of 1946 was also instrumental in the hastening of India’s freedom. Mumbai was one location of the mutiny. What was the other?
explanation: The returning sailors of the Royal Indian Navy (RIN) from WWII were demobilized in very shoddy conditions at Mumbai. They were incensed by the trials of INA personnel. Small frictions led to a full fledged revolt in Feb 1946 . 20000 sailors, 78 ships, 21 shore establishments and two major cities – Mumbai & Karachi, were involved. The Maratha regiment disobeyed orders to shoot straight at the mutineers. Air force personnel asked to bomb them reported maintenance trouble in their planes.
