Finally, ethnic British troops were brought in to quell the revolt._x000D_ _x000D_ Clement Atlee, British PM in 1947, said that the INA and RIN revolts were the main reasons why the British withdrew from India. Unfortunately, independent India treated both the INA and RIN mutineers shoddily. Utpal Dutt’s Bengali play ‘Kallol’ on the naval mutiny was hounded by the state govt in the 60s._x000D_ _x000D_ The Wikimedia image shows the memorial to the Naval Mutiny at Colaba, Mumbai.

hook: The official name of our country is ‘Republic of India’. Indian Passports state the Hindi equivalent - Bharat Ganarajya. “Gana” refers to a republic. Which is the earliest recorded use of the term Gana in India?
explanation: Gana is derived from “to count” or “numbers.” It meant rule by numbers or a republican form of governance. “Bharat Ganarajya” means Bharat whose state is a “gana” i.e. a republic. The Hindi word for a republican form of government is “gana-tantra.” Gana is first mentioned in Rig Veda in 46 places. Its exact meaning in Vedic texts is interpreted variously, as tribes, as tribal republics or republican form of governance. Prof. Altekar of BHU wrote, “even if we grant that these were tribes, they also had a republican form of government. It can be proved beyond doubt that gana indicated a certain type of state, sharply distinguished from monarchy.” In any case, by the 6th century BCE, the association of ganas with republican states is well established in historical records. Source: A S Altekar, “State & Government in Ancient India” R. S. Sharma, “Aspects of Political Ideas and Institutions in Ancient India” K. P. Jayaswal, “Hindu Polity” Picture Credit:

hook: The word “Sabha” in Lok Sabha too is of ancient origin. It was used to refer to a council. Where is the first reference to “Sabha?”
explanation: Sabha is referred to in Rig Veda eight times. Sabha means “a body of men shining together,” thus referring to a group of eminent people. The head of the sabha was called sabhapati, another term that continues to be in use today._x000D_ _x000D_ Free discussions were held in the Sabha and a resolution of the Sabha was considered binding on all and inviolable. Sabha was also a judicial forum. Rig Veda describes a man who has come back successfully from Sabha as “free from blame.” The Jataka stories enjoin members of the Sabha to speak out Dhamma or justice._x000D_ _x000D_ In addition to Sabha, vedic texts refer to other forms of governance too, such as Samiti, Vis, Vidatha and Parisad._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: K. P. Jayaswal, “Hindu Polity”_x000D_ J. P. Sharma, “Republics in Ancient India”_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Republics had become common by the Buddhist period and influenced Buddhist principles. Which of the following aspects of Buddhism captures the prominence of republican traditions of those times?
explanation: Sangha is the monastic order of Buddha. “Sangham Sharnam Gatchami,” ( I take refuge in the Sangha), is a basic Buddhist chant. Sangha is one among the Triratnas (Three Jewels) of Buddhism, the other two being Buddha and Dhamma. Mahabharata and Panini use the term Sangha to describe a republic. The rules of the monastic order of Buddha were modelled on the rules of the republics in his time. The Mahaparinibbana­suttanta, a Buddhist text, mentions that the rules for conducting the Buddhist sangha were based in principle on those commonly found in political sanghas or ganas. Prof Jayaswal says, “The birth of the religious brotherhood of Buddha from the constitutional womb of an Indian republic is of interest to the world.” The key organizational virtue of Sangha was the full participation (like in a republic) of all the monks in the ritual and disciplinary acts of their group. Source: KP Jayaswal, “Hindu Polity”; C Rajendran, “Democratic Principles in the Polity of Ancient India”, SVU Oriental Journal, Golden Jubilee Volume. Steve Muhlberger, “Democracy in Ancient India” Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons, “The Buddha preaches the Vessantara Jataka to the Sangha and his Family, Chedi Traiphop Traimongkhon Temple, Hatyai”

hook: This republic in modern day Bihar is known to us in several ways. The Hindi film ‘Amrapali’ starring Vyjantimala and Sunil Dutt is set in this republic. The Republic formed the first known confederation with other republics. The Republic also survived for about 1000 years in some form or the other. Which is it?
explanation: Lichcchavi was a republic centered around Vaishali, north of Patna, across the river Ganga. It formed the Vajji confederacy with Videhas, Mallas and others._x000D_ _x000D_ Lichcchavi was ruled by a large body of families. The Jatakas refer to Lichcchavis having 7707 kings, probably a reference to the council of warriors. The Lalitavistara, in an obvious satirical jab, depicts Vesali as being full of Lichcchavi rajans , each one thinking, "I am king, I am king."_x000D_ _x000D_ Amrapali was born here and she was beautiful and perfect. It was a law in the Lichcchavi republic that the perfect woman cannot marry and should become common property of the State. Thus she became a courtesan. Bimbisara, king of neighbouring Magadha kingdom is reported to have had an affair with Amrapali. Bimbisara’s elder son Ajatasatru developed enmity with the kingdom and carried out a long-drawn battle. The Lichcchavis were defeated and their power was weakened. They rose again after the fall of the Mauryan empire. They continued till the Gupta era and thus reigned for about 1000 years. The first Gupta emperor rose to power through a marriage alliance with a Lichcchavi princess, Kumaradevi. The republic then slides into oblivion._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: A S Altekar, “State & Government in Ancient India”_x000D_ J. P. Sharma, “Republics in Ancient India”_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Cinestaan

hook: Both Buddha and Mahavira were born in republics. Buddha also attained Parinirvana in a republic. One of the names of Buddha is derived from the name of the republic in which he was born. Which republic is this?
explanation: Buddha is also known as Sakyamuni. Siddhartha, before he became Buddha, is represented as a royal prince. But his father Suddhodana was not a hereditary King, nor was there monarchy in Sakya region where he was born. Sakya was one of the republics of the era. All the chiefs of Sakyas, who were probably large and wealthy landowners, were called kings. The eldest son would inherit the position of the chief in the assembly and was called uparaja or prince. The multiplicity of chiefs or kings in Sakya is referred to by several scholars. Thus Buddha was not a crown prince the way we understand the term these days. Sakya was founded by a cadet prince of the Ikshvaku dynasty of Lord Rama. The Buddha fondly mentioned this fact many times in his life. Probably because of this connection, in later Puranic tradition, he was incorporated as Vishnu’s incarnation. Buddha attained Parinirvana at Kusinagara, which was in the Malla republic. Sakyas claimed a share in his relics and later built a stupa at their capital town. Both the Mallas and Lechcchavis are supposed to have requested Buddha to deliver a sermon during the opening of their assembly hall. Sakya was controlled by Kosala, the neighbouring monarchy, but was not fully subordinate to it, and had its own identity. However, during Buddha’s time, the Sakya state was invaded multiple times by the king of Kosala; and soon after destroyed. Literature mentions how the Sakya assembly met, discussed and voted to surrender, a republican way even in the dissolution of power! Source: KP Jayaswal, “Hindu Polity” A S Altekar, “State & Government in Ancient India” Koenraad Elst, Lectures on Buddha available on you tube. Picture Credit: Root Buddha at Ayuthaya, Thailand. S. R Ramanujam

hook: “There seems to be a strange fatality in their history. They rose to power with the help of a republic, they conquered most of the republics, and they in turn were shaken off their foundations by a republic”. About which great dynasty is this written?
explanation: The Guptas rose to power in the early 4th century through a marriage alliance - Chandragupta I married a princess of the Lichcchavi republic. Samudragupta is known as Lichchhavi-dauhitra (grandson of the Lichchhavis) in inscriptions. Their imperium of roughly 200 years saw the almost complete eclipse of republics in North India. The prashasti of Samudragupta on the Prayagraj Ashokan Pillar records the ganas that were overcome by Guptas. This included the Malavas, Arjunayanas, Yaudheyas, Madrakas, Abhiras, Prarjunas, Sanakanikas, Kakas and Kharaparikas. The Gupta’s rule was weakened by the short-lived republic of the Pusyamitras around the Vindhyas. The Emperor Kumaragupta is supposed to have been killed in battle with the them around 450CE. His successor Skandagupta defeated them but he candidly mentions that they had made the empire totter. Skandagupta revived the Guptas and even pushed back the Huna invasion but the empire went into terminal decline after him. Source: Upinder Singh, “A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India from the Stone Age to 12th Century” KP Jayaswal, “Hindu Polity”. Picture Credit:

hook: The Uttaramerur inscriptions are celebrated as a record of democratic traditions in South India from 1100 years ago. What tradition do these inscriptions attest to?
explanation: Uttaramerur is about 80 kms south west of Chennai, near Kancheepuram. Inscriptions on temple walls tell us about the democratic traditions of the village sabha starting in the 8th century._x000D_ _x000D_ The village sabha continued through the reigns of different kings in the region, from Pallavas to the Cholas and till the decline of Cholas. The sabha performed several public welfare functions, e.g widened roads, created drinking water facilities, set standards for testing gold, desilted tanks, took loans, alienated land to retire debt and created schools._x000D_ _x000D_ The village had a refined electoral system for its Sabha and committees (for testing gold, tank maintenance, gardens etc). It also had a written constitution prescribing the mode of elections. Interestingly, the constitution was also amended formally to alter voting procedures._x000D_ _x000D_ “This inscription, dated around 920 A.D. in the reign of Parantaka Chola [907-955 A.D.],” explains Dr. R. Nagaswamy, former Director of the Tamil Nadu Department of Archaeology, “is an outstanding document in the history of India. It is a veritable written constitution of the village assembly that functioned 1,000 years ago”._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: K. A. Nilakanta Sastri, “Studies in Cola History and Administration.”_x000D_ https://www.thehindu.com/news/national/tamil-nadu/Uttaramerur-model-of-democracy/article16566830.ece_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Nilakanta Sastry

hook: Kautilya’s Arthashastra advises that republics cannot be defeated in war. It recognizes the presence of republics; and provides advice on how they can be overcome. What is the method suggested in Arthashastra?
explanation: Kautilya considered republics as invincible through war. The republic was considered a nation-in-arms. The whole community was their army compared to hired soldiers of monarchies. The Arthashastra outlines the special strategies that the would-be conqueror could use to vanquish the ganas. Because they were different, the strategies recommended to defeat monarchies would not work. Kautilya’s advice focused on creating dissension among their ranks. He marked out personal rivalry and greed of power in aristocracies as a fit soil for sowing seeds of dissension. The Mahabarata also comments that it is almost impossible for the enemy to crush republics. Buddha too advised Ajatasatru that Lechcchavis, who were a republic, could not be conquered by war. Heeding the advice, Ajasasatru sent his minister to create dissension within the Lechcchavis and this strategy succeeded. Source: Upinder Singh, “A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India from the Stone Age to 12th Century” KP Jayaswal, “Hindu Polity”. Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: The Yaudheya republic halted Alexander from progressing further beyond the river Beas. Fear of Yaudheyas (and Nandas too) created doubts and dissent among Alexander’s troops triggering a retreat. Yaudheyas were also known as Mattamayurakas (dancing peacocks) as they were devotees of a God of war, now worshipped mostly in Tamil Nadu. Which God are we referring to?
explanation: Yaudheyas worshipped Kartikeya whose vehicle is the peacock. They were a large republic ruling from Bhawalpur in the West to Panchala in East, from Ludhiana in North to Delhi. Their war like reputation and size of their military alarmed Alexander’s army (as did the reputation of Nandas) which turned back from the Beas and did not cross into their territory._x000D_ _x000D_ They were temporarily defeated by Kanishka, the Kushan emperor but they reasserted their independence and dealt the Kushan empire a death blow in the process. They were governed by a central assembly with 5000 members. They pass out from history about 350CE._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: A S Altekar, “State & Government in Ancient India”_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Karthikeya at the Parasurameshwar temple in Bhubaneswar, S. R. Ramanujam

hook: The Andhaka Vrishnis were a republic in modern day Gujarat that were mentioned in Mahabharata, Panini and Arthashastra. The republic is also believed to have been headed by a key player in Mahabharata. Who was that leader?
explanation: The Andhaka Vrishnis were a republic with a dyarchy – there were two elected leaders whose titles included Vasudeva and Bhojaka. Their constitution was called Bhaujya in the Aitreya Brahmana. Bhojas later emerged as a separate clan & ruling dynasty and the city of Bhuj is their legacy. Krishna’s Yadavas are part of the Vrishni clan who migrated from Mathura to Dwarka. In the Mahabharata, Krishna mentions that “he had control over half his realm” alluding to this dyarchy. He laments to Narada that he has to endure a lot of nonsense in the assembly from rival factions. Towards the end of Mahabharata, we know that the clan and their city Dwarka were destroyed in a civil war between factions which also resulted in Krishna’s death. Puranic legend suggests that the Andhaka Vrishnis were cursed to never have kings, probably reflecting their proud republican tradition. Source: KP Jayaswal, “Hindu Polity” A S Altekar, “State & Government in Ancient India” Upinder Singh, “A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India from the Stone Age to 12th Century” Picture Credit:

hook: What is the significance of Kanak Mahal in Ayodhya?
explanation: Kanak Mahal is now a huge temple complex. It is built like a palace as it was gifted by Kaikeyi to Sita after her marriage to Lord Rama. It is recognized as an important centre of madhuropasana (sweet devotion). Traditionally musical gatherings have been organized on important occasions here. Stalwarts like Pt Jasraj have performed in Kanak Mahal._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ Ayodhya, UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ayodhya, UP Tourism

hook: What is the significance of Bharat Milap in Chitrakoot?
explanation: The Bharat Milap temple is on the spot where Bharat asked Rama to return to Ayodhya and take over the kingdom. There are footprints embedded on rocky surface believed to belong to the several personalities from the epic._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ Chitrakoot, UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Chitrakoot, UP Tourism

hook: Which place in the journey of Rama is also deeply connected with poet Kalidasa?
explanation: Ramtek literally means the place where Lord Rama stopped or rested. He is believed to have rested here for a while during his exile._x000D_ _x000D_ Ramtek is also the location from which Kalidasa is believed to have composed the immortal Meghdoot. The geographical features of Ramtek closely match the description in the verses of the poem._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni.

hook: Out of the following four places which lay claim to the location of the slaying of Jatayu by Ravana in Ramayana, only one is considered valid. Which is the valid one.
explanation: Le Pakshi is supposed to mean, Rise, O Bird in Telugu and is considered the place where Jatayu fell. Thiruputkuzhi has a temple pond dedicated to the bird. The Jatayu rock near Trivandrum is a recent construction. Though they are popularly believed to be the spots where Jatayu fell, Sarva Teertha at Taked near Nashik is considered as the spot where Jatayu was actually slain. It is about 40 kms south of Nashik, very much in the route that Ravana would have taken to go towards Sri Lanka. Picture Credit https //www.facebook.com/sandhya.bhatia.92_x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ www.facebook.comsandhya.bhatia.92

hook: Yedai or Yedeshwari means mad mother. This is a location near Solapur in Maharashtra. Shakti is worshipped here as a mad mother. What is the reason for this form of worship?
explanation: Parvati was amused by Shivji worshipping Rama. She taunted him saying that Rama is just a mortal and he himself is running around the forest trying to find his wife. She quizzed why Shivji should be worshipping the nomad. Shivji replied stating that Rama is not a mere mortal and that he is Vishnu himself in human form. Parvati wanted to prove Shiva wrong and appeared before Rama in the form of Sita to taunt him. Rama understood that it was Parvati and asked her beseechingly why she is tormenting him. This is why she is called Yedai, which means mad mother._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ www.facebook.comShreeYedeshwariDeviYermala

hook: In the village of Kardiguda near Hubli in Karnataka, there is a rock in the shape of a grotesque human being. The temple is called the Basavanna temple, after the modern social reformer of Karnataka. However the connection is far more ancient and is to Ramayana. How is this connected?
explanation: _x000D_ Kabandha is a demon whose face and neck were crushed into his stomach. His mouth was in the belly and he had no legs. He was blessed with arms 13 kms wide with which he used to grab and devour everything around him. When he tried to eat Rama and Lakshmana, they remained calm and Rama cut off his arms. This released Kabandha from his curse and he advised Rama on where to find Sabri who will in turn lead him towards Sugreeva. The grotesque sculpture is that of Kabandha which is now revered as Basavanna. Locals believe Basavanna held on to the demon who became part of his body._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Speaking Tree

hook: Which is the birthplace of Hanuman?
explanation: _x000D_ Anjeyanadri is a hill near Hampi in Karnataka which is accepted as the birthplace of Hanumanji. There are also competing claims for the birth place of Hanumanji from Gokarna in Karnataka and Anjanadri in Andhra. Hanumangarh is in Rajasthan. Anjeyanadri near Hampi is widely accepted as the birthplace of Hanumanji. There is a temple on top of a hillock for Hanumanji._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ www.facebook.comanjanadrihillanegundi

hook: This famous temple in South India is closed on the day of Dussehrah; and the slaying of Ravana is enacted and celebrated only in a neighboring temple. Which temple is this?
explanation: The lingam at the main Ramanathaswamy temple in Rameswaram was consecrated on the day after the slaying of Ravana and coronation of Vibhishana. Therefore it is believed not to have existed on the day of Dussehrah. Hence it remains closed on the day of Dussehrah. The slaying is celebrated in the neighboring Kothandaramaswamy temple._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ramanujam

hook: Bascule bridges are bridges that can be raised to allow traffic to move beneath it. There is only one bascular bridge in India. Where is it?
explanation: _x000D_ The bascular bridge in Rameswaram can be raised and lowered and allows ships to pass beneath it. This is a railway bridge; till recently, Rameswaram was connected only by this railway line. A new road bridge has been built now._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ _x000D_ Ramanujam

hook: Istripuram is a location in Sri Lanka connected with the Ramayana itihaasa. What is the event that it is associated with?
explanation: After Hanumanji landed in Sri Lanka and located Sita mata, Ravana moved her to a labyrinth of caves. The caves open out at strategic places that are associated with Ravana's life like his dairy farm, his airport, his place of meditation etc. The caves are located in Istripuram. Even though they are well known these are not frequented. There is no religious worship in the place. Source Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni. Ramayana in Lanka Picture Credit

hook: This temple is called Dakshin Kailasha and is at the same longitude as Mount Kailash. Where is this temple?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Trinconamalee in Sri Lanka is called Trikuta Parvat and hosts the Koneswarama temple. It is considered as the capital of Ravana. There are other temples in the coast which are now sunken and are believed to be very ancient. The temple has a recorded history from 250 BCE._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ramayana Tirtha

hook: A place in Uttarakhand does not worship Hanumanji. Why?
explanation: Dronagiri in Uttarakhand is the place where Hanuman ji is not worshipped. The local people are supposed to be upset with Hanumanji as he had desecrated the place by dislocating the mountain and carrying it to Lanka. Even today, there is a reddish discharge from the portion said to have been torn off by Hanumanji, which is a discharge of Sulphur and oil. Ramlila performances stop at the abduction of Sita, since after that the narration involves Hanumanji._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ramanujam

hook: Kelaniya is a Buddhist temple in Sri Lanka but also has an important connection to Ramayana. What it it?
explanation: By the time Ravana was killed, Lanka had been devastated. Vibhishana sought a blessing from Rama to build a new capital on the western coast and chose Kelaniya, near Colombo. A Buddhist temple is at the site of Vibhishana's palace and houses the only temple to Vibhishana in the world. There are deities of Vibhishana in temples near Rameswaram but those are not solely dedicated to him. The coronation of Vibishnana by Lakshmana is also represented in a sculpture in Kelaniya._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ Ramayana in Lanka_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ https //kelaniyatemple.lk/

hook: Shatrughan cremated Dasaratha. Which is the site of cremation of Dasaratha in Ramayana?
explanation: Vilvahari Teertha, called Billori, is the site of cremation of Dasaratha. It is believed that Bharata wanted him to be cremated where no other mortal had been cremated before. His body was preserved in oil for several days before such a place could be located. As Dasaratha had disowned Kaikeyi and Bharata after Rama's exile, Shatrughan cremated Dasaratha._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ Ayodhya UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ayodhya UP Tourism

hook: Makhaura Dham is an hour's drive from Ayodhya. It has a deep connection with Ramayana. What is its significance?
explanation: Makhaura Dham is in the Basti district of Uttar Pradesh. As advised by Vashishtha, Raja Dasaratha performed putreshti yajna here. There are two temples for Ram Janaki in Makhaura Dham._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Ayodhya UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ayodhya UP Tourism

hook: Which poem of Kalidasa is set in monsoons? It describes the journey of a monsoon cloud from Ramtek and Ujjan to the Himalayas. It carries a romantic message from an exiled lover.
explanation: In Meghdhootam a exiled Yaksha sends this message to his lover through the monsoon clouds. He describes to the cloud the sights it would see on its way from Ramtek and Ujjain till it reaches Alaka in Himalayas. Kalidasa describes the arrival of monsoon clouds over Ujjain on the first day of Ashadha 15 June. This is a surprisingly accurate estimate of the date on which the monsoon rains arrive over this part of India._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Monsoon and morning mist at NandaPrayag, Uttarakhand._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: The Monsoons, P.K.Das_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: Cheerapunjee is not the wettest place on earth any longer. It is Mawsynaram. In which State is Mawsynaram?
explanation: Mawsynaram is also in Meghalaya and is close to Cheerapunjee. Mawsynaram records were not as reliable as Cheerapunjee, but the available records suggest that the mean annual rainfall in Mawsynaram is 1141 cm. This is a little higher than Cheerapunjee's._x000D_ _x000D_ Both these places lie on the southern slopes of Khasi hills, at the end of a deep valley running from south to north. The monsoon winds blow from the south and are trapped within the valley and eventually strike these two places at the end of the valley. That is what causes immense rain in these two places._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: The Monsoons, P.K.Das_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: The Atlantic

hook: More than two thousand years ago, Indian mariners used the shifting monsoon wind direction to sail to Indonesia. It was called Bali Yatra. Where did the Yatra start from?
explanation: By the end of the second century BC, Indian mariners appear to have learned enough about the monsoon winds and ocean currents. They sailed through a southern route across the Indian Ocean to the islands of Indonesia, with a stop over in Sri Lanka, instead of sailing along the coast. Odisha’s Lake Chilika was an important starting point for this voyage. That era of maritime exploration and trade is still remembered in Odisha in folklore and festivals. The festival of Kartik Purnima takes place in mid-November when the winds shift and begin to blow from the north. This marks the time of year that ancient mariners would have set sail for Indonesia. Families, especially women and children, gather at the edge of a water-body and launch paper boats with oil-lamps in the water._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Monsoon on the Railway Tracks at Nilambur, Kerala._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Sanjeev Sanyal, The Ocean of Churn_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: What are Kal Baisakhis, Coffee Shower and Mango Shower?
explanation: There are large areas in India, such as Assam and Bengal, which experience violent thunderstorms in the pre-monsoon months of April and May. These are the well known Nor Westers or Kal Baisakhis, meaning a mass of dark clouds in the month of Baisakh. The rainfall is convective and can be as much as 5 cm in an hour but the rainfall is generally of short duration._x000D_ _x000D_ Pre monsoon showers in Kerala are known as Coffee Showers. In Karnataka they are called Mango Showers. These help ripen the crop._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Monsoon mist and clouds over Beas river at Mandi, Himachal Pradesh_x000D_ _x000D_ Source: The Monsoons, P.K.Das_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: The English astronomer Halley explained what causes monsoons. He is also famous for another discovery. What was it?
explanation: The English astronomer Edmond Halley noticed the similarity between the monsoon and the land or sea breeze. He ascribed the monsoon winds to the movement of the Sun from the equator to the tropic and the consequent heating of land; and to the differential heating between land and ocean. Just like the sea breeze and land breeze._x000D_ _x000D_ He also identified that repeated appearances of comets to the naked eye in earth are visits of the same comet. The comet was named after him as Halley's comet. Interestingly, the last visit of Halley's comet was in 1986, exactly three hundred years after he published his theory on the cause of monsoons. The comet would next be visible in 2061._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Monsoon is the lifeline of the otherwise snowclad and remote Lahaul Valley, Himachal_x000D_ _x000D_ Source: The Monsoons, P.K.Das_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: Vassa is the monsoon season in Buddhism. Why do Buddhists (and some Jains) forbid monks from traveling in monsoon season?
explanation: Wandering monks were common in India. However during the rainy season monks may end up crushing green herbs, vegetative life and little living creatures. The sacred canon of Theravada Buddhism, Vinaya Sukta, explicitly states that all bhikshus (Buddhist monks or devotees) are to enter Vassa, a three–month annual retreat. During this period (rainy season), the monks are to stay in one place. Vassa was practiced even among Jains and other Sramana sects like Ajavikas._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Jog Falls on a lean monsoon day, Karnataka_x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Dimri AP et al. 2022. Monsoon in history and present. Journal of Palaeosciences 71(1): 45–74._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: Varshamana is an ancient instrument described in Arthashastra. What is it used for?
explanation: Kautilya’s Arthasastra (50–125 CE) refers to Varshamana. It gives instructions for setting up a rain guage or Varshamana. "In (front of) the store-house a bowl (kunda) with its mouth as wide as an aratni (24 angulag) shall be set up as rain-gauge (varshamána)."_x000D_ _x000D_ It also provides a description of various types of rain clouds and a simplistic method for forecasting rains._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture. DudhSagar falls on the Karnataka - Goa border_x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Kautilya's Arthashastra, R. Shamasastry._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: The Thar desert plays an important role in Indian monsoons. How?
explanation: Himalayas keep the Indian land mass warm by blocking the cool air from the North. The Thar Desert is also a vital feature. The extreme heat in the Thar Desert generates a low pressure region as the hot air rises. This draws the monsoon winds towards the north west of India, advancing the monsoon._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Lectures of Professor Ojha, Allahabad University_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: A Rig Vedic hymn welcoming monsoons features an animal that aestivates - that buries itself in sand and wakes up with the rain. Which animal is featured?
explanation: Rig Veda has the famous Frog Hymn. It describes the onset of the rainy season with the croaking of frogs. In tropical countries, frogs bury themselves in sand during summer and await the rains. They emerge with the coming of rains and croak in chorus. The Frog Hymn describes the emergence of frogs and their behaviour at the advent of rains. The hymn also mentions the riches that frogs will deliver, an allusion to rains which will revive the earth and grant all riches._x000D_ _x000D_ Even today, Jyapus, the farmers of the Kathmandu valley, perform frog worship during the rainy season in order to secure their harvest. The Jyapu puts cooked rice and beans in the cavity of four clay containers resembling the figure of a frog. Then the Jyapu places the containers or the leaves in the four corners of his field. He stands in the middle of the field, looks upward and praises the frogs “O frogs, last year you have provided for us with plenty of rice and other grains. This year too give us plenty.” The concept of this simple ritual is almost exactly as that of the Ṛgvedic Frog Hymn._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Ganga on a clear monsoon day, upstream of Rishikesh_x000D_ _x000D_ Source: The Rig Veda, Bibek Debroy and Dipavali Debroy._x000D_ _x000D_ The Adaptation of Monsoonal Culture by Ṛgvedic Aryans: A Further Study of the Frog Hymn. Gautama V. Vajracharya_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: The discovery of Hippalus wind revolutionized sea trade. The wind offers a direct sea route to India. Which is the starting point for this sea route?
explanation: Hippalus was a Greek navigator who discovered a direct sea route from Red Sea to India, aided by the South West monsoon. This route would sail to India directly across the Indian ocean. The earlier practice was to ply along the coast which would take a longer time. The direct route also allowed the traders to return the same year when the wind reverses direction during the north wast monsoon. In his honour, the south west monsoon was named Hippalus wind. The Periplus of the Erythraen Sea, a first century navigational text, describes this legacy._x000D_ _x000D_ Though the reference to Hippalus wind is documented, there are doubts whether Hippalus was a historical figure. It is possible that this legacy is only a foundation myth._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: The Birth of the Monsoon Winds: On the Existence and Understanding of Hippalus, and the “Discovery” of the Apogeous Trade Winds. Scott Vincent Hatcher_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikmedia Commons

hook: El Nino phenomenon is connected to monsoons. It is also connected to Christmas. How?
explanation: El Nino in Spanish means "The Child." This event refers to unusual warming of waters off the coast of Peru. It reduces the yield of fish and is known to increase the probability of a weak monsoon in the succeeding year. This event normally occurs around Christmas. The phrase El Nino refers to the "The Child" or the birth of Jesus._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: The slopes of Matheran on a velvet green monsoon day, Maharashtra_x000D_ _x000D_ Source: The Monsoons, P.K.Das_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: Monsooned Malabar is a special coffee variety which has a GI tag. Why it is called so?
explanation: India's most unusual coffee is Monsooned Malabar. It is coffee that has been exposed for three or four months to the moisture laden winds of the monsoon. The process swells and yellows the bean, reduces acidity and imparts a heavy, deep, syrupy sweetness to the cup. They also acquire mustiness. Monsooned Malabar has acquired a Geographical Identification (GI) tag. Monsooning was originally devised by Indian exporters to produce a cup similiar to Old Brown Java coffees, which were similiarly transformed in taste by exposure to salt air and moisture in the hulls of wooden sailing ships on their voyage from Java to Europe. Picture: Kedarnath on a clear monsoon morning Source: Coffee, A guide to buying, brewing and enjoying. Kenneth Davids Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam

hook: If the Yatris are singing the popular bhajan "Mata Ne Bulaya Hai," or "Çhalo Bulawa Aaya Hai," where are they likely to be headed?
explanation: It is a belief that the Divine Mother herself calls her children to visit her and receive her blessings. Therefore the phrase "Bulawa Aaya Hai." Shri Mata Vaishno Devi Ji resides in a Holy Cave located in the folds of the three peaked mountain named Trikuta in the Jammu region. She is the collective embodiment of all three Devis, Saraswati, Lakshmi and Kali. Vaishnavi is in the form of a five and a half feet rock with three heads or pindis (petrified stone) representing the three Devis. A geological study of the Holy Cave has indicated its age to be nearly a million years. This shrine, like with many others, is associated with the itihasa purana tradition. The Pandavas of the Mahabharata are said to have invoked the Mother for victory. Overlooking the cave are five stone structures associated with the five brothers. Perhaps the oldest reference of a visit by a historical figure to the Holy Cave is that of Guru Gobind Singh. The 14 km Yatra has been a part of several film songs and Bhajans. Gulshan Kumar, the founder of T Series, popularised Vaishno Devi with his philanthropy and collection of songs. Source: Sacred Landscape and Pilgrimage to Vaishno Devi, Abha Chauhan www.maavaishnodevi.org Picture Credit: Travel Triangle

hook: This Yatra is the most protected Yatra in India due to terrorist threats. Which is it?
explanation: The Amarnath Yatra has been repeatedly attacked by terrorists and is now guarded by over 60,000 security men with a variety of technology._x000D_ _x000D_ Amarnath is the Immortal Lord. Anantnag in Kashmir is famous for the Amarnathji cave with the ice Lingam representing Shiva. It is at a height of 13,000 feet. Two smaller ice stalagmites represent Parvati & Ganesha. Hindus believe that the lingam waxes & wanes with the moon. The traditional yatra reaches the cave every year on the Raksha Bandan Purnima day._x000D_ _x000D_ Legend has it that Shiva recounted to Parvati the secret of creation in the cave. Kalhana's Rajtarangini, a 12th century work, mentions Amarnath as a tirtha known since ancient times. Old chronicles call it Amaresbara and many Kashmiri kings including the 15th century Sultan Zain-ul-Abedin visited the cave. The origin of the pilgrimage is described in a Sanskrit book “Bringesha Samhita.” A rishi instructed his disciples to visit the cave and Shiva gifted the pilgrims a scepter for protection, that is even today known as ‘Chari (stick in Hindi) Mubarak’. A Muslim shepherd Buta Malik is also associated in some tales with the rediscovery of cave and till date some percent of donations are given to his family._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Shri Amarnath Kashmir Yatra, Madan Mohan Sharma._x000D_ _x000D_ https://anantnag.nic.in/tourist-place/amarnath/_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Economic Times

hook: The famous anthropologist Irawati Karve defined the region of Maharashtra through a pilgrimage. She said, “Maharashtra: the land whose people go to __________ for pilgrimage." Which pilgrimage was she referring to?
explanation: Every Ashadh, devotees across Maharashtra take a pilgrimage on foot to the Vithoba temple in Pandharpur. The 800-year-old tradition is called the Wari and the devotees-the Warkaris (literally 'those who do the wari'), a community in the bhakti tradition. The Wari is a distinctive part of Maharashtrian culture. Men in Gandhi caps, women with tulsi pots on their heads, saffron flags, musical instruments, bhajans, kirtans and tastefully decorated vehicles are characteristic of the Wari today._x000D_ _x000D_ The tradition is traced to Vitthala Pant. He was the father of Sant Dyaneshwar, who wrote Dyaneshwari in the 13th century - the oldest known Marathi literature that led to the flowering of Maharashtra’s renowned Sant Parampara (saint tradition). Many other saints in the 14th to 16th centuries have been known to do the wari - Namdev, Bhanudas, Eknath. In the 17th century, Sant Tukaram performed the wari with the padukas (sandals) of Sant Dyaneshwar around his neck._x000D_ _x000D_ The present system of carrying the padukhas on palkis (palanquins) was first introduced by Haibatravbaba, a courtier of the Gwalior based Scindias, in the 19th century. Under his supervision, the Wari began to resemble a military procession with elephants & horses and groupings of thousands of devotees called the Dindis._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: On the Road, A Maharashtrian Pilgrimage. Irawati Karve._x000D_ _x000D_ https://swarajyamag.com/culture/the-pandharpur-wari-the-journey-of-the-devout_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: The wonderful photographer Salonee Jain, Instagram @thesaloneejain

hook: Which word in the English language is inspired by the annual Jagannath rath yatra at Puri, Odisha?
explanation: The Ratha Yatra of Puri is the Yatra of the deities themselves. It is celebrated in June-July. During the festival, the three deities (Jagannath, his brother Balabhadra and sister Subhadra) are pulled in three massive, wooden chariots to Gundicha Temple whereby they reside there for a week and then return to the Jagnannath temple. The grandeur of the festival impressed the British so much that they coined the term juggernaut, an anglicized form of Jagannath. It refers to an overwhelmingly powerful and unstoppable force. Rev Claudius Buchanan was the first to popularize the word in the early 1800s, though as a missionary he described the yatra as a violent and bloody cult._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Word Origins and their Romantic Stories. Wilfred Funk._x000D_ _x000D_ blog.oup.com_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Srijat Mishra, Rath Yatra 2023

hook: This lakeside Guruwara is one of the holiest spots of Sikhism and is accessible only through a trekking trail. Where is this?
explanation: Hemkund is a special Gurudwara. This is the place where Guru Gobind Singh is supposed to have meditated in his earlier life. In his own words he says "I was busy performing penance on the hills of Hemkunt where seven peaks are prominent. The place is called Sapt Shring where King Pandu had performed Yoga." The location was identified only in 1930s. Hemkund is in the Garhwal Himalayas and adjoins the Valley of Flowers which is a very popular hiking and nature destination. Hemkund is accessible only by a steep hiking trail. The roadhead from Haridwar to Badrinath branches off towards the Hemkund hiking trail after Joshimath. Source: Sikh Shrines in India, G.S.Randhir Picture Credit: Hemkund Sahib Gurudwara, Facebook

hook: This Yatra requies an austere and arduous preparation for 41 days. Which is it?
explanation: Thousands of black bearded, black clad men are seen everywhere in Kerala in the Yatra months. Their foreheads are smeared with Vibhuti, Kumkum and sandal paste. They are chanting “Swamiye, Saranam Ayyappa,” meaning Lord Ayyappa, I surrender to you. They are headed to Sabarimala in Kerala, situated on a hilltop. The pilgrim trail is difficult, passing through forest areas. Before starting the Yatra, the pilgrims are expected to follow an austere vratham (cleansing period) of 41 days. The vratham requires wearing only black clothes, being barefooted, bathing twice a day, abstaining from meat, tobacco and alcohol. The vratham requires austerity in language and demeanor. Pilgrims are also required to maintain celibacy. The temple doors of Sabarimala are open to everyone, irrespective of caste, creed, religion or social status. The deity being a celibate, only girls below 10 years who have not attained maturity and women who have reached menopause, can perform the Yatra. This restriction is now being litigated in the Supreme Court. The 2022 Malayalam movie Mallikapuram captures the joy of an 8-year-old girl on the Sabarimala Yatra. Source: Pilgrimage to Sabari, K.R. Vaidyanathan Picture Credit: ABP Live

hook: The original Char Dham yatra covers the four directions of the country. Who is said to meditate at Badrinath (Uttarakhand), take bath at Rameshwaram (TN), dine at Puri (Odisha), and retire at Dwarka (Gujarat)?
explanation: The popular Char Dham Yatra is actually a chota (small) char dham yatra featuring the four Uttrakhand temples. The Original Char Dhams mark four extremes of India, Badrinath, Puri, Dwarka and Rameswaram. This yatra is very sacred and said to provide moksha (salvation). Yatris aim to do it at least once in their lifetime. Each of Dhams came into prominence in a particular Yuga. Badrinath came into prominence when Nar-Narayana, a Vishnu incarnate, did penance in a plum (badri) forest during Satya yuga. Rameshwaram came into prominence in the Treta-Yuga when Lord Rama bathed and worshipped Shiva. Dwarka Dham got its prominence in the Dwapara Yuga when Lord Krishna made Dwarka his residence instead of Mathura. Jagannath Puri Dham is famous for its chappan bhog (56 course) prasad (offering) and it is the dham for Kaliyuga (age of enjoyment). Thus, the char dhams are associated both with the four directions and the four ages of time. Source: Immotral India, J.J Dave. Anuradha Goyal’s You Tube lecture on Tirthas Picture Credit: Swarajya

hook: The Navanu yatra involves pilgrims ascending a hill 99 times. Which community takes part in this yatra?
explanation: One of the major Jain tirtha-yatras (pilgrimages) in Gujarat is the Navanu Yatra (99 yatras), undertaken from Palitana in Bhavnagar district. From Palitana, yatris ascend Shatrunjaya Hill (altitude 580 m), considered to be tirthadhiraja (the king of all yatras), whose summit has hundreds of temples built over several centuries. The objective of the Navanu Yatra is to ascend Shatrunjaya 99 times, hence the name navanu. This is a re-enactment of the number of times Adinatha (the first Jain tirthankara) is believed to have ascended the hill. He is believed to have given his first summon under a banyan tree on the summit of the hill. Because of this association, the cynosure of Navanu Yatra is the Adishvara temple (popularly called Dadanu Derashar), where the yatra culminates. Jains believe that 23 out of 24 tirthankaras attained moksha on Shatrunjaya Hill. Source: https://www.sahapedia.org/journey-moksha-navanu-yatra-shatrunjaya-hill Picture Credit: Ankita Jain

hook: Kashi (Benaras/ Varanasi) is famous for its ‘Panchkroshi’ pilgrimage. Panch signifies five, what does kroshi refer to?
explanation: Kashi Kshetra is delimited by an outer circle, known as Brhat Panckrosi Yatra. The area within it is believed to be the most sacred. The Yatra is believed to cover 108 sacred places (144 in some accounts). The radius of the circle is five kroshas – approx. 10 miles. The Panchkroshi pilgrimage is a five-day pilgrimage to cover this radius of five kroshas with five stops. The first historical reference to these areas was made in 7th Century Padma Purana. Source: Panchakroshi Yatra of Banares. Rana P.B. Singh. India: A Sacred Geography. Diana Eck. Picture Credit: Rana P.B. Singh

hook: This is the longest Yatra in India. This river is circumambulated from source to sea and back. Which river is this?
explanation: The sacred Narmada River, the lifeline of Central India, is worshipped as Narmada maiyya (mother) or Ma Rewa (derived from “rev” meaning leaping one). Being the longest west-flowing river, the Narmada parikrama is a formidable spiritual exercise and challenge—an incredible journey of about 2,600 km. The journey usually starts at the river’s source at Amarkantak in the Maikal Hills in Madhya Pradesh, goes along its southern banks, all the way to its mouth at Bharuch in Gujarat. The pilgrims then cross over to the northern bank and trace the trail back to the source of Narmada. Pilgrims can, however, start from the mouth of the river, or anywhere in-between, but they must complete the circuit and return where they began. Source: Immotral India, J.J Dave. https://www.outlookindia.com/traveller/mp/inspire-me/culture/narmada-parikrama-great-circumambulation/ Picture Credit: Backpackers and Travellers India, Facebook

hook: The annual Kanwar yatra is a big pilgrimage for Shiva devotees in North India. Thousands of men carry Ganga Jal to their hometown. What does ‘kanwar’ mean?
explanation: ‘Kanwar’ is a single pole made of bamboo with two containers filled with holy water dangling from the opposite ends._x000D_ _x000D_ Kanwar Yatra is an annual pilgrimage of devotees of Lord Shiva, also known as ‘Kavad.’ They carry the waters of Ganga from Sultanganj in Bihar to Devghar Jyotirling in Jharkhand. They walk or run barefooted for the 105 kms of this Yatra. Some even prostrate the entire distance. Nowadays the Yatra also takes place from Haridwar, Gaumukh, Gangotri in Uttarakhand back to the Shiv temple in their respective hometowns. Many complete the Yatra in motorcycles too._x000D_ _x000D_ The journey is usually undertaken in the monsoon month of Shravana (between July and August). The kanwarias generally wear distinct saffron-coloured robes and chant ‘Bol Bam’ to glorify Shiva._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Walking With Pilgrims. Ruma Bose._x000D_ _x000D_ https://indianexpress.com/article/lifestyle/life-style/route-religious-significance-kanwar-yatra-7997871/_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: The Quint

hook: Sankranti is also a day for bathing in sacred waters. This photograph captures a pilgrimage to a sacred waterbody for a Sankaranti bath. Which pilgrimage is this?
explanation: Gangasagar Mela is held at an island in the delta of River Ganga in Bengal every Makar Sankranti. It is said to be the second largest pilgrimage gathering after the Kumbh Mela. It is associated with the story of Kapila muni who systematized Samkhya philosophy. In Puranic stories King Sagara’s sons falsely accused Kapila muni of stealing their sacrificial horses. Kapila muni in anger at the false accusation burnt them to ashes. Many years later, Sagara’s descendant, King Bhagirath came to Kapila muni and requested him to release his ancestors from hell. On Kapila muni’s suggestion, Bhagirath requested Ganga to come down to earth and the touch of her waters released Bhagirath’s ancestors from hell. It is believed that bathing in the Ganga at this spot on Makar Sankranti leads to salvation. Source: River of Moksha, Pilgrimages along the Ganga. Swami Kailashanand Giri https://www.gangasagar.in/en/about/mythologicalsignificance Picture Credit: Avishek Das Flickr

hook: Sati immolated herself at Kankhal, leading to the creation of the Shakti Peetha tradition. Where is Kankhal?
explanation: According to the Puranas, Sati immolated herself during a Yagna when her husband, Shiva, was insulted by her father. This act enraged Shiva, and in his grief and anger, he carried her body across the country in a destructive dance. To calm Shiva, Vishnu was requested to intervene. Vishnu gradually dismembered Sati's body, and these different parts fell at various places, which became known as Shakti Peethas. Kankhal is near the famous pilgrimage center of Haridwar._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: Which Shakti Peetha is regarded as the greatest and is one of the 4 Adi Peethas according to various texts?
explanation: Kamakhya in Assam is considered one of the greatest Shakti Peethas and is mentioned as one of the four Adi Shakti Peethas representing the cardinal directions. The Buddhist text Hevajra Tantra and Kalika Purana both attest to its significance. Kamakhya defines the land of Kamarupa, where the Goddess is believed to reside in every household._x000D_ _x000D_ The temple has a unique tradition of closing for three days during the monsoons, symbolizing the menstrual period of the Goddess. Kamakhya is also linked to the concept of birth. Situated on the Nilanchal hill overlooking the mighty Brahmaputra River, the area around Kamakhya temple is undergoing development, much like the Kashi Vishwanath corridor._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: DC Sircar, The Shakta Pithas_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: Shakti, known as Mahisharuramardini, took residence in this ancient Peetha after defeating Mahisha. Where is this Peetha located?
explanation: Situated near Mirzapur in Uttar Pradesh, Vindhyachala overlooks the holy Ganga river. Shakti, also known as Mahisharuramardini, settled here after slaying Mahisha._x000D_ _x000D_ Vindhyachala is home to three main temples dedicated to different manifestations of Devi: Vindyavasini, Kalo Khoh, and Ashtabhuja. Pilgrims often embark on the Trikon Parikrama, a sacred journey, visiting these three Devi temples._x000D_ _x000D_ The Devi Mahatmya, an ancient text, references the Great Goddess who vanquished Mahisha and reveals her various appearances, including her eventual manifestation as the Goddess of Vindhya._x000D_ _x000D_ The region around the Vindhyachala temple has a rich history of Devi worship. Baghor, located approximately 100 kilometers away, bears evidence of Devi worship dating back 11,000 years. You can read about Baghor in IndiYatra’s Navaratri 2022 quiz. https://indiyatra.in/devi-a-quiz/ _x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Inditales.com_x000D_ Vindyachalmandir.com_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Government of UP

hook: This south Indian city derives its name from Mahisha, whom Devi vanquished. What is the name of this city?
explanation: The city named after Mahisha, the demon whom Devi defeated, is Mysore. The name "Mysuru" is a variation of "mysooru," which is derived from "mahishur" or "Mahishasurana Ooru" in Kannada, meaning the town of Mahishasura. According to the Puranic story found in the Devi Bhagavatha, Mysore was under the rule of the demon King Mahishasura, depicted as a buffalo-headed monster. Responding to the pleas of the devas to save them from this demon, Devi incarnated as Chamundeshwari and ultimately defeated Mahishasura atop the Chamundi Hill near Mysore. This hill and the city itself were named after Chamundi Hill and Mysore, respectively. The Chamundeshwari temple on the hill is an ancient Peetha and holds great reverence, especially among the Mysore royals. The picture is that of Mahisha in front of Chamundi Temple at Mysore. Source: Lewis Rice, Mysore mysore.nic.in. Picture Credit: Deepti Deshpande. Wikimedia Commons.

hook: Which Shakti Peetha in Maharashtra is revered as the family deity of Chatrapati Shivaji?
explanation: Tuljapur Bhavani is the family deity of Chatrapati Shivaji. It faced adversities from his enemies as a result. Afzal Khan encamped in Tuljapur, broke the deity into pieces and reduced it to powder. Legend has it that Afzal Khan received a curse for these actions and was subsequently killed by Shivaji. Shivaji is said to have undertaken the task of rebuilding the temple. The temple has four gates, with two of them named after Shivaji's parents and one dedicated to Shivaji himself._x000D_ _x000D_ Tuljapur is mentioned as one of the four Adipithas, with the name Puranagiri. The historical records of the temple date back to as early as 1204 CE. The tradition of Tuljapur Bhavani as the Kulaswamini was continued by the Bhonsales and Peshwas._x000D_ _x000D_ The Tuljapur deity is believed to go into nidra (rest) for eighteen days each year, divided into three periods. Darshan continues but rituals are temporarily suspended. Additionally, it is believed that Parvati once appeared in front of Rama near Tuljapur in a playful manner._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Bhavans. Temples and Legends of Maharashtra_x000D_ Swarajya_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Kalidasa, whose name means "Servant of Kali," found inspiration from which Shakti Peetha?
explanation: Kalidasa drew inspiration from the Shakti Peetha known as Sri Gadhakalika Devi in Ujjain. This particular Peetha has historical associations with great rulers from the past, such as Emperor Harshavardhan._x000D_ _x000D_ Devi is venerated at this Peetha in the form of a married lady. Notably, a distinctive ritual at this temple involves devotees drawing a reverse Swastika when making their wishes. Once their wishes are fulfilled, they return to the temple to draw the correct Swastika as an act of devotion and gratitude._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Ujjain. K.B. Dongray_x000D_ dhar.nic.in_x000D_ sanatan.org._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Vikas Singh, TripAdvisor

hook: Which Shakti Peetha is southernmost and is believed to be the protector of Bharatvarsha?
explanation: Kanyakumari stands as the southernmost Shakti Peetha. While there is a Maha Shakti Peetha in Trinconamalee, Sri Lanka, it is situated to the north of Kanyakumari. Attukkal is known for hosting the largest gathering of women devotees but is not a recognized Shakti Peetha. Kanyakumari holds special significance because the "Virgin Goddess" is believed to protect the entirety of Bharatvarsha (India). According to legend, the demon Bana could only be vanquished by a virgin. Responding to the plea of the Devas, Devi incarnated as a virgin girl to slay Bana. She became betrothed to Shiva, who resided in the vicinity at Suchindram. In an attempt to prevent Devi from leaving Kanyakumari, Narada, acting on behalf of the Devas, deceived Shiva by convincing him that the time for their marriage had passed. As a result, Shiva turned back. Devi, disheartened but resolute, cast the wedding feast into the sea but remained in Kanyakumari. She successfully defeated Bana and continued to safeguard the land. It is believed that the feast she discarded into the sea caused the confluence of the Bay of Bengal, Arabian Sea, and the Indian Ocean to display three distinct colors. Source: Diana Eck, India : A Sacred Geography Picture Credit: Anantharaman. K. Google Photos

hook: Hinglaj Devi is the westernmost Shakti Peetha, located in Balochistan. Who is believed to have visited this site to atone for a sin?
explanation: Hinglaj Devi in Balochistan stands as the westernmost Shaktipeetha. The Hinglaj Mata Yatra has a documented history dating back to at least the 14th century. To this day, an estimated 250,000 people participate in the Hinglaj Mata Yatra, serving as a unifying event for the small Hindu community in Pakistan. The shrine itself is a natural cave with a vermilion-smeared stone representing Devi. Yatris (pilgrims) also ascend the nearby Chandragup and Khandewari mud volcanoes, which are considered sacred. Rama embarked on a Yatra to Hinglaj, as it was believed that only she could absolve his sin of killing Ravana, who was a Brahmin. Accompanied by his entire army, Rama's progress was halted at a mountain pass. Devi instructed that he must approach not as a triumphant king but as a humble worshipper. Following this guidance, Rama proceeded with only Sita, Lakshmana, Hanuman, and Ganesha to worship Devi and seek forgiveness for his sins. Source: National Geographic Diana Eck, India : A Sacred Geography Picture Credit: National Geographic

hook: The Char Dhams mark the geographical edges of modern-day India. Which of the four Dhams also has a Shakti Peeth?
explanation: At the renowned Jagannath temple in Puri, Odisha, the ancient temple for Devi Vimala is recognized as a Shakti Peeth. Historians suggest that Her peetha existed long before the site became a center of Krishna worship. Despite the massive presence of the Jagannath temple today, Devi Vimala's ancient status is still acknowledged. As a tribute to her, Prasad is offered to Devi Vimala before it is distributed to devotees as Mahaprasad. The temple also features the unique practice of worshipping Durga and Madhav together during the two Navaratris. Notably, it is believed that a goat sacrifice is also performed for Vimala, which is an uncommon practice in a Vaishnavite temple. Source: Orissa Review

hook: This Shakti Peeth is among the 18 Maha Shakti Peethas, is the northernmost, and was once a great university. Which is it?
explanation: The Sharada Pith is mentioned in tantric texts, Kalhana's history of Kashmir, and in Alberuni's account. All of these references date back to the 10th-11th century CE. This Shakti Peeth, now located in Pakistan-occupied Kashmir (POK), was not only a sacred site but also home to a university that accommodated 5,000 scholars._x000D_ _x000D_ Between the 6th and 12th centuries CE, it was among India's most prominent temple universities and was renowned for its vast library. Scholars from distant places would travel to access its invaluable texts. The Sharada script, which was popularized by the university, eventually became synonymous with the Sharada Pitha._x000D_ _x000D_ The region of Kashmir, where Sharada Pitha is situated, was historically referred to as "Sharada Desh."_x000D_ _x000D_ Source: DC Sircar, The Shakta Pithas_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Umar Jamshaid. Wikimedia Commons

hook: This is possibly the easternmost Shakti Peeth. Where is it located?
explanation: The Nartiang temple is believed to have been constructed in the 16th century by a Jaintia king when he converted to Hinduism. To this day, Durga Puja is celebrated at Nartiang following Bengali rituals. It is said that human sacrifices took place at this temple in ancient times. The temple priests in Nartiang were brought from Maharashtra in the late 14th century. They were recruited because local Brahmin priests refused to perform animal or human sacrifices. Remarkably, even today, the 29th generation of these priests, known as Deshmukhs, continues to oversee the temple's rituals. The residents of Nartiang carried water from the local rivers, Myntdu and Myntang, as well as local soil for the Ayodhya Bhumi Pooja. Their act is a reminder that the cultural heart and geographical boundaries of Bharat are united; and Shakti Peethas are the markers of that unity. Source: Pyngrope, Bathsheba Goswami, PhD Dissertation Swarajya Magazine Picture Credit: IndiaDivine.Org

hook: This Shakti Peeth is not only sacred but also a beloved tourist destination. Which is it?
explanation: The eyes of Devi fell at Nainital, specifically at a location now known as Smuggler’s Rock. Devi Narayani is believed to have emerged from the area and resided in a cave before descending into the lake. Nainital derives its name from the "eye" of Shakti, referring to her divine gaze, and the lake itself. When the British discovered Nainital in 1841, they realized the lake's sacred significance to the hill people. The locals vehemently denied the existence of the lake and took measures to prevent its pollution by Europeans. Source: J. M. Clay, Nainital Naintal Gazetteer Picture Credit: Uttarakhand Tourism

hook: Devi worship in India has deep antiquity. Which great Buddhist emperor of India claimed that his kingship came from Nana (a form of Devi) and all gods?
explanation: _x000D_ Kanishka ruled over North West India upto Mathura, Afghanistan & parts of Central Asia in 1st/2nd CE. In his Rabatak inscription in Afghanistan, he invokes Nana and mentions that the kingship is derived from Nana. Nana is a bactrian female divinity and is shown, like Durga, seated on a lion. The Rig Veda uses Nana as one of the names of the Goddess. The ancient Sogdians from central Asia called the Mother Goddess Nana Devi Amba. Amba is a commonly used name for the Devi even today. Ambala, the town in Haryana takes its name from Devi Amba. _x000D_ Source: When Devi Comes home. Bibek Debroy and Anuradha Goyal. _x000D_ Picture Credit: WikiCommons _x000D_

hook: "Sapta Matrikas" refers to worship of seven Devis, often in a single slab. This archeological find represents one of the earliest possible evidence for this form of worship. Which period is it traced to?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ A seal from Mohenjodaro shows in its top right corner a schematised figure with a long pigtail and horned head-dress. It stands in between the trunks of a tree and is fronted to the left by a kneeling supplicant who is followed by an animal, possibly a unicorn. The lower panel shows a row of seven standing figures identical to the figure standing inside the trunks of the tree. Some kind of personification of a tree deity can certainly be envisaged in this case. Furthermore, the row of seven deities suggests the concept of Saptamatrikas or "seven mothers" known to Hinduism, and thus the tree deity itself is likely to be a female deity. Several mother Goddess images have also been unearthed in Harappan sites._x000D_ _x000D_ Saptamatrikas are common across India. In Kerala temples, Saptamatrikas are also depicted as a set of small discs on a stone platform. The second picture is an example from Sri Chandramouleeshwara temple in Udupi, just opposite the famous Udupi Sri Krishna temple. Each of the circular discus in the bottom row represent one of the mothers._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Archaeology and World Religion-Routledge (2001) DK Chakrabarti_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Harappa.com_x000D_ _x000D_ S.R. Ramanujam

hook: In Baghor in Madhya Pradesh a unique form of Devi worship has been discovered from archeological remains. The practice continues in almost the same form even at present in the nearby regions. This marks a really long record of worship. How far back is the practice dated?
explanation: _x000D_ Baghor is within 100 kms of Ganga and very close to Prayagraj. In 1982, a team of archaeologists found a heap of discarded stones on a circular rubble platform. In the middle of the rubble was a bright stone with concentric triangles, but broken. They found most of the broken pieces near the rubble itself. When the workers saw the unusual stone they dropped the tools and stopped digging. "This is our Devi!" they said. The excavating team did not take them seriously. But the local people too immediately recognised the rubble and the stone as their Devi. One was upset that the digging team had broken the stone. When they told him that they had actually dug this out and it is ancient, he immediately fell on the ground and did a pranam to the stone. _x000D_ The site watchman took the team to his house. He had built a small shrine for Devi near his house which looked exactly like the one they had discovered. A sandstone rubble; a bright stone with concentric triangular pattern in the middle. Then the team also saw a temple called Kerai ki Devi which had a similar form of worship. As they looked around, they saw more and more of similar forms of worship in the area. _x000D_ The team then recognised the significance of what they had found. They had known that the Baghor site is very ancient. It belonged to a period between two other layers which had been dated. This gave a time period of 9000 BC to 8000 BC to the Devi shrine. A practice of worship that was prevalent 11,000 years ago and which continues till date. _x000D_ The picture that you see here, with the answer, is the present day shrine, which looks remarkably similar to what was unearthed. _x000D_ Source: Archaeology and World Religion-Routledge (2001) DK Chakrabarti. _x000D_ Interview with Prof Jagannath Pal, Ex Allahabad University _x000D_ Antiquity. An Upper Paleolithic Shrine in India? J.M. Kenoyer, J.D. Clark, J.N.Pal, G.R. Sharma _x000D_ Picture Credit: J.M. Kenoyer _x000D_

hook: Devi Sukta is the earliest literary reference to Devi as the supreme master of the universe. Where is this from?
explanation: The Devi Sukta is the first literary evidence of Devi worship and is in the tenth mandala of Rig Veda. This is one of the rare hymns of Rig Veda that are in first person (Indra is the speaker in other first person hymns)._x000D_ _x000D_ Devi Sukta concludes by saying_x000D_ _x000D_ " I myself blow forth like the wind_x000D_ Grasping all living beings_x000D_ Beyond heaven, beyond this earth_x000D_ Such I have become through my greatness"_x000D_ _x000D_ It is generally believed that the Vedas had a male centric pantheon of Gods like Indra or Varuna with Goddesses shown mostly as consorts like Usha or Prithvi. Devi worship is also widely said to be non Vedic in origin. Yet the Rig Veda characterizes the Goddess as the "sovereign" divinity._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Devi Sukta. Kshitish Chandra Chatterjee._x000D_ _x000D_ When Devi Comes home. Bibek Debroy and Anuradha Goyal._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Ms Sarah Welch. WikiCommons.

hook: The glory of Devi is described in Sri Durga Saptha Sathi. This is known as Sree Devi Mahatmyam in South India and as Sree Chandi or Chandi Path in eastern India, especially Bengal. These are a part of a book from a larger corpus. Which text is this?
explanation: The nine days of worship of Devi in her three aspects of Kali, Lakshmi and Saraswati has its base in Markandeya Purana. It consists of 700 mantras. It talks about Devi overwhelming asuras like Madhu-Kaitabha, Mahishasura and Shumba-Nishumbha. It was narrated by Rishi Sumedhas to King Suradha and trader Samadhi, who had lost their kingdom and wealth respectively, and who had sought remedy for their sorrows. _x000D_ This is one of the most important books for Devi devotees. The very presence of this book in the house is considered to have the power to ward off all evil spirits. In ancient days the faithful ones carried this book with them during long journeys to ward off all dangers. _x000D_ Source: Glory of the Mother. Chinmaya Mission. _x000D_ Picture Credit: Gita Press _x000D_

hook: Circular Yogini temples are well known in eastern India.They are open to the sky and Yoginis are worshipped in groups. What is the most common number of Yoginis?
explanation: The most common number is 64. The Chausat Yogini temples, considered to be associated with tantric sects, are found in eastern India. The most well known are in Bhedaghat (near Jabalpur), Hirapur (near Bhubaneswar) and Mitaoli (near Gwalior). The circular Mitaoli temple is popularly considered as an inspiration for the Parliament building, though there is no firm evidence for it. The simplest and common usage of the term Yogini is as the female counterpart of Yogi. She is a woman who follows the path of yoga bodily and spiritual discipline. They are also often interpreted as sorceress who are feared. The picture is of Mitaoli temple near Morena and Gwalior in Madhya Pradesh Source: The Yogini Temples of India. Vidya Dehejia Picture Credit: S R Ramanujam

hook: Raj Jat is a three week 290 km long pilgrimage in worship of Devi. She is supposed to move to her in laws home in the hills during this journey. Which State of India is it in?
explanation: Raj Jat is the "Royal Procession" of the goddess Nanda Devi through the mountains of Uttarakhand. It is over a duration of 19 days and is conducted every 12 years (though not always). Goddess Nanda Devi leaves her home at Nauti near Karnaprayag and travels through Roopkund to her in law's home at Hom Kund at a height of 5200 mts. A sheep leads the procession and rests beside the Goddess's palanquin during the intervening campsites. _x000D_ Interestingly, Mahalaya, which marks the commencement of Durga Puja, is interpreted as the great journey in Bengal. It is the journey of the Devi from Himalayas to Her father's house. In Bengal and other eastern parts of India, Durga Puja is celebrated as the homecoming of Maa Durga and Her children to Her maternal house, "maika." _x000D_ Source: Nanda Raj Jat: Mahakumbha of Uttaranchal. Rakesh K Maikhuri et al. _x000D_ Picture Credit: vaibs_14oct flickr.com _x000D_

hook: What is Navapatrika form of Devi worship? In this picture you can see the Navapatrika being carried.
explanation: The navapatrika ceremony of Durga Puja personifies the vegetation spirit of Devi. Nine leaves of various plants are bound together and worshipped as Devi. The nine plants are plantain tree, colocasia, turmeric, barley, wood apple, pomegranate, asoka, mana and paddy. Each plant represents a Devi. These nine forms of Devi can be described as Navadurgas. _x000D_ Source: Jitendra Nath Banerjee. The Evolution of Hindu Iconography _x000D_ Picture Credit: India Scanner _x000D_

hook: Shitala is one of the forms of Devi. What does she represent and why do people worship her?
explanation: Shitala devi is invoked to ward off diseases. She is particularly invoked to seek a cure from small pox even as her anger can bring it on. Sitala Devi is revered in Bengal and north India in general and small pox is referred to as her grace. Devi is also worshipepd as Mariamma in Tamil Nadu. Scholars opine that worshipping Sitala and to pay attention to what she represents provides oneself with a more realistic and less fragile view of life. This makes the inevitable outburst of diseases or tragedy less devastating. The picture is that of Shitala Devi in Mahim, Mumbai. Though it is only a niche within the much older and larger Radha Rukmini complex, the temple is known as Shitala Devi temple. Source: Animals and Hinduism - Nivedita Krishna Hindu Goddesses. David Kinsley. Picture Credit: S R Ramanujam

hook: Chaitra Navaratri is observed in spring (March/ April) and is as important as the Sharada Navratri celebrated in Sep/Oct. The Chaitra Navratri also coincides with another major festival day. Which day is this?
explanation: It is also known as Rama Navratri because the ninth day of this festival is also celebrated as Rama Navami, the day Shri Rama was born in Ayodhya in Treta Yuga._x000D_ _x000D_ In some parts like Himachal Pradesh and Rajasthan Vasanta Navratri is more popular than Sharada Navratri. The beginning of the Chaitra Navratri is also the new year day, Ugadi in Andhra and Karnataka, as Gudi Padwa in Maharashtra and Navreh in Kashmir._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is a celebration of Chaitra Navaratri in Mata Longa Wali Devi mandir in Amritsar, Punjab_x000D_ _x000D_ Source: When Devi Comes home. Bibek Debroy and Anuradha Goyal._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Outlook

hook: Navratri is divided into three parts each celebrating a form of Devi. Which form does the middle part celebrate?
explanation: Each of the three days symbolically represent the liberation of humans. The first three days are dedicated to Durga or Kali; the last three days are dedicated to Saraswati and the middle three days are dedicated to Lakshmi (Raja Rajeshwari). Symbolically, in the first three days the devilish tendencies within us have been won over with the help of Durga. With the ensuing peace of mind, in the middle three days, with the help of Lakshmi, the organisation of order and security and divine wealth is achieved. Divine wealth is not material wealth that we associate with Lakshmi normally. It refers to Love, Charity, Kindness, Devotion, Peace, Trnquility, Honesty, Courage, Faith and desire for liberation. In the last three days, with the help of Saraswati, Sat-Chit-Ananda, the last stage in evolution is achieved. This represents absolute knowledge, absolute existence and absolute bliss. Source: Glory of the Mother: Chinmaya Mission Picture Credit: Isha Foundation

hook: In Bengal, Navratri is considered Akalabodhan (untimely invocation). This is because Devi was woken up for worship when she was asleep. Brahma woke her up so that Devi can help someone win a key battle. For whom was she woken up?
explanation: _x000D_ Navratri falls in the months of Devi's sleep and She was woken up for untimely worship. Kalika Purana, a minor Purana, notes that Brahma woke the Devi by using the branches of a bilva tree to show favour to Rama and to ensure destruction of Ravana. _x000D_ Akala Bodhan is related to Bengali tradition of Shardiya Durga puja. This story was retold in the Bengali retelling of Ramayana, the Krittivasi Ramayana by Krittibas Ojha composed in 15th century CE. Thus it became widespread in Bengal and surrounding areas. This shows how different Hindu traditions (the Ramayana & Shakti traditions in this case) intersect and are integrated in a historical process of assimilation. _x000D_ Source: When Devi Comes home. Bibek Debroy and Anuradha Goyal. _x000D_ Picture Credit: Statesman _x000D_

hook: For last almost 90 years, the same voice renders Chandipaath on All India Radio Kolkata to mark the start of Navaratri. Whose voice is this?
explanation: Birendra Krishna Bhadra (1905-91) used to work for All India Radio. In 1931, AIR (Calcutta) broadcast a radio programme known as "Mahisasura Mardini" at 4am on day of Mahalaya. This had a combination of songs, vocal renditions of the story and chants of Sanskrit shlokas. The shlokas were primarily from the "Devi Mahtmaya" section of Markandeya Purana, known in Bengal as "Chandi."_x000D_ _x000D_ More than the script & songs, it was Bhadra's voice which was a roaring success. Originally a live performance, a pre recorded version was broadcast since 1966. So successful was this that an attempt in 1976 to replace Bhadra's voice with even that of Bengali movie superstar Uttam Kumar backfired, and the Bhadra version was brought back._x000D_ _x000D_ The rendition continues to be played on Mahalaya even now and is now synonymous with Durga Puja._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: When Devi Comes home. Bibek Debroy and Anuradha Goyal._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Prasar Bharati

hook: Last day of Navratri is celebrated as Ayudhya Puja in South India. What is worshipped on this day?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ The word "Ayutham" literally means weapons, refers here to "tools of trade." It has a great significance for factory workers, mechanics and for anyone who works with tools. People clean up the tools and machines and apply turmeric and kumkum. It is common to see all cars and motorcycles also cleaned and garlanded. All mechanical, electrical and hand tools are worshipped as they are a source of living._x000D_ _x000D_ People in South India also worship books, musical instruments & stationery observing the day as Saraswati Puja._x000D_ _x000D_ In Karnataka this is also associated with the day in which Pandavas reclaimed their weapons which they had hidden in a tree before proceeding for their exile. This is another example of integration of Shakti tradition with a great epic, Mahabaratha._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is of a hospital operation theatre being worshipped during Ayudha Pooja._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Various Sources_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Dr Anil Bhat Twitter

hook: In Tamil Nadu, Andhra and some parts of Kerala, an artistic arrangement of dolls is displayed undisturbed during the nine days of Navratri. What are these doll displays called?
explanation: Golu or Kolu is an arrangement of dolls in a multi tiered staircase during Navaratri. This is popular in Tamil Nadu and in other southern States. The arrangement of dolls starts with that of a couple made of wood (marappachi) which is at the top tier. Various dolls of divnities and saints are arranged in other tiers as the arrangement proceeds towards the lowermost tier. At the ground level earthly scenes are depicted through dolls. Apart from the divinities there is a lot of freedom on which dolls to keep._x000D_ _x000D_ The festival season marks an eye catching display of clay dolls in bright colours. Kolu is considered a family tradition and once started, it is believed that it should not be discontinued._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Various Sources_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Vijayakrishnan WikiCommons

hook: In this Indian City, Dussehrah starts on the tenth day of Navratri and continues for 10 further days. All devtas in the district arrive and stay in the city for this period. Which city is this?
explanation: Jagat Singh of Kulu is believed to have been instrumental in bringing a small metal image of Rama from Ayodhya to his royal palace. The deity is Raghunathji and is considered to be the Raja of the entire district. Every year, on Dussehrah day, all the village devis and devtas of Kulu district arrive in Kulu to pay respects to Raghunathji. _x000D_ The deities take the form of mohra masks carried in richly decorated rathas, sometime simple karadu baskets. They are accompanied by the priests, shamans and devotees on foot. They first arrive at the royal palace to pay their respects to Raghunathji. Then they move to the Dhaulpur plain where each devta is assigned a specific place where they camp for the next ten days. An image of Raghunathji is installed in the ratha in the maidan and the ratha is pulled to its designated spot in the Maidan. Each day is celebrated with song and Natti dance to greet the deities. The Raja routinely enquires the deotas about their comfort and well being. On the last day, the Rath Yatra of Raghunathji is performed. A traditional sacrifice of five animals called panchabali is carried out. _x000D_ This festival has been going on for more than 350 years with only occassional interruption. _x000D_ Source: Dussehrah of Kullu. Karuna Goswamy _x000D_ Picture Credit: S. R. Ramanujam _x000D_
