hook: Bathukamma is a form of worship of Devi during Navratri, common in Telangana and Andhra Pradesh. In which form is Devi worshipped? In this picture you can see Badminton Champion PV Sindhu celebrating Bathukamma. _x000D_
explanation: _x000D_ Bathukamma is a beautiful flower stack, arranged with different unique seasonal flowers most of them with medicinal values, in seven concentric layers in the shape of temple gopuram. In Telugu, "Bathukamma" means "Mother Goddess come Alive" and Goddess Maha Gauri-Life Giver is worshipped in the form of Bathukamma the patron goddess of womanhood. _x000D_ Preparing a Bathukamma is a folk art. Women start preparing Bathukamma from the afternoon. They cut the flowers leaving the little length base. Some dip Gunugu (Celosia) flowers in various vibrant colours. They arrange them on a wide plate called thambalam spread with big leaves. The flowers are stacked up in a conical mound, filling the cone with leaves and stems of the flower stalks. The arrangement is decorated with a Lotus or Pumpkin Flower on top of the stack along with Gouramma (a symbolic idol of Gowri made of turmeric). _x000D_ Government of Telangana has declared the Bathukamma Festival as a state festival. The picture is that of a bathukamma of height more than 20 feet competing for a guinness record. _x000D_ Source: EPTRI: ENVIS Centre _x000D_ Picture Credit: Telangana Tourism and Zee Telugu _x000D_

hook: Navratri is celebrated as Dashain in Nepal. It includes a special form of worship. What is it?
explanation: Navratri is the biggest festival of Nepal and is celebrated over 15 days. A Dashain Kotha or any secluded corner in the house is readied. The floor cleaned with a paste of cow dung and clay and the area is darkened with thick curtains. The Goddess is worshipped in a secretive and almost tantric manner in this Dashain Ghar. All the rituals for the nine days would be done by male elders of the family or a hired priest. Like in other places, there are community celebration encompassing varied practices like fasting, bali (animal sacrifice) & meat eating, Kanya puja, tool worship and community dancing. _x000D_ On Dussehrah and afterwards, elders apply Tika, a mix of vermillion, curd and rice on the foreheads of their younger ones. This is an important ritual and people travel to all their relatives to receive the Tika. _x000D_ Source: When Devi Comes home. Bibek Debroy and Anuradha Goyal. _x000D_ Picture Credit: Ajay Kumar Shrestha WikiCommons _x000D_

hook: Navratri is celebrated as Makhar Utsava in this small State. Makhar means a seat and this form of worship is also used in another popular festival in the same State. Which State is this?
explanation: The Utsava murti, the representation of the deity is placed in a makhar. The makhar is a swing of elaborate woodwork or silver which is decorated with coloured papers, mirrors, intricate beadwork, embriodery and colourful flowers. The objective is to create a peaceful or joyous environment for the deities, resembling the swing of a child. _x000D_ Source: When Devi Comes home. Bibek Debroy and Anuradha Goyal. _x000D_ Picture Credit: Soul Travelling _x000D_

hook: While Devi worship is well documented from several millennia, the first deity of Mashishasuramardini evolved only much later. Approximately to which period can we ascribe the first deity of Mahishasuramardini discovered so far?
explanation: The sculpture you see is from the Mathura museum. It is the best preserved from a group of eight sculptures that were discovered at the bottom of a well. They were all badly eroded. They are made of red sandstone characteristic of Mathura region and none are more than 20cm high. They have been dated to the Kushana period. In this sculpture, Mahisasurmardini is shown killing the Buffalo Mahisasur with her hands by pressing it on its back and choking its neck. _x000D_ Source: The Goddess Mashishasuramardini in Kushana Art. Odette Viennot _x000D_ Picture Credit: Odette Viennot _x000D_

hook: The first literary reference to Diwali occurs around 100 CE - 300CE. This work is famous across the globe. Diwali is referred to as Yaksha-ratri in this book. Which is this book?
explanation: Kamasutra mentions the Yaksha-ratri as among the three grand festivals celebrated across the country in contrast to local festivals. Later writers like Hemachandra (11th century CE) have explicitly equated Yaksha-ratri to Diwali or Dipalika. Yaksha-ratri was celebrated on the full moon night of Kartika and involved yagyas and gambling. In some parts of the country, diyas or earthen lamps are laid out outside the house on the night of Dhanteras. They are laid in the southern direction by the lady of the house. The diya burns the whole night to drive away evil spirits, Yakshas who can be both nice or malevolent. This practice is called Yam ka diya, Yama’s lamp. Source: Some Notes on the History of Diwali Festival, P.K. Gode Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Lighting diyas (earthen lamps) is the most defining image of Diwali. A great scholar king of 7th Century CE highlighted this in his work. Who was this scholar king?
explanation: King Harshavardhana of Kannauj (now in Uttar Pradesh) wrote many books. In his book Nagananda he refers to deepa-pratipad-utsava, festival of lighting lamps. An even earlier book Neelamata-purana of around 500CE refers to ‘all around illumination’ at the Deep-mala festival. The practice of lighting diyas on Diwali is indeed a very ancient practice. Source: Some Notes on the History of Diwali Festival, P.K. Gode Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Bhaubij or Bhaidooj is one of the celebrations linked to Diwali. It celebrates the brother sister bond. Which celestial brother sister combination is honored through this celebration?
explanation: Bhaubij or Bhaidooj is also called Yama-dwitya and is normally celebrated a day after Diwali. Hemadri’s Chaturvarg Chintamani of 13th century describes the Bhaubij festival. It quotes the story from Bhavishya Purana which states that once Yamuna (also called Yami) had hosted her brother Yama, the God of death at her house. It says that since that time this festival was observed by brothers and sisters. A marathi text of 13th century, Lilacharitra, describes how it is celebrated. It states people cooked choice dishes like modak and laddoo. Source: Some Notes on the History of Diwali Festival, P.K. Gode Picture Credit: http://hindumyths.blogspot.com/2013/10/47-yama-and-yami.html

hook: Al Beruni's account of Diwali is one of the first accounts of a foreigner. He associated it with the story of a mythical king who is a household name in Kerala. Who is this king?
explanation: Al Beruni came to India with the forces of Mahmud of Ghazni in 11th century. He wrote a famous book Tehkeek-e-Hind (Discovery of India). The Diwali description of Al Beruni is very close to how modern day Diwali is celebrated. He describes Diwali thus, “1st Karthika or new moon day, when the sun marches in Libra is called Diwali. Then people bathe, dress festively, make presents to each other of areca nuts & betel leaves; they ride to the temples to give alms and play merrily with each other till noon. In the night they light a great number of lamps in every place so that the air is perfectly clear. The cause of the festival is that Laxmi, the wife of Vasudeva once a year on this day liberates Bali, the son of Virochana, who is the prisoner in the seventh earth. Therefore the festival is called Balirajya i.e. the principality of Bali.” One of the days of Diwali festival is called Bali Pratipada._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Some Notes on the History of Diwali Festival, P.K. Gode_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: The main Diwali celebrations in most of South India happens a day before that in the North of the country. It celebrates the killing of a demon by Krishna and his wife Satyabhama. Which demon was killed?
explanation: In South India Diwali is associated with day of the killing of Narakasura by Satyabhama. Naraka was the King of Pragjyotisha, Kamrup in Assam. He was a powerful king who became drunk with power and conquered the earth and enslaved 16000 women. He also received a boon from Brahma to have power over all males. Krishna fought Narakasura and was hit by the thunderbolt of Narakasura. Krishna feigned injury and collapsed. In rage, Satyabhama shot an arrow and killed Narakasura. Later, Narakasura's son Bhagadadta, a legendary warrior would fight on the side of the Kauravas. It would take the might of Arjuna to kill Bhagadatta._x000D_ _x000D_ In the picture you can see Krishna battling Narakasura who is seated on an elephant._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Hindu Festivals and Sacred Days, Ramakrishna Math_x000D_ _x000D_ Diwali - Festival of Lights, Rina Singh_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: In Eastern India on the night of Diwali, a great Goddess is worshipped. Who is this Devi?
explanation: In Bengali tradition Kali Puja is celebrated on the night of Diwali. The night worship is perhaps a nod to the tantric origins of the worship. According to a legend, Lord Shiva lay down on the path of goddess Kali, who went on a rampage slaughtering demons. After she stepped unknowingly on Shiva's bare body, she calmed down. The festival is celebrated to control ego and other negative tendencies that hinder spiritual progress._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Hindu Festivals and Sacred Days, Ramakrishna Math_x000D_ _x000D_ https://www.indiatoday.in/india/story/diwali-kali-puja-onam-naraka-chaturdasi-celebrated-return-of-rama-mahabali-349147-2016-10-29_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: The day after Diwali also celebrates Krishna’s defeat of Indra’s arrogance. Krishna lifted the Govardhan mountain to shield the villagers of Gokul from Indra’s wrath who unleashed rain & thunder to make the villagers worship him. It is called Govardhan Puja but is also known by a more colloquial name. What is it called?
explanation: On the day following Diwali, Govardhan Puja is celebrated in northern and western India. Several varieties of food are heaped in the shape of a mountain representing Govardhan Giri. The feast is known as Annakoot, meaning mountain of food._x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is that of an see ISCKON celebration of Govardhan Puja in Vrindavan – see the recreation of the Govardhan mountain with food on it._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Bhoga Naivedyam, Sujata Shukla_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Dhanteras, the first day of Diwali is also celebrated as ‘National Ayurveda Day’. Who is worshipped on this day?
explanation: Dhanavantri is considered the God of Ayurveda and brings good health and prosperity. Hindus consider it auspicious on this day to make new purchases of utensils or any item made of metal. As per legend Dhanvantri is supposed to have emerged from the churning of the celestial ocean, Samudra manthan bearing a kalash (utensil) full of amrit (nectar)._x000D_ _x000D_ In the picture you can see Dhanvantri holding Amrita Kalash (medicine pot) on walls of Keshava Temple, at Somanathapura, Karnataka._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Dr. Shreenand Bapat's Lecture History of Diwali, Bhandarkar Oriental Research Institute_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Diwali is a very important festival of Jains. Diwali commemorates which event in the life of Mahavira, the 24th Jain Tirthankara?
explanation: As per Jain tradition, Lord Mahavira attained Nirvana on Diwali. Jains celebrate this occasion by lighting lamps. Laxmi Puja and the worship of books of accounts are also part of Jain tradition._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Some Notes on the History of Diwali Festival, P.K. Gode_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Diwali is also linked to an important Guru of Sikhs and celebrated. Which Guru is this?
explanation: Guru Hargobindji is the sixth Guru of Sikhs and is the son of Guru Arjan Ji who was killed by Jehangir. Guru Hargobindji wanted to avenge his father's death and started building an army. Threatened by this, Jehangir imprisoned him in Gwalior fort. Sikhs protested his imprisonment and forced Jehangir to order the release of the Guru. By a ruse, Guru Hargobindji also secured the release of 52 other Kings who had been imprisoned with him and then marched to Amritsar. The city was supposed to have been lit to welcome him just like it was lit to welcome Lord Rama. Diwali is celebrated as Bandi Chor Diwas to celebrate his release. Source: Diwali - Festival of Llights, Rina Singh Picture Credit: http://www.columbia.edu/itc/mealac/pritchett/00routesdata/1500_1599/sikhism/bandichor/bandichor.html Wikimedia Commons

hook: Gambling is one of the defining celebrations of Diwali in North India. We remember the gambling of Pandavas, but the Diwali gambling celebrates a game of dice between a celestial couple. Whose game is being celebrated on Diwali night?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ One of the most enduring motifs throughout Indian art has been Shiva and Parvati playing a game of dice. The Puranic tradition insists that the entire world is created as a result of this divine play between Shiva and Parvati, representing Purusha and Prakriti, with each move of the players mirroring different states of the world, potential or realised. The dice game between Shiva & Parvati is depicted in many sculptures especially in Western India. Traditionally, Shiva loses but there are a few rare reliefs where Shiva wins by cheating. On such occasions, Parvati lifts her hand and expresses vismaya or amazement. Vismaya is an important part of Shiva–Parvati games._x000D_ _x000D_ In the picture - Sculpture at Ellora. Parvati defeats Shiva at gambling. She asks with Her hand in the quizzical mode as to why He is cheating. He asks for one more chance by raising His right index finger._x000D_ _x000D_ This is very vividly captured in this Deepavali Special Edition of Kalki, a Tamil magazine in the year 1965._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Hindu Festivals and Sacred Days, Ramakrishna Math_x000D_ _x000D_ https://www.sahapedia.org/towards-cultural-history-indian-board-games-backgammon-chaupar-and-chaturanga_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: https://indianexpress.com/article/religion/yours-faithfully-when-goddess-parvati-defeated-lord-shiva-at-dice-on-mount-kailash-4896859/_x000D_ _x000D_ Kalki, 1965

hook: Gujarati communities (and some others) celebrate the day after Diwali as New Year, which marks the coronation of a renowned King. Which king is it?
explanation: King Vikramaditya is the celebrated king who reigned from Ujjain. Several subsequent kings also took the name of Vikramaditya which is a reflection of his glory. Vikramaditya started his reign in 57 BCE defeating the Shakas. Diwali is celebrated to mark the start of his reign and the Vikram Samvat. Please note that there is no historical proof of a Vikramaditya around 57 BCE._x000D_ _x000D_ In the picture you can see a page from an almanac for the fortnight of Shraavana Shukla Paksha (August 1911) with the headings Vikram Samvat 1968, Saka Samvat 1833 and Nepal Sambat 1031._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Some Notes on the History of Diwali Festival, P.K. Gode_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: In Nepal, the second day of Diwali is dedicated to an animal. Which animal is this?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Kukkur Tihar is the second day of Diwali in Nepal. All dogs, including street dogs are decorated with big red tikkas on their foreheads, garlanded, fed lavishly and worshipped with Arati. The dogs are worshipped as the vehicle of Bhairava, in which role they guard homes from theft and destruction._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Animals and Hinduism - Nanditha Krishna_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Many of us know the Nandigram of West Bengal. It was the epicenter of Mamata Banerjee's political fight against CPM Govt. There is also a Nandigram just outside Ayodhya. How is it connected with Ramayana and the Diwali celebration?
explanation: Bharata ruled from Nandigram during Rama's exile. When Rama was returning to Ajodhya, Hanuman came in advance and met Bharata here. Rama wanted to confirm that Bharata desired the return of Rama to Ajodhya to take up the throne. Therefore Hanuman came in disguise as a Brahmin and prompted Bharata to reveal his mind. The temple here shows Bharata and Hanuman in embrace after Hanuman revealed to Bharata his true form. Bharata received Rama at Nandigram. The Jata Kund here is believed to be the place where Lord Rama and Lakshman cut their matted hair at the end of their exile._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: In the Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni_x000D_ _x000D_ Uttar Pradesh A to Z, UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Up Tourism

hook: Which coastal town has a legend of a twisted Linga that has a connection to Ganapati. The linga is rooted to the ground and was twisted when Ravana tried to retrieve it?
explanation: Gokarna is a popular tourist and pilgrimage place in Northern Karnataka. The famous Gokarna linga, a piece of solid reddish rock is twisted like an old stump. Here Ravana had to perform his evening rituals and gave the linga that he was carrying to Ganesha who was in disguise. Ganesha put the deity down and Ravana could not pick it up again. He tried so hard to remove the linga forcibly from the place where it was stuck. In this process the lingam was twisted. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck PicureCredit:

hook: This place in South India celebrates the spot where Vibishana gave a deity of Vishnu to Ganesha who put it down. It could not be lifted again. Which is this place?
explanation: Rāma had given this ancestral deity, called Ranganātha, to Vibhīshana and told him not to put the deity down until he reached Lanka. When Vibhīshana came to the beautiful Kāverī River, he was overwhelmed with the desire to bathe. He gave the deity to Ganesha in disguise, and Ganesha agreed to hold it for him, under the condition that Vibhīshana would come promptly if he called for him three times. When Vibhīshana was underwater, taking his dips in the Kāverī River, the boy called out thrice. Of course, Vibhīshana could not hear him. The boy put down the deity of Ranganātha, and so it is that the deity has been worshipped on the island of Shrīrangam ever since. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck PicureCredit: SR Ramanujam

hook: Like Char Dham, there are a group of Ganapati temples that form a circuit in Maharashtra. How many temples are there in this circuit?
explanation: _x000D_ Popularly called Astavinayak temples, the group of eight Ganapati temples are around the Pune and Konkan region. The deities are monolith and are also Swayambhu, self formed and not man made. Astavinayak is a popular pilgrimage that many pilgrims cover in two or three days. It is a commonly held belief that if a pilgrim visits any one of the 8 temples, they must visit the others as well to receive the full benefit of the pilgrimage._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Temples and Legends of Maharashtra, Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan_x000D_ _x000D_ PicureCredit: Scoutmytrip.com

hook: A temple in India offers a single modak weighing about 72 Kg to Ganpati once a year. Which temple is this?
explanation: Mukkuruni Vinayagar within Madurai Meenakshi temple in Tamil Nadu receives this offering on Vinayaka Chaturthi each year. Once Ganpati was looking for physical work and was asked to transport some material in a cart. At the end of the work when he was asked to specify his wages for the work, Ganpati asked for 30 kgs of rice (Mukkuruni in Tamil). To mark this legend, a single modak is made with 30 kgs of rice and 30 kgs of jaggery and other material, eventually weighing about 72 kg. The cooking takes 24 hours. Source: Bhoga Naivedyam by Sujata Shukla Rajan PicureCredit: Indian Express

hook: This rock is the abode of Ganapati who, in local language, is known as Ganapti at the top. The rock itself is believed to be 3.8 billion years old. Where is this Ganapati temple?
explanation: _x000D_ This temple is called Uchhi Pillayar, meaning Ganpati at the top. The temple is on top of a Droog (steep rock). It served as a fortification and was a scene of many battles involving the Vijayanagara empire, Nayaks of Madurai, Nawabs and the British. The rock is 83 mts tall. The Ganapati temple on top of the hill, another Ganapati temple at the foothills and a rock cut Shiva temple are among the most visited in the city. Rockfort is also a popular evening spot due to the strong breeze on top and as the rockfort offers commanding views of the Cauvery river, the agricultural fields and the Srirangam temple._x000D_ _x000D_ Source:_x000D_ _x000D_ PicureCredit: SR Ramanujam

hook: This Ganapati temple is administered by the Madras regiment of the Indian Army. Which temple is this?
explanation: A Yakshi would knock down the soldiers who were guarding the Padmanabhapuram palace of the Travancore dynasty. A soldier once retrieved a Vinayaka deity from a river and held it with him during the guard duty and was spared the wrath of the Yakshi. The deity was then celebrated as the war God and the Nair Brigade of the dynasty was made responsible for protecting the deity. The deity was later moved to Trivandrum and the Madras regiment of the Indian Army is now responsible for maintaining the temple. The Apex Board and the Executive Authority of the temple are headed by the Colonel and the Commandant of the Madras Regiment of the Indian Army. Source: Bhoga Naivedyam by Sujata Shukla Rajan PicureCredit:

hook: In which Indian city would you find Ganesh Tok, a Ganesha temple on a hill top?
explanation: Ganesh Tok is a popular tourist place in Gangtok. It offers excellent views of the city and is at a height of about 6500 feet. Source: PicureCredit: Kailas98, Creative Commons

hook: Which of the following started the popular Sarvajanik Ganapati, the worship and celebration of Ganapati in public places?
explanation: Ganesh Chaturthi had been a popular festival for a long time. Tilak introduced the large scale public worship of Ganpati (Sarvajanik) as a form of patriotic movement to overcome the restrictions that British had imposed on large gatherings in the civil society. Further, the festival had already gathered a character of a classless casteless celebration. The organisation of Sarvajanik Ganpati was also intended to overcome a perceived weakness of the Hindu society to organise itself. Tilak initiated the Sarvajanik Ganpati in 1893 in Pune. Source: Ganesha Chaturthi: Cultural-Historical Background and Celebration by Singh, Rana P.B. and Ratate, Madhav J. PicureCredit: SR Ramanujam

hook: What is the significance of the Lenyadri Ganapati temple in Maharashtra?
explanation: In Lenyadri, Ganesha is worshipped as Girijatmaj. Ganesha is Swayambhu in a cave. The name of the idol ‘Girijatmaj’ comes as ‘Girija’ meaning Devi Parvati and ‘Aatmaj’ meaning Son. It is said that in this particular cave Devi Parvati performed tapascharya for twelve long years for the gain of a son. After this long tapascharya, Lord Ganesh himself came before her. Leni means caves in Marathi. The temple faces the southern direction and is rock cut within the cave. It closely resembles a Buddhist Vihara. Source: Temples and Legends of Maharashtra, Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan. Shree Lenyadri Ganpati Devsthan Trust, https://lenyadriganpati.com/en/a-homepage-section/ PicureCredit: https://lenyadriganpati.com/en/a-homepage-section/

hook: In which of the following countries is Ganesh Chaturthi a national holiday?
explanation: Ganesh Chaturthi is a national holiday in Mauritius. In addition, Shivratri, Thaipoosam, Ugadi and Deepavali are also national holidays. This is a reflection of the strong connection that Mauritius has with the Hindu culture. Source: https://www.officeholidays.com/countries/mauritius/2022 PicureCredit: Prakash Sookun Facebook

hook: Worship of this Ganesha atop a hilltop was stopped for a brief period in 2017 and then restored. What was the reason?
explanation: Dholkal Ganesh is at a beautiful spot located 3000 feet high in Dantewada district in Chattisgarh. The Ganesha is a 3 feet stone deity believed to be made during the Naga dynasty between 10th and 11th centuries. The deity commands a panoramic look over the dense forests around. As the Ganesha became popular it attracted visitors to the area. Irked by this, Maoists are believed to have broken and pushed the deity down the cliff in January 2017. The deity was restored by an ASI team led by Padmashri Arun Kumar Sharma, aged 84. The local residents desired that the deity is reinstalled at the same spot rather than relocated to the safety of a museum. This was done on Basant Panchami day on February 1, 2017. Source: https://dantewada.nic.in/ https://openthemagazine.com/features/dispatch/the-return-of-god/ PicureCredit: Open, The Magazine

hook: In which part of India would you find this rock cut Ganpati?
explanation: Unakoti is in Tripura. It is a Saivite pilgrimage site that dates back to atleast 7th – 9th century. Unakoti means one less than a crore. It is believed that as many rock cut carvings are available here. These carvings are located at a beautifully landscaped forest area with green vegetation all around. The main Shiva sculpture is about 30 feet tall. There are various other stone as well as rock cut images at Unakoti. Source: Tripura Tourism https://tripuratourism.gov.in/archaeological-sites PicureCredit: SR Ramanujam

hook: In which country outside India would you find this Ganesha deity at the edge of a volcano?
explanation: Mount Bromo is in Java, Indonesia. It is an active volcano. The word Bromo is derived from Brahma. The Ganesha deity is supposed to have been installed 700 years ago and he is believed to protect the residents from the volcano. The Ganesha is actively worshipped even now. The volcano itself is one of the most visited tourist places in Java. Source: PicureCredit: Virendra Dhir

hook: Where will you find the earliest reference to Ganapati as a word, even though it is not Ganesha it refers to?
explanation: The earliest literary references to Ganapati as the Lord of the ganas are in connection with deities other than Ganesha himself. Rig Veda addresses Brihaspatu as ganaanaam tvaa ganapatim havaamahe. The description is also transferred to Indra on another occasion when he is also addressed as Ganapati. Later work such as Taittiriya Aranyaka refers to Dantin, who has a twisted trunk (vakratunda) and who holds a sheaf of corn, a sugarcane and a club. Source: Ganesha; The Auspicious, The Beginning by Shakunthala Jagannathan and Nandhita Krishna PicureCredit: https://greenmesg.org/stotras/ganesha/gananam_tva_ganapatim.php

hook: The earliest archeological evidence resembling Ganapati comes from a plaque around 1200 to 1000 BC. It contains an elephant headed figure holding a sword and snake on one hand and a quill in another. Where was this found?
explanation: This was found in Luristan in western Iran. The figure in the plaque appears to be bearded, a sign of great wisdom. The quill is also a symbol of learning and knowledge. The plaque is dated prior to the Taittriya Aranyaka and corresponds to what is called as later Vedic period by historians. Source: Ganesha; The Auspicious, The Beginning by Shakunthala Jagannathan and Nandhita Krishna PicureCredit: Ganesha; The Auspicious, The Beginning by Shakunthala Jagannathan and Nandhita Krishna

hook: One of the earliest depictions of Ganapati (in 1st or 2nd century AD) is found in a decorative frieze around a Stupa. In which country would you find this?
explanation: The frieze is around the Kantaka Cetinga Stupa, near Mihintale in Sri Lanka. A double procession of ganas converge towards a central dwarfish elephant headed figure whose trunk is turned to the left. This is undoubtedly, Ganapati Source: Ganesha; The Auspicious, The Beginning by Shakunthala Jagannathan and Nandhita Krishna PicureCredit: Ganesha; The Auspicious, The Beginning by Shakunthala Jagannathan and Nandhita Krishna

hook: This important dynasty of India had Ganapati as the family deity. Which dynasty is this?
explanation: Ganesha was the family deity of Peshwas. They devoted a good deal of money and attention on the construction and renovation of Ganesha temples around Pune region. These temples pre date the Peshwa region and the avataras date back to far remote times. But the form in which these temples are seen today is a result of Peshwa patronage. The Sarasbaug Ganapati temple in Pune is considered as the family temple of Peshwas. Source: Temples and Legends of Maharashtra, Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan PicureCredit: Pakshya Creative Commons

hook: In which country outside India is this form of Ganesha, as an embracing couple, worshipped popularly?
explanation: Ganesha worship is very popular in Japan and spread through Buddhism. Sōshin Kangiten is the most enigmatic and elusive Japanese version of Gaṇeśa. Ganesha represents Dual God of Boundless Bliss. The Japanese Dual Kangiten is an elephant-headed deity couple in embrace. It represents the unity of contrasting genders and opposites. Avalokiteśvara (a Bodhisatva) is believed to have seduced and calmed down Vinayaka resulting in the harmonious union. Source: Lode Rosseels, Gaṇeśa’s Underbelly, Universiteit Gent PicureCredit: Ganesha; The Auspicious, The Beginning by Shakunthala Jagannathan and Nandhita Krishna

hook: This Ganapati temple is housed within a national park and the entire family of Ganesha is worshipped here. Which national park is it in?
explanation: Trinetra Ganapati temple is in Ranthambore Fort within the national park. Ganesha is present in this temple with his full family consisting of Riddhi and Siddhi (consorts) and two sons (Shub and Labh). His vahana, mouse, is also present. Source: Rajasthan Tourism PicureCredit:

hook: In Sringeri in Karnataka, Ganapati is worshipped in a unique form. Which form is it?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Torana Ganapati in Sringeri is worshipped in the doorframe. There is no GarbhaGrha and the door is always in closed state. The 32nd Acharya of the Peetham, Jagadguru Sri Vriddha Narasimha Bharati Mahaswamiji used to reside adjacent to the temple of Goddess Sharada. One occasion, the Acharya had a vision of the Ganapati when he was contemplating on resolving an issue in the mutt affairs. He offered His prayer by offering a dhurva to the image of Lord Ganapati carved on the Torana of the door nearby, and the problem got resolved. Since then, the Ganapati has come to be worshipped as Torana Ganapati._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Dakshinamnaya Sri Sharada Peetham, Sringeri_x000D_ _x000D_ https://sringeri.net/temples/sri-torana-ganapati_x000D_ _x000D_ PicureCredit: SR Ramanujam_x000D_ _x000D_ https://sringeri.net/temples/sri-torana-ganapati

hook: Which great Saint of Maharashtra composed the famous bhajan to Ganpati, "sukhkarta, dukhharta". He was a contemporary of Chatrapati Shivaji Maharaj.
explanation: Swami Samarth Ramdas is supposed to have composed the aarti in the Mayureshwar temple. It is believed that the pilgrimage of the Ganesha temples should start and end with Mayureshwar. The bhajan itself is very popular with every aarti being accompanied by the bhajan. Source: Temples and Legends of Maharashtra, Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan PicureCredit: Kalyandasdomgaon, Creative Commons

hook: Maruti, another name for Hanumanji, was the first Japanese car manufactured in India. Who was Suzuki’s joint venture partner in this endeavor?
explanation: Hanuman is also called Maruti, meaning "son of Marut" or the wind god. Sanjay Gandhi, the younger son of Indira Gandhi, conceived the idea for this car. Between 1964 and 1967, he apprenticed at Rolls-Royce (though it seems he didn’t complete it). In 1966, he began assembling a car in a garage and incorporated Maruti as a company in 1971. He acquired land for a factory and, to raise funds, purchased a fabrication company, selling road rollers and building bus bodies, primarily for government buyers. When Indira Gandhi lost power, the venture stalled. Sanjay died shortly after her re-election in a plane crash. Following his death, the government revived the project as a joint venture with Suzuki. Maruti largely became the first modern engineering product available to Indians during the era of the License Raj. Ironically, the man who named the car "Maruti" perished in an airplane accident. Source: R.C. Bhargava, The Maruti Story Picture is of Harpal Singh who received the first Maruti Car. He never changed his car.

hook: One of Hanuman’s popular names is Anjaneya. To whom does it refer?
explanation: Hanuman was born to a royal monkey couple: his father, Kesari, and his mother, Anjana. He is called Anjaneya ("son of Anjana") and Kesarinandan ("son of Kesari"). The name Anjaneya Swami is especially common in South India. His birth was a result of blessings from the wind god—known as Marut, Vayu, or Pawan—to his parents. Some stories suggest Hanuman was born simultaneously with Rama. During the yajna Dasharatha performed to beget sons, the gods blessed him with payasam (a divine dessert) to be shared among his three wives to aid conception. At the same time, Hanuman’s parents were praying for a child. As the gods sent the payasam from heaven, they instructed Vayu to divert a few drops to Anjana. Thus, Rama and Hanuman were born concurrently, bound by the same divine blessing, making them inseparable. Because of Vayu’s role, Hanuman is also known as Maruti, Vayuputra, or Pavanputra. Source: “The Story of Hanuman,” Durham University Raja Ravi Varma’s painting

hook: Hanuman literally means "disfigured jaw." Which Puranic story explains the origin of this name?
explanation: One morning, baby Hanuman felt ravenous. Spotting a bright red, glistening sphere in the sky, he mistook it for a juicy fruit and leapt to grab it. That sphere was the rising sun! Indra, angered by this audacity, struck Hanuman with his thunderbolt, hitting his chin. Hanuman fell to earth, lifeless, with a broken jaw. His father, Vayu, the wind god, grew furious and withheld air from all beings. Shiva intervened, reviving Hanuman, and Vayu relented. However, Hanuman’s jaw remained disfigured. Source: “The Story of Hanuman,” Durham University Picture is Raja Ravi Varma’s painting

hook: Whose chariot in the Kurukshetra war bore Hanuman Ji on its flag?
explanation: Hanuman features prominently in two Mahabharata stories. In one, Arjuna encountered Hanuman and questioned why Rama needed monkeys to build a stone bridge to Lanka when he could have used arrows. Hanuman argued that an arrow bridge couldn’t bear the monkey army’s weight. Arjuna boasted he could build such a bridge, but Hanuman easily destroyed it. Krishna mediated, and Hanuman agreed to adorn Arjuna’s chariot flag. In another tale, during the Pandavas’ exile, Draupadi sent Bhima to fetch a flower she admired. Bhima encountered an old monkey blocking his path—Hanuman in disguise. Unable to move even the monkey’s tail, Bhima realized his identity and sought forgiveness. Hanuman blessed him and promised to appear on Arjuna’s flag in the great war. Thus, the saffron flag with Hanuman symbolizes victory for Hindus. Bhima, born to Kunti through Vayu’s blessings, is Hanuman’s celestial brother, both being Vayuputras. Yet, Hanuman graced Arjuna’s flag. Source: Various Hanumanji’s flags on sale in Ayodhya

hook: Who blessed Hanuman with immortality, making him a "Chiranjeevi"?
explanation: After the Battle of Lanka, Rama ascended as king. He rewarded all who aided him, giving Hanuman the finest gifts. Hanuman discarded them, perplexing onlookers. He explained that Rama and Sita resided in his heart, needing no material reward. When challenged to prove it, Hanuman sliced open his chest, revealing their image within his heart. Bleeding fatally, he demonstrated his devotion. Rama saved him, granting him immortality. Source: “The Story of Hanuman,” Durham University Picture is Raja Ravi Varma’s painting of the episode

hook: Hanuman is humanity’s eternal protector. Which of his names reflects this?
explanation: Sita and Rama blessed Hanuman as "Chiranjeevi" (immortal) and "Sankat Mochan" (reliever of distress). Hindus believe he alleviates pain and suffering, a role he continues to fulfill. Popular prayers by Tulsidas, such as the ‘Hanuman Chalisa’ and ‘Sankat Mochan Ashtak’, are recited daily by millions for protection. The ‘Sankat Mochan Ashtak’, with eight verses, seeks well-being, while the ‘Bajrang Baan’, chanted in distress, contains ‘beej mantras’ to invoke Hanuman. The ‘Hanuman Chalisa’ remains Tulsidas’s most celebrated work. In the ‘Sankat Mochan Hanuman Ashtak’, Tulsidas writes: "Ko nahin janat hai jag mein, Kapi, Sankat Mochan naam tiharo," meaning, "O Hanuman Ji, all your devotees know you as the remover of difficulties." The Sankat Mochan Mandir in Kashi (Varanasi), slightly removed from the bustling Kashi Vishwanath Mandir, is a revered site. Source: Speaking Tree Picture is entrance to the Sankat Mochan Temple in Varanasi

hook: Hanuman Garhi is Ayodhya’s most revered site. In what role does Hanuman reside there?
explanation: At Hanuman Garhi, Hanuman Ji serves as Ayodhya’s protector, a duty entrusted by Lord Rama before his departure from earth. Fittingly, the deity, though small, sits atop a throne. The temple, a fortified structure with bastions at each corner, lies near the Ram Janmabhoomi site on Ramkot’s hilly terrain. Source: Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni Picture is Hanuman Garhi temple in Ayodhya

hook: People say Hanuman was always a Brahmachari (someone who doesn't marry). But some stories say he had a son. What was his name?
explanation: Even though Hanuman was a Brahmachari, there's an interesting story about his son. After Hanuman burned Lanka with the fire on his tail, he took a swim in the sea. A drop of his sweat fell into the mouth of a big fish. This fish gave birth to Makardhwaja. People who worked for Ahiravana, who was Ravana's son and ruled the underworld (Patala), caught the fish. When they cut it open, they found Makardhwaja. Ahiravana saw how strong he was and made him guard the gates of his kingdom. Ahiravana kidnapped Rama and Lakshmana and took them to Patala. Hanuman followed to save them. At the gate, a creature who was part monkey and part reptile (called a Makara) stopped Hanuman. This creature said he was Hanuman's son. Using his spiritual powers, Hanuman learned the story was true. Even so, he fought his son and put him in prison. Then, Hanuman killed Ahiravana and saved Rama and Lakshmana. When they were leaving, Rama made Makardhwaja the king of Patala. This story is found in some local versions of the Ramayana. Source: https://www.speakingtree.in/allslides/lord-hanuman-had-a-son-what-was-his-name/75028) Sketch of Makardhwaj

hook: Hanuman first met Rama at Kishkinda. Which state is Kishkinda in?
explanation: Hanuman was the advisor for Sugriva, who was Vali's brother. The two brothers together ruled Kishkinda but fell out due to a misunderstanding. Vali made Sugriva leave, and Sugriva lived on Rishyamuka mountain where Vali couldn't reach him due to a Rishi’s curse. When Rama and Lakshmana came to Kishkinda, Hanuman disguised himself to learn about them. He had never met Rama before, but as soon as he saw him, he knew who he was. That's why people see Hanuman as the greatest 'Rambhakta' (devotee of Rama) – he recognized the Lord right away. People believe that Kishkinda is now the town of Hampi in Karnataka, on the banks of the Tungabhadra River. It used to be the capital of the powerful Vijaynagara empire. You can still see many old buildings in Hampi that remind people of Hanuman's connection to this place. Source: hampi.in Picture is the Anjaneya temple at hilltop, Hampi

hook: There's a famous Jyotirlinga town that also has a 'Panchamukhi' (five-faced) Hanuman temple. Which town is it?
explanation: The Panchamukhi Hanuman Temple, which has five faces, is about 2 kilometers away from the Sri Ramanathaswamy Temple in Rameshwaram. Hanuman fought Ahiravana, Ravana's son. To defeat Ahiravana, Hanuman learned that he had to put out five lamps burning in different directions at the same time. So, Hanuman took his Panchamukhi form and blew out all the lamps together. The five faces are Varaha (north), Narasimha (south), Garuda (west), Hanuman (east), and Hayagriva (upwards). A special thing about this temple is the 'floating stones.' People believe these stones were used to build the bridge (Sethu Bandhanam) from Rameshwaram to Lanka. Source: https://rameshwaramtourism.co.in/panchamukhi-hanuman-temple-rameshwaram Picture is Panchmukhi Anjaneya from Rameshwaram

hook: When Hanuman was going to the Himalayas to get the Sanjeevani herb, he rested at a place in Himachal Pradesh. Now, there's a famous temple there. Where is it?
explanation: On his way from Lanka to the Himalayas to get the Sanjeevani herb, Hanuman met a wise man named Yaaku on a hilltop. After getting information about the herb, he promised Yaaku that he would meet him again on his way back to Lanka. However, on his return journey, Hanuman had to fight with someone named "Kalnemi" who tried to trick him, and this took a lot of time. To reach Lanka quickly, he decided not to meet the wise man again and took the fastest route. The wise man was sad that Hanuman didn't keep his promise. However, as Hanuman flew past the area, a deity of him appeared on the hill by itself. To honor Hanuman's visit, Yaaku built a temple there. People still believe that the temple has Hanuman's footprints, and the monkeys around the temple are his descendants. The temple is at the highest point in Shimla. Source: https://shimlatourism.co.in/jakhu-temple-shimla Picture is Jakhoo temple at Shimla

hook: It is said that Hanuman rested at Hanuman Tok in Gangtok, Sikkim, while he was going to get the Sanjeevani herb. Who takes care of this temple?
explanation: Pictures of Hanuman carrying the Sanjeevani hill are very common. Many of his names are related to this story. This is also why people see him as someone who helps remove troubles. Many places want to be connected to this story. "Tok" means hill, and Hanuman Tok is about 11 kilometers from Gangtok town. It was built in 1968 at a high altitude (about 7200 feet or 2194 meters). The soldiers of the Indian Army who live nearby carefully look after this temple. Many people believe that if you make a wish here, it will come true. Source: sikkimtourism.gov.in Picture is Hanuman Tok temple at Gangtok

hook: Rock carvings in this far off region attest to the spread of Krishna tradition widely. Balarama and Krishna are seen in these rock carvings. Where is this found?
explanation: Rock carvings at Chilas in Kashmir, along a major trade route in the Gilgit valley, include a pair of figures, holding what seem to be a plough and disc, wearing wide open coats similar to that of the Kushana period. They are identified as Balarama and Krishna based on the Kharoshthi inscriptions found in the area. Source: A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India From the Stone Age to the 12th Century by Upinder Singh

hook: This Greek Ambassador to India erected a stone pillar venerating Lord Krishna. This is one among the earliest surviving Hindu religious structures in India. Remains of a temple too have been found in front of this pillar. In which modern State of India will you find this pillar still standing?
explanation: Heliodorus, the Greek ambassador, describes himself as a Bhagavata—i.e., a worshipper of the god Vasudeva Krishna, and that he set up this pillar in honor of this god. The pillar is dated around 120 BCE. Nearby the remains of an ancient temple dated to the 3rd Century BCE have been found. The pillar is still standing in Besnagar, near Vidisha in Madhya Pradesh. Source: A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India From the Stone Age to the 12th Century by Upinder Singh

hook: As Krishna was being carried from Mathura in a cart to escape Mughal destruction, his deity got stuck in this place and is now a popular pilgrimage spot. What is this place called?
explanation: Shrinathji’s idol was brought to Rajasthan from Govardhana near Vrindavan to protect it from the hands of the Mughal emperor Aurangzeb who in 1665 was bent upon vandalizing the area of Vrindavan by widespread destruction of Hindu temples. For almost six months the statue stayed in Agra after which the custodians of the idol of Shrinathji left that place with the idol in search of a new location. When the wheel of the chariot carrying the Lord got stuck in the mud at a place called Sihar, the Maharana Rajsingh of Mewar saw it as a divine sign that Lord Krishna wished to settle here, and thus a temple was built at this spot and the holy township of Nathdwara grew around the temple._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: https://www.nathdwaratemple.org/history/shrinathjis-arrival-at-nathdwara

hook: Somnath, a Jyotirling is also closely connected to Krishna's life, how?
explanation: Bhalka Teerth is the location where the arrow of the poacher Jara hit Sri Krishna. It is about 4 or 5 kms away from Somnath. Bhagvan Shree Krishna was resting in meditation pose under a pipal tree when the poacher misread the foot of Bhagvan Shree Krishna as a deer and hit from a distance. This divined leela of Bhagvan Shree Krishna is immortalized by a beautiful temple and an ancient pipal tree. Bhagvan Shree Krishna then walked a small distance and arrived at the holy banks of river Hiran from where he took his last journey to his Neejdham. Source: Somnath Trust

hook: What is the material from which the deities in Puri Jagannath temple are made of ?
explanation: The deities are made of wood. The deities are replaced at intervals of 8, 11, 12 or 19 years, most often after 19 years. The ritual is called Nabakalebara. Four holy neem trees are identified and cut to make the famous wooden embodiments of Jagannātha Krishna. The goddess Mangalā is the guardian of the forest from which trees are cut. The priests come to the forest to seek the right tree for the new image. Source: Immortal India Volume 1, https://www.shreejagannatha.in/nabakalebara/

hook: Goddess Vimala is a Shakti Peeth and is housed within a famous Krishna temple. Where is Goddess Vimala?
explanation: At the famous pilgrimage temple of Krishna Jagannātha at Purī in Orissa, the goddess Vimalā is known as the ancient goddess of the place. Those who have studied the folk deities of Orissa and the emergence of the Krishna worship here say that this place, too, was a pitha of the Goddess long before being appropriated by Lord Krishna. Nīlāchala, on which the temple sits today, was once the domain of the goddess named Vimalā. Her shrine still is present in the sacred precincts, and she is the first to receive the prasād, the consecrated food offerings that only then become the mahāprasād, the “great grace of the Lord,” to be distributed to the worshippers. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: Seva Kunj in Vrindavan is the location of one of the Leelas of Krishna.
explanation: Among the most famous of the līlāsthalas near the Yamunā bank are the forest groves where Krishna would meet the gopīs at night and dance the rās dance with each of them in a wide circle. He created a well for them when they were exhausted with the dance and too thirsty to walk to the Yamunā River. There, too, is Sevā Kunj, the very bower, so they say, where Krishna met Rādhā for lovemaking. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: The site called Gopishvara in Vrindavan marks Shiva in the life of Krishna. Which incident is this
explanation: Shiva himself became a gopī to witness and enjoy the sacred rās dance of Krishna with the gopīs. He was drawn to Braj by the sound of Krishna’s flute, but was refused entry and could not cross the Yamunā until he became a woman. So Shiva bathed in the river and emerged a gopī. At Gopīshvara in the evening, Shiva is adorned by the priests with a bright sari and the silver mask of a woman wearing lipstick and eye makeup. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: Pushti Marg, the Path of Grace, is a tradition of Krishna worship. What aspect of Krishna is worshipped?
explanation: The Pushti Mārg tradition of Krishna worship creates yet another landscape—a linked network of havelīs, each housing a small and powerful image of Krishna, and a network of baithaks where Vallabha taught. At any one of these havelīs, the pilgrim experience brings vicarious participation as members of the extended family in the daily life of Krishna—his waking and sleeping, his caring for the cows and coming home at dusk, his swinging and his water sports. Visiting a havelī, one will catch the Lord somewhere in the daily domestic cycle and enter into that very moment._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: Krishna in this temple turned 180 degrees to allow his disciple Kanakadasa his glimpse since he was not allowed into this temple. Where is this temple?
explanation: A devotee named Kanakadāsa came as a pilgrim to Udupi but was unable to enter the temple because of his station in life as a shepherd. So he sang songs of devotion outside the temple, and Krishna, they say, was moved by his song. The Lord opened up a crack in the temple wall and turned 180 degrees to face west so that Kanakadāsa could have his darshan. Rather than patch up the crack, the temple authorities turned it into a window, which they dubbed Kanakadāsa’s window, through which everyone can peep in from the outside and see Lord Krishna. Today, it is customary to peek through Kanakadāsa’s silver-framed and highly ornate window before entering the temple for darshan. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: Rinchor in Dakor, close to Ahmedabad is a Krishna temple. What is the origin of the deity?
explanation: An aging devotee used to make the trek from Dakor to Dvāraka on a regular basis to honor Krishna with tulsī leaves he had grown himself. Eventually, he became too old and lame to do so, and so he drove to Dvārakā in a cart intending to seize Krishna and take the image home with him to Dakor. Despite the security precautions of the Dvārakā Temple, Krishna was willingly kidnapped and spirited away in the cart to Dakor, where he is honored today as Rinchor. Source: India: A Sacred Geography by Diana Eck

hook: A gigantic boulder precariously positioned is known as Lord Krishna’s butter ball. Where will you find this?
explanation: Mahabalipuram is a UNESCO heritage site developed by the Pallava dynasty in the 7th and 8th centuries. Several rock cut temples, cave sanctuaries and rock carvings dot this area. The Krishna's Butter Ball appears precariously tilted, but has defined several attempts to move it. Source:

hook: Which Krishna temple is called the Dwaraka of the South
explanation: The Lord Krishna asked his foremost disciple and devotee, Uddhava to retrieve the idol from the impending submergence of Dwaraka in the sea and install it at a spot equally holy so that He could shower His blessings on His devotees and save them from the evil effects of Kaliyuga. Uddhava entrusted the job to Guru, the preceptor of the gods, and Vayu, the god of winds. They took possession of the idol and moved in search of a proper place. Ultimately they decided to install it near a lake full of lotuses, on one side of which there were Siva and Parvati. That is Guruvayur, the place having derived its name from Guru and Vayu who jointly installed the vigraha. Source: Temples and Legends of Kerala by Bharatiya Bhavan

hook: In what form does Krishna appear in Guruvayoor
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ The deity in Guruvayur represents the full manifestation of the Lord Mahavishnu as was revealed to Vasudeva and Devaki at the time of Krishnavatara; form of Sri Krishna endowed with the four lustrous arms carrying the conch, the discus, the mace and the lotus._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Temples and Legends of Kerala by Bharatiya Bhavan

hook: In one of the Krishna temples in Kerala, Tiruchambaram, an animal is banned and is also not supposed to be brought near the temple. Which animal is it?
explanation: Tiruchambaram is associated with Kamsavadha. Here the jubilant Krishna after the killing of Kamsa is depicted in all his splendor at Tiruchambaram. Kamsa had arranged for the Kuvalayapida, the royal elephant to encounter Krishna and Balarama as they marched towards Kamsa. Krishna fought with the elephant and killed it. Therefore elephants are not taken near the temple although elephants are very much part of all other Kerala temples. The belief is also that Krishna, after killing Kamsa went to his mother Devaki and asked for food stating that he was very hungry after the ordeal. Therefore, unlike in other temples, Naivedya is offered as soon as the temple is opened. Source: Temples and Legends of Kerala by Bharatiya Bhavan

hook: Chappan Bhog, the feast of 56 items, has its origins in one of the Krishna Leelas. Which one is it?
explanation: Krishna sheltered the villagers of Gokul under the GovardhanGiri for seven days and seven nights. During this entire period, he did not eat and was focused on protecting his people. Normally he is fed eight times a day. Therefore 56 items were prepared to quench his hunger after the Leela representing each of the meals he had missed. Source: Bhoga Naivedyam by Sujata Shukla

hook: In which Indian city are you likely to find a famous temple for Krishna as the Charioteer of Arjuna
explanation: The Parthasarathy temple in Chennai is more than 1500 years old. Here Krishna is manifest as the Charioteer of Arjuna and is depicted with a moustache and with a conch in his hand. His face is full of arrow marks, representing the arrows that he stopped during the war. Source:

hook: Agathacoles coins refer to set of die struck coins issued by Indo Greek king Agathocles. These have one of the earliest images of Krishna and Balarama. Where were these coins found?
explanation: These coins were found in Al Khanoum in Afghanistan. Both sides of the coins bear images of male figures. What is most striking about these figures are the attributes that differentiate them. The figure on the obverse holds a miniature plough in his left hand, identifying him as Balarama, who is also known as Haladhara (wielder of the plough). In his right hand, he holds up a pestle (musala). The figure on the reverse holds a large, six-spoked wheel in his left hand against his left flank, which makes it look like a shield. This wheel (chakra) is the symbol of Vasudeva Krishna. In his right hand, he holds up something which may be a conch shell. The figure on the obverse stands between the two lines of a vertical legend in Greek, giving the name of king Agathocles. The iconographic details make the identification of the two figures certain, not ambiguous. These coins have been dated with precision to the reign of Agathocles, c. 180–170 BCE. Source: A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India From the Stone Age to the 12th Century by Upinder Singh

hook: Tens of thousands of people walk every year to this Krishna pilgrimage site. Which is this?
explanation: Pandharpur, west of Solapur in Maharashtra, is one of the major pilgrimage sites. Vithoba or Vithala or Panduranga is worshipped in Pandharpur. Vithoba is a form of Krishna. His consort is Rukmini. Pandharpur Vari is the yatra of Varkaris the followers of saints of Maharashtra, notably, Jnaneesvar and Tukaram. Every year, they reach Pandharpur on the Ekadashi of the Ashadha month carrying Palkhis. The main Palkhis start from Alandi and Dehu and meet in Pune. Palkhis of several other saints also join the pilgrimage. They reach Pandharpur on the Ashadha Shukla 10th. The Varis have been a continuous tradition for several centuries and had united the masses. Source: Immortal India Volume 1 by Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan.

hook: Yamuna is curved in one place along its course and it is supposed to be due to Balarama dragging the river with his plough. Where is this location?
explanation: This is in Vrindavan. In one story, Balarama was mocked by Yamuna for trying to imitate Krishna's dances crudely. In a rage, Balarama drew the river with a deep furrow of his plough. An another version, Balarama got intoxicated and desired to take a bath. He ordered the river to come near him and on her refusal to come, he dragged her. Source: Immortal India Volume 2 by Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan.

hook: India’s national calendar is based on an era that some traditions say honors a Satavahana ruler, the first of the Deccan dynasties. Which is this calendar?
explanation: The national calendar, based on the Saka Era, was adopted on March 22, 1957, along with the Gregorian calendar for official use. The Saka era started in 78 CE. Historians disagree on the events it marks. A later tradition (after the 13th century CE) suggests the Saka era marks King Shalivahana's reign. The Satavahana ruler Gautamiputra Satkarni defeated the Saka satraps of Western India, who were of Iranian origin. This was considered an important event. Other traditions say the Saka era celebrates the Saka rulers. Satavahanas (also called Shalivahanas) are known as Andhras in the Puranas. They were governors of the Deccan under the Mauryas and became independent in the 1st century BCE. They ruled for 300 years and fought against the Sakas, who were invading India. The Sakas outlived the Satavahanas but were eventually defeated by the Guptas. Under the Satavahanas, the Konkan coast became a major trading area. A 1st-century CE book by an Egyptian merchant mentions Chaul (Vasai), Sopara, Kalyan, and Gharapuri (Elephanta) as important ports on the Konkan coast. Although they were Saivites, the Satavahanas supported all religious groups. The Konkan area became a stronghold of Buddhism. During their rule, some famous Buddhist cave temples, such as the Karla Caves, were built. Source: RC Majumdar, “Ancient India”; AS Altekar, writing on Satavahanas in the Maharashtra State Gazetteer. The picture is Satavahana architecture at Pandavleni Caves at Nashik, during the 2nd century BCE.

hook: Queen Prabhavati, a Vakataka, was one of the first women to hold power over a large kingdom. Which famous dynasty was she born into?
explanation: The Vakatakas succeeded the Satavahanas. They ruled parts of the Deccan from the mid-3rd to the late 5th century CE, from Nandivardhana, near Nagpur. Their rule overlapped with the Guptas to the north. Chandragupta II gave his daughter Prabhavati Gupta in marriage to the Vakataka ruler Rudrasena II. This was likely to gain Vakataka support for his campaign against the Sakas in Gujarat and Malwa._x000D_ _x000D_ After her husband’s death, Prabhavati became regent for her three young sons and later ruled directly for two decades (390-410 CE). The Pune Copper Plate inscription of Prabhavati Gupta mentions the year she became regent. It also identifies her gotra as her father’s dynasty, not her husband’s, and gives details about her father and her mother, who was a Naga princess. This inscription shows that she ruled independently._x000D_ _x000D_ Because of her regency, the Guptas and Vakatakas became allies, bringing political unity to a large part of India. Prabhavati is considered the original philosopher queen of India. It is said that Kalidasa, the great Sanskrit poet, was in her court._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Priya Jyoti Samanta, ‘Poona Copper Plate Inscription of Prabhavati Gupta’, Sadvidya - Journal of Research in Sanskrit; RC Majumdar, “Ancient India”_x000D_ _x000D_ A painting of Prabhavatigupta by Asit Kumar Haldar

hook: The Gangas commissioned one of the greatest monuments of Jainism at Shrvanabelagola in Karnataka. What is the monument called?
explanation: Shravanabelagola is an important Jain pilgrimage site in South Karnataka. It has an 18-meter-high statue of Lord Gometeshwara Bahubali. It is considered one of the world’s tallest free-standing monoliths. Chamundaraya, a minister and general of Rachamalla IV of the Ganga dynasty, built it in 981 AD. The statue is carved from a single block of granite, on a hilltop, and is visible from up to 30 km away. Nearly 700 steps are cut into the rock to reach the statue. Chandragupta Maurya is believed to have spent his final days here after giving up his throne, though this is debated. He was accompanied by his guru, Bhadrabahu. The Western Gangas ruled in Mysore state (Gangavadi) from about 250 to about 1004 CE. They maintained their power through marriages and military alliances with the Pallavas, Chalukyas, and Kadambas. They ruled between the Tungabhadra and Krishna rivers, from Talakad (the capital) to Vatapi. The Cholas conquered them around 1004. Most of the Western Gangas were Jains, but some supported Brahmanical Hinduism. They supported Kannada scholarship, built notable temples, and promoted deforestation, irrigation, farming, and trade across the peninsula. Shravanabelagola and its surroundings have many Jain bastis (of the Digambara sect). Source: Karnataka Tourism, Brittanica. The picture is the deity of Gometeshwara Bahubali.

hook: Like Birbal was to Akbar, Tenali Rama was to which Vijayanagara king?
explanation: Tenali Ramakrishna was a 16th-century CE Telugu poet in the court of Krishnadevaraya, the Vijayanagara ruler. He was one of the eight prominent poets (astadiggajas) patronized by the court. He wrote several religious works. His work, Panduranga Mahatmya, is considered a landmark in Telugu kavyas. He was also called ‘vikatakavi’ (jester poet) and was known for blending wisdom with humor. Like Birbal, Tenali Rama is known for folktales where he used his wit to solve problems. However, there are no historical sources for these stories. From the mid-14th to the mid-16th century CE, the Vijayanagara empire influenced the culture south of the Vindhyas. They supported the temple at Tirupati, which is still one of the most visited temples in India. Statues of Krishnadevaraya and his two wives are in one of the temple's mandapas. The Dussehra celebrations at Mysuru are a Vijayanagara tradition still followed by the Wodeyar royal family, who were feudatories to the empire. The Vijayanagara empire ended after a defeat by the combined forces of the five Deccan sultanates in 1565 CE. The ruins of their capital, Hampi, show the empire's greatness. Source: MAP Academy The picture is Tenali Rama’s statue at Tenali

hook: The Yadavas of Devagiri fought the Delhi sultans for three generations. Their defeat allowed Muslim rule in the Deccan. What is the modern name of Devagiri?
explanation: Like the Hoysalas, the Yadavas of Devagiri (now Daulatabad) claimed to be descendants of Krishna. Both rose to power in the 10th century CE when the Rashtrakuta and Chalukya power declined. The Yadavas ruled in Maharashtra, and the Hoysalas ruled in Karnataka. They often fought each other, despite their claimed kinship. They also fought with the Kakatiyas of Andhra. The three dynasties did not unite against the Sultans and were defeated one by one. The Yadavas were the first to be defeated. Alauddin Khilji defeated Ramachandra Yadav in 1296 CE. Ramachandra took refuge in his fort at Devagiri, which was thought to be impenetrable. However, due to mismanagement of food supplies, he was forced to surrender. A few years later, he refused to pay tribute. Malik Kafur defeated him again in 1307 CE. Ramachandra accepted vassalship, but his son Sankara refused and was killed in a war with Kafur. Ramachandra’s son-in-law Harapala continued the fight but was also defeated, captured, and killed. The Yadava resistance ended, and the Deccan came under Muslim rule. Source: RC Majumdar, “Ancient India” The picture is Devagiri Fort

hook: Kempa Gowda is considered as the founder of Bengaluru. He was the governor of which kingdom?
explanation: Kempa Gowda was born in Yelahanka, now a suburb of Bengaluru. His family were vassals of Yelehanka for the Vijayanagara empire. In 1537, he erected a mud fort and started a township within it with the permission of the Vijayanagara emperor. He called it the Bengaluru Fort. Contrary to popular stories about the name Bengaluru being linked to Brinjal or Bengal gram, historical inscriptions refer to Bengaluru from 1200 years back. The fort consisted of eight gates, referring to now popular areas such as Halsoor, Anekal, Kengeri and Yelehanka. He shifted his capital from Yelehanka to Bengaluru. Numerous “pete” areas came up within the fort dedicated to particular professions and goods. The fort is near the areas of Nagarathpet and Chikpet. Kempa Gowda made the mistake of minting his own coin which infuriated the Vijayanagar emperor. Kempa Gowda was imprisoned for five years as a result. The fort was improved by Tipu Sultan and was eventually brought down the British. Shivaji’s father obtained a Jagir for the Fort and surrounding areas from the Bijapur sultanate. The area passed on to Shivaji’s stepbrother who then lost the Fort to Aurangzeb’s army. They later on sold it to the Wodeyar’s of Mysore. Source: Fazlul Hasan, “Bangalore through the Centuries.” The picture is of the remains of Bangalore Fort

hook: INS Kadamba is an important naval base of India Navy celebrating a famous dynasty. Where is INS Kadamba?
explanation: Kadambas were a minor dynastic power that held sway in coastal Karnataka between 4th-6th cent CE. Their chroniclers claim that the family migrated from northern India, but other records suggest that they were indigenous to Kuntala (northern Kanara). An early inscription, the accuracy of which is unknown, describes the dynastic founder, Mayurasharman. He was a learned Brahman who was insulted by a Pallava official. He took up a military career and acquired sufficient territory to bargain with the Pallavas for a feudal principality on the western coast. Pulakesi II, the warlike Chalukya captured their capital Banavasi in the mid 7th cent CE and their rule ended. They reappear in historical records as the rulers of Goa between 10th-14th cent CE, having defeated the Shilaharas. The Kadambas have left a more lasting impact on Goan history. The bus service in Goa is named after them. Many old houses in Goa still sport the lion emblem of the Kadambas. The Indian navy named one of its bases INS Kadamba. Source: Britannica The picture is of INS Kadamba at Karwar

hook: The Hoysala temples have the most exquisite carvings as they used a softer stone. Which stone is this?
explanation: Hoysalas ruled southern Karnataka, mainly the Hassan, Mysore, Shimoga, Chitradurga districts. Their reign lasted between the 10th and 14th centuries. They extended their influence southwards into the areas ruled by Chola and Pandyan kings. Hoysalas are renowned for their temple architecture. They used soap stone for their intricate carvings. Soapstone has a high talc (magnesium) content, making it easy to carve. The Hoysala temples display a unique artistic style and extraordinary intricacy of carving. The Hoysala temples at Belur, Halabeidu and Somanathapura are UNESCO heritage sites. There are several hundred Hoysala temples in southern Karnataka. While they have been damaged extensively in invasions, the remains are still magnificent. Source: William Coehlo, Hoysala Vamsa The picture is from Hoysaleswara temple of Halabeidu

hook: Which wondrous temple, carved from a single rock, will forever be associated with the greatness of the Rashtrakutas?
explanation: Cave Number 16 of Ellora is the Kailasanatha temple. It represents an audacity of imagination like none other. It is a massive monolithic rock cut temple. There is nothing like this anywhere else in India or the world. Bulk of the carving of the temple was completed under the patronage of Krishna I of the Rashtrakuta dynasty. The Kailasanatha temple at Ellora is influenced by the Virupaksha temple at Pattadakal. Krishna I had defeated the Badami Chalukyas who had constructed the Virupaksha temple. It in turn was inspired by the Kailasanatha temple at Kanchipuram built by Pallavas. The Chalukyas had defeated the Pallavas in battle. Thus, the rock cut temple of Ellora traces its origin across three empires. Ellora’s Kailasanatha was earlier known as Manikeshwar and Ghrishneshwar and was believed to be one of the 12 Jyotirlingas. At present, the Ghrishneshwar temple built by Holkars in Verul, adjacent to Ellora, is worshipped as a Jyotirlinga. Rashtrakutas reigned from the 6th to 10th century, but were at their peak in the 8th to 10th centuries. Their capital was at Manyakheta, a famous city of antiquity. At some point or other, they defeated the great powers of the time such as the Pratiharas, Palas, Eastern Chalukyas, and Cholas. They had invaded Kannauj. Their greatest king was Amoghavarsha who is said to have turned Jain in later life. A muslim traveler called him among the four greatest monarchs of the world. Like most other dynasties, the Rashtrakutas were ecletic in their patronage to sects. They were also instrumental in encouraging the development of Kannada. Source: M.K. Dhavilkar, Kailasa – Stylistic Development and Chronology; Britannica The picture is Kailasanatha Temple, Ellora

hook: Ikshvakus were a short lived dynasty of Andhra and ruled from Nagarjunakonda, named after philosopher Nagarjuna. Which religion/sect did Nagarjuna follow?
explanation: Satavahana power in the Deccan collapsed by 3rd cent CE. New dynasties rose. Ikshvakus ruled the Andhra region between Godavri & Krishna. They lost power within a century to Pallavas from the South. It is unclear if they were connected to the Ikshvakus of Ayodhya and thus Sri Rama’s lineage. The appearance of names like Kosala & Ikshvaku in the Deccan in the period suggest migration from the north. The Ikshvakus ruled from Nagarjunakonda. Under their patronage it turned into a prominent university centred around Mahayana Buddhism. The town itself was named after Nagarjuna, the most famous of Mahayana philosophers, who had lived a few centuries earlier. Ikshvakus were eclectic in their patronage and the founder of the dynasty, Chamtamula even performed the traditional Vedic Ashvamedha sacrifice to announce his kingship. Source: RC Majumdar, “Ancient India.” The picture is Nagarjunakonda Ayaka pillar inscription of the time of Ishvaku ruler Vira-Purushadatta.

hook: Which dynasty built three capitals at Aihole, Badami and Pattadakal and left a legacy of magnificent temple architecture?
explanation: The Chalukyas rose to power in the region around Vatapi (near Bijapur) in Karnataka in 540CE. They quickly expanded their territory under warlike kings. The Chalukyas were grand temple builders in Indian history. Their trio of capital cities, Aihole-Vatapi (Badami) & Padatkkal in Karnataka are the cradle of Hindu temple architecture, with temples in all styles - Nagara(northern style), Dravida (southern style) & Vesara (mixed) and in all types – rock cut, brick, stone etc. Pulakesi II was their greatest ruler. He had defeated Kadambas of Banavasi, Alupas, and Gangas of Mysore. He made expeditions into the eastern Deccan, south Kosala, and Kalinga; and even won against Harshavardhana on the banks of the Narmada. Pulakesi II was eventually defeated & killed by the Pallavas in 642CE. His remarkable achievements were inscribed on the walls of a Jain temple at Aihole. It uses old Kannada script to write Sanskrit. It starts with the now familiar Jain greeting ‘Jai Jinendra.’ It then describes Pulakesi’s family tree & achievements, comparing him to Vedic gods and dating his reign from the Mahabharata war. The family tree helps in dating the Mahabharata war to 3137 BCE. The Chalukyas eventually lost to Rashtrakutas around 750CE. Source: Upinder Singh, A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India The picture is of Pattadakal Temples

hook: Just like the Ashokan pillar serves as our national emblem, a temple arch serves as the emblem of Telangana State. Which dynasty created the temple arch?
explanation: Kakatiyas were powerful rulers of eastern deccan between the 11th and 14th centuries. In the late 13th century the kingdom was ruled by a woman, Queen Rudramba. Her grandson & successor, Pratapa Rudra was a great ruler but was the last and was defeated & taken prisoner by Ulugh Khan (later the Delhi sultan Muhammed Tughlaq)._x000D_ _x000D_ The Kakatiyan capital was at Warangal. The original city had three concentric ring of walls. The innermost was 1.2 km in diameter and is called as the Warangal Fort. The innermost ring housed a Shiva temple which was demolished in invasions. Only the freestanding entrance portals (Toranas) on the four sides remain. The are about 10 mts in height. They were spared perhaps because they lacked any obvious religious themes._x000D_ _x000D_ The temple arch was incorporated into the State emblem along with Charminar immediately after the formation of Telangana State. In 2024, the new Congress Government finalized a new State emblem which replaced both the Kakatiya Arch and the Charminar with a martyr’s symbol. It was supposed to have been unveiled on Telangana State day, June 2, 2024, but was deferred._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: George Michelle, “Southern India”; RC Majumdar, “Ancient India”_x000D_ _x000D_ The picture is the Telangana State Emblem.

hook: Ahilya was cursed and transformed after her infatuation with Indra. She attained salvation after Rama and Lakshmana meet her. What did she actually become?
explanation: In the Valmiki Ramayana Ahilya is only cursed to become invisible, feed on air and starve for food. There is no mention of her turning into a stone. Kalidasa, in Raghu-vamsa first describes her as having turned to stone. This in fact suggests that she remained introspective and unaffected by the world. In the popular culture, Ahilya was liberated when Rama’s feet touched her. It is also a belief that the dust from Rama’s feet touched and liberated her. In the Valmiki Ramayana, both Rama and Lakshmana fell at her feet and she looked like a brilliant flame enveloped by smoke. In fact, in a different episode, it is Rishi Vishwamitra who curses Rambha to turn into a stone when she tries to seduce him. The latter day variations emphasise Ahilya’s detachment from the world; and the positive effect that even a young Rama had (before he was even married to Sita) to mend a broken marriage. Picture: Ahilya painting by Raja Ravi Varma.

hook: In Sita Rama Kalyanam, which of these episodes is not in Valmiki Ramayana?
explanation: Sri Rama did not win over Sita in a Swayamwaram. In fact no Swayamwaram was even held. Shiva had bent the bow in rage during the destruction of Daksha’s Yajna. The bent bow was kept in the custody of Janaka’s clan. Many kings had earlier tried to lift Shiva’s bow. None could succeed. The defeated kings tried to siege Mithila and Janaka had warded them off. The story that Ravana felt insulted when Sita and her friends laughed at his failure to lift the bow is also not in Valmiki Ramayana. Janaka had vowed that he would only marry Sita to a man who would string the bow. At the invitation and with the permission of Vishwamitra, Rama lifts the bow easily, strings it and breaks it. The sound was so deafening that all except Janaka, Vishwamitra, Rama and Lakshmana fell down to the floor. Janaka then tells Vishwamitra that he would offer Sita in marriage to Rama. Picture: Rama stringing the bow by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Surpanaka was infatuated with Rama and beseeched him to take her. In what form did she approach him?
explanation: In many popular tellings, Surpanaka approaches Rama as a beautiful woman. However, in Valmiki Ramayana, she approaches Rama as a rakshasa in a ghastly form. She in fact tells Rama that she is a Rakshasi and can take any form she likes. She also describes her entire family to Rama. Further, Rama advises Lakshmana that he should never ridicule Rakshasas like her. The description that she came as a beautiful woman only serves to underline the ability of Rakshsas to change their form at will; and to resort to deceit to win over strong and Dharmic people. Picture: Lakshmana disfigures Surpanakha by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Which of the narrations related to Sita abduction is not there in Valmiki Ramayana?
explanation: Lakshamana does not draw a line in front of the hut to protect Sita. This incident is not in any other Sanskrit version of Ramayana too. The latter narratives remind us of the immense sense of duty that Lakshamana had to protect Rama; and the moral valour of Lakshamana that his mere line could stop anyone from entering the hut. In fact, in Valmiki Ramayana, Lakshmana says that he will go and look for Rama and that the deities of the forest may protect Sita. Picture: Sita is enthralled by the Golden deer, by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Ravana carries Sita in a chariot. Who pulls the chariot?
explanation: In popular narrative, Ravana carries Sita in a Pushpaka-Vimana or a flying chariot. In Valmiki Ramayana, he carries her in a magical chariot pulled by donkeys. However, Jatayu fights Ravana, smashes his chariot, slays the donkeys and kills the charioteer. Ravana then carries Sita physically and flies away to Lanka. The popular narrative that Ravana carried Sita scooped the earth around Sita and carried her is also not in the original Valmiki Ramayana. He holds her by the hair and thigh and carries her in his lap. The narrative only reminds us of the link that Sita has with her mother earth. No one would be able to separate Sita from mother earth and therefore she had to be carried along with earth. Picture: Ravana slays Jatayu by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Which of the following regarding Sabri's meeting with Rama and Lakshmana is not part of Valmiki Ramayana
explanation: There is no mention of Sabri biting the fruits. In popular narrative, Sabri bites the fruits as she is blind and cannot ensure their ripeness by checking the fruits. In Valmiki Ramayana, when Rama and Lakshamana reach Sabri’s ashram, she falls at their feet. She says that once she meets them, she is also destined to go to heaven. She mentions that she has collected several forest produce for them and then offers them. She also points them to Lake Pampa. She then seeks the permission of Rama and Lakshmana to leave her body. She immolates herself and resembling a flame, ascends to Swarga. The popular narrative underlines the Bhakti of Sabri and the acceptance of Rama of the acts of his true devotees. Picture: Sabri meets Rama, unknown source

hook: What did Hanuman not do in Lanka as per the Valmiki Ramayana?
explanation: Indrajit is sent by Ravana to capture Hanuman after Hanuman kills Ravana’s son Aksa. Hanuman is bound by the Brahmastra fired by Indrajit and is taken as a captive to Ravana. Here, popular narratives claim that Ravana did not offer a seat to Hanuman. To insult Ravana, Hanuman extended his tail and coiled it and created a chair for himself. It was higher than the seat of Ravana. This is not part of Valmiki Ramayana. Hanuman declares himself as a messenger of Rama. Ravana spares his life at the advice of Vibhishana as a messenger cannot be killed. Ravana only tries to punish Hanuman by lighting his tail on fire. Agni listens to Sita’s prayers and prevents the flames from burning Hanuman. Eventually, Hanuman ends up burning Lanka and flying back. Picture: Hanuman by Raja Ravi Varma

hook: Which of these incidents at Rameswaram is not part of Valmiki Ramayana?
explanation: Rama is believed to have consecrated a Shivalinga at Rameshwaram before crossing the ocean; and in some accounts while returning with Sita. These are not part of Valmiki Ramayana.
